questions.q:
The following compound

can readily be prepared by Williamson ether synthesis by reaction between
questions.solution: Williamson ether synthesis is the reaction in which an alkyl halide is allowed to react with alkoxide to give ether. The reaction involves SN2 attack of an alkoxide ion on primary alkyl halide. In option a and b there are alkoxide and alkyl halide, but option b is preferred because I is better leaving group than Cl-

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Upon reaction with $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr}$ followed by protonation, the compound that produces ethanol is
questions.solution:
\(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_{3}\)
\(\mathrm{HCHO}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}\)
\(\mathrm{HCOOH}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}\)
So \(\mathrm{HCHO}\) on reaction with \(\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{MgBr}\) followed by protonation will produce ethanol.

When reacted with acetic anhydride in the presence of a trace amount of conc. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mo>,</mo></math> compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> as the major product. Compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> is
questions.solution: Reaction of phenol with NaOH followed by heating with CO2 under high pressure and subsequent acidification gives salicyclic acid X as the major product, which can be purified by steam distillation. When reacted with acetic anhydride in presence of a trace amount of conc. H2SO4, compound X produces methyl salicyclate Y as the major product.

questions.q: Benzaldehyde can be converted to benzyl alcohol in concentrated aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution using
questions.solution:
Benzaldehyde can be converted to benzyl alcohol in concentrated aq.NaOH solution using formaldehyde.

This reaction is known as Cannizzaro reaction, where one molecule of benzaldehyde is reduced to alcohol while formaldehyde is oxidised to carboxylic acid salt. Aldehydes, which do not have an $\alpha$ -hydrogen atom undergo this type of reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A tetrapeptide is made of naturally occurring alanine, serine, glycine and valine. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>-terminal amino acid is alanine and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>-terminal amino acid is chiral, the number of possible sequences of the tetrapeptide is
questions.solution:
A tetra-peptide is made of naturally occuring alanine serine glycine and valine.

It is given that C-terminal amino acid is alanine and N-terminal amino acid is chiral, thus glycine can't be N-terminal amino acid as it is an achiral amino acid. Therefore possible sequence of tetra-peptide are as follows.
(i) Alanine, glycine, serine, valine
(ii) Alanine, glycine, valine, serine
(iii) Alanine, serine, glycine, valine
(iv) Alanine, valine, glycine, serine

Thus it can have 4 possible sequences of the tetrapeptide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The average energy of each hydrogen bond in A-T pair is x kcalmol-1 and that in G-C pair is y kcalmol-1. Assuming that no other interaction exists between the nucleotides, the approximate energy required in kcalmol-1 to split the following double stranded DNA into two single strands is

[Each dashed line may represent more than one hydrogen bond between the base pairs]
questions.solution:
Number of H-bonds in each A-T pair =2

Number of H-bonds in G-C pair =3.

The given double stranded DNA has 5 A-T and 3 G-C pairs.

∴ Total number of H-bonds in 5 A-T pair
=5×2=10

Total number of H-bonds in 3 G-C pair
=3×3=9

⇒ Average energy of 10H-bonds in A-T pair =10x.

Average energy of 9H -bonds in G-C pair =9y

Thus, the approximate energy required to split the double stranded DNA in two single strands =10x+9y

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The hybridisations of N,C and O shown in the following compound

respectively, are
questions.solution:
Hybridisation is determined from the steric number (number of atoms bonded to the central atom + the number of lone pairs). Number of hybrid orbitals must be equal to the steric number.
From the Lewis structure.

(i) Steric number of N-atom =3 (2 bonded atoms +1 lone pair), ∴ Hybridisation =sp2 (3 hybrid orbitals).
(ii) Steric number of C-atom =2 (2 bonded atoms),
∴: Hybridisation =s p ( 2 hybrid orbitals).
(iii) Steric number of 0 -atom =3 (1 bonded atom +2 lone pair) ∴ Hybridisation =sp2(3 hybrid orbitals).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following species, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> angle ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> ) follows the order.
questions.solution:
As the electronegativity of the central atom increases the bond angle also increases. So, NH3 will have high bond angle than PH3. The bond angle of NH4+ is highest because of its symmetrical geometry, also bond pair and lone pair on N of NH3 suffer repulsion, which decreases its bond angle in comparison to NH4+. Thus, the correct order of bond angle is

PH3<NH3<NH4+

But in the given question none of the option is correct because there is no H-X-H bond angle in BF3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>B</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>F</mi></math> bond angles in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>r</mi><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>l</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>l</mi></math> bond angles in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>C</mi><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>5</mn></msub></math>, respectively, are
questions.solution:
The geometry of BrF5  is square pyramidal.

Here, the lone pair occupies the axial position and hence axial bonds will suffer more repulsion than equatorial bonds. Thus, the axial Br-F bond length will be different from equatorial Br-F.

The geometry of PCl5 is triangular pyramidal.

The axial bonds suffer more repulsions than equatorial bonds, so they are larger in bond length.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The diamagnetic species among the following is
questions.solution:
To be diamagnetic, no unpaired electron must be present. 

Hint Write molecular orbital electronic configuration of each species.

For all options given,

σ1s2σ1s⋆2σ2s2σ2s*2σ2pz2π2px2=π2py2 is common. 

After that, O2+:π2px*1,

O2-:π2px*2=π2py*1,

and O2:π2px*1=π2py*1,

all contain unpaired electron. They are paramagnetic, but O22-:π2px*2=π2py⋆2 has no unpaired electrons. 

∴ It is diamagnetic.

questions.solution:
The bond order can be calculated
as

B.O=12Nb-Na

Where, Nb= Electrons in bonding orbitals Na= Electrons in anti bonding orbitals.
(a) CO and O22-

The electronic configuration of CO (14) is

σ1s2σ*1s2σ2s2σ*2s2σ*2pz2π2px2π2p2y

∴  B.O=12(10-4)=62=3

(b) O2- and CO

The electronic configuration of 02-(17) is

σ1s2σ*1s2σ2s2σ*2s2σ2pz2π2px2

π2py2π*2px2π*2py1

B.O=12(10-7)=32=1.5

B.O. of CO is 3

[as calculated in option (a)]

(c) B.O.. of O22- is 1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the equilibria (1) and (2) with equilibrium constants $\mathrm{K}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2}$, respectively
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \leftrightharpoons \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) ...(1) \\ 
2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \leftrightharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) ...(2)
\end{array}
$$
$\mathrm{K}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{K}_{2}$ are related as
questions.solution:
$\left.\mathrm{K}_{1}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{sO}_{3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}\right]$
$\mathrm{K}_{2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]^{2}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]^{2}}$
Hence: $\mathrm{K}_{2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{1}^{2}}$
$\mathrm{~K}_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{2}}$

questions.q: Among the following, the correct statement about the chemical equilibrium is.
options: Equilibrium constant is independent of temperature.
Equilibrium constant tells us how fast the reaction reaches equilibrium.
At equilibrium, the forward and the backward reactions stop so that the concentrations of reactants and products are constant.
Equilibrium constant is independent of whether you start the reaction with reactants or products.
questions.solution:
• Equilibrium constant is dependent on temperature.
  • Equilibrium constant do not tell us about the rate of reaction.
  • At equilibrium, the forward and backward reactions do not stop but they have same rate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The reaction

CaCO3s⇌CaOs+CO2g

is in equilibrium in a closed vessel at 298 K. The partial pressure (in atm) of CO2g in the reaction vessel is closest to:

[Given: The change in Gibbs energies of formation at 298 K and 1 bar for

CaO(s)=-603.501 kJ mol-1

CO2g=-394.389 kJ mol-1

CaCO3s=-1128.79 kJ mol-1

Gas constant R=8.314 J K-1 mol-1]
questions.solution:
CaCO3(s)⇌CaO(s)+CO2(g)

ΔrG0=ΔfG0(CaO)+ΔfG0CO2-ΔfG0CaCO3

=-603.501-394.389+1128.79=130.9 kJ mol-1

ΔrG0=-2.303RTlogKP

logKp=130.9×1000-2.303×298×8.314=-22.94

Kp=antilog (-22.94)=1.13×10-23

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $783 \mathrm{~K}$ in the reaction $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{I}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})$, the molar concentrations $\left(\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right.$ ) of $\mathrm{H}_{2}, \mathrm{I}_{2}$ and HI at some instant of time are $0.1,0.2$ and $0.4$, respectively. If the equilibrium constant is 46 at the same temperature, then as the reaction proceeds
options: the amount of HI will increase
the amount of HI will decrease
the amount of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ will increase
the amount of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ will not change
questions.solution:
Reaction quotient
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{Q}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_{2}\right]}=\frac{0.4 \times 0.4}{0.1 \times 0.2} \\
\mathrm{Q}=8 \\
\mathrm{Q} < \mathrm{K}
\end{array}$
So reaction will proceeds in forward direction. 
Hence amount of HI increases.

questions.q:

$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}+1 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2}\left[\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{cl}}=9.1 \times 10^{-13} \text { at } 1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right](\text { eq. } 1) \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons{\mathrm{H}_{2}+1 / 2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\left[\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{cl}}=7.1 \times 10^{-12} \text { at } 1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]
\end{array}
$$
the equilibrium constant for the reaction $\mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ at the same temperature, is-
questions.solution:
(i) $\mathrm{CO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \quad \mathrm{~K}_{1}=9.1 \times 10^{-13}$
(ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{~K}_{2}=7.1 \times 10^{-12}$
Object $\quad \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
equation (i) - (ii) $\therefore \mathrm{K}^{1}=\frac{\mathrm{K}_{1}}{\mathrm{~K}_{2}}=1.28$ $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{C}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{kt}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{kt}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: ${ }^{227} \mathrm{Ac}$ has a half-life of 22 years with respect to radioactive decay . The decay follows two parallel paths : ${ }^{227} \mathrm{Ac} \rightarrow{ }^{227} \mathrm{Th}$ and ${ }^{227} \mathrm{Ac} \rightarrow{ }^{223} \mathrm{Fr}$. If the percentage of the two daughter nuclides are $2.0$ and $98.0$, respectively, the decay constant (in year $^{-1}$ ) for ${ }^{227} \mathrm{Ac} \rightarrow{ }^{227}$ Th path is closest to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ac}^{\mathrm{R}_{1}} / \mathrm{R}_{2} \backslash \mathrm{Ac}$
$\% \mathrm{Th}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{2}{100}$
$\% \mathrm{Ac}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{98}{100}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{2}{98}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}$
$\frac{0.693}{22}=\mathrm{R}_{1}+\frac{98}{2} \mathrm{R}_{1}$
$\therefore \mathbf{R}_{1}=\mathbf{6 . 3} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{-4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The enthalpy (H) of an elementary exothermic reaction $\mathrm{A} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{B}$ is schematically plotted against the reaction coordinate. The plots in the presence and absence of a catalyst are shown in dashed and solid lines, respectively. Identify the correct plot for the reaction.
questions.solution:
For exothermic reaction
$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \mathrm{H}=& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{P}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{R}} < 0 \\
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{A}} < 0 \\
& \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{B}} < \mathbf{E}_{\mathrm{A}}
\end{aligned}$
Catalyst does not changes the initial and final position of the reaction so correct answer is (A)

questions.q: It takes $1 \mathrm{~h}$ for a first order reaction to go to $50 \%$ completion. The total time required for the same reaction to reach $87.5 \%$ completion will be
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=1 \mathrm{hr}$
$\mathrm{t}_{87.5}=\frac{2.303}{\mathrm{k}} \log \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{x}}$
$=\frac{2.303}{\frac{0.693}{1}} \log \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}-.875}$
$=\frac{2.303}{0.693} \log 8$
$=\frac{2.303}{0.693} \times 3 \times .3010$
$=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order chemical reaction,
options: the product formation rate is independent of reactant concentration.

the time taken for the completion of half of the reaction <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>69</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of the rate constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mfenced></math>
the dimension of Arrhenius pre-exponential factor is reciprocal of time.
the concentration vs time plot for the reactant should be linear with a negative slope
questions.solution:
Arrhenius equation for rate constant, k is k=Ae-Ea/RT, where A is the pre-exponential factor. Since, e-Ea/RT is dimensionless, dimension of A=k. For first order reaction, dimension of k=time-1

∴ Also same for A.

As for the other options,

For a first order reaction, the product formation rate is directly proportional to reactant concentration, half-life =0.693k or 69.3% of 1k, and concentration of reactant changes with time by the equation At=A0e-kt, which shows exponential decreases, not linear.

A plot of concentration of X and Y as function of time is

Keq=21=YX

⇒2X=Y

or 2Xeq=Yeq

∴Y>X

Thus, as the rate of reaction increases, there will be increased in the concentration Yeq, but it will be less than initial concentration of XX0, whereas the concentration of Xeq decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The following data are obtained for a reaction, $\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Y} \rightarrow$ Products.
$\begin{array}{llll}\text { Expt. } & {\left[\mathrm{X}_{0}\right] / \mathrm{mol}} & {\left[\mathrm{Y}_{0}\right] / \mathrm{mol}} & \text { rate } / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ 1 & 0.25 & 0.25 & 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \\ 2 & 0.50 & 0.25 & 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \\ 3 & 0.25 & 0.50 & 8.0 \times 10^{-6}\end{array}$
The overall order of the reaction is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{X}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{Y}]^{\mathrm{y}}$
Total order $=\mathrm{n}=\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}$
By exp. (1) \& (2)
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\mathrm{r}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}[.25]^{\mathrm{x}}[.25]^{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{K}[.50]^{\mathrm{x}}[.25]^{\mathrm{y}}}=\frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\frac{1}{(2)^{\mathrm{x}}}=\frac{1}{4}, \mathrm{x}=2
\end{array}$
By exp. (1) \& (3)
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{r_{1}}{r_{3}}=\frac{K[.25]^{x}[.25]^{y}}{K[.25]^{x}[.50]^{y}}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\frac{1}{(2)^{y}}=\frac{1}{8}, y=3
\end{array}$
So Total order $=2+3=5$

questions.q: A zero-ordÃ©r reaction, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow$ Product, with an initial concentration $[\mathrm{A}]_{0}$ has a half-life of $0.2 \mathrm{~s}$. If onÃ© starts with thee concentration $2[\mathrm{~A}]_{0}$, then the half=life is $=$

for zero order reaction $\mathrm{n}=0$ so $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2} \propto \mathrm{a}$
so
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}}{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}}=\frac{a_{1}}{a_{2}} \\
\frac{.2}{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}}=\frac{\left[A_{0}\right]}{2[A]_{0}} \\
t_{1 / 2}=.4 \mathrm{sec}
\end{array}$

questions.q:
A reaction has an activation energy of $209 \mathrm{~kJ}$ $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. The rate increases 10 -fold when the temperature is increased from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\mathrm{X}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature $\mathrm{X}$ is closest to
[Gas constant, $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ ]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The atomic radii of $\mathrm{Li}, \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{Na}$ and Si follow the order.
questions.solution:
Among the given elements Li and Na belong to group 1, i.e. they are alkali metals. So, they will have maximum atomic radii. Between Li and Na, Na will have the largest atomic radii. This is because as we move down the group atomic radii increases.
Si belongs to group 14 and \(\mathrm{F}\) belongs to group 17 . So, as we move from left to right in period the atomic radii decreases. Thus, Si will have large atomic radii than \(\mathrm{F}\) but less than Li.
So, the correct order of atomic radii of given elements is, Na > Li > Si > F.

The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>IP</mi></math> (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>eV</mi></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is closest to :
questions.solution:
The ionisation energy decreases down the group. Hence, the first ionisation potential of K is less than Na. The first ionisation potential of sodium is given as 5.14 eV.

∴ The first ionization potential of K is closest to 4.3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The ionic radii of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mo>+</mo></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>-</mo></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> follow the order
questions.solution:
The given species are isoelectronic (same number of electrons) to each other. In case of isoelectronic species, cation with greater positive charge will have a smaller radius because of the greater attraction of the electrons to nucleus. Anion with greater negative charge will have the largest radius. This is because the net repulsion of electrons will out weight the nuclear charge and the ion will expand in size. Thus, the order of ionic radii will be,

N3->O2->F->Na+

questions.q: For an octahedral complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MX</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> transition metal, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> are monodentate achiral ligands), the correct statement among the following is
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MX</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> geometrical isomers, one of which is chiral

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MX</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> geometrical isomers both of which are achiral

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MX</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> geometrical isomers, all of which are achiral

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>MX</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> geometrical isomers, two of which are chiral
questions.solution:
Two geometrical isomers (cis and trans with respect to ligand Y) are possible. The trans isomer has both centre of symmetry and several planes of symmetry.

and, therefore it is achiral.

The cis isomer has two planes of symmetry and, therefore it is also achiral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, the pair of paramagnetic complexes is
questions.solution:
K3FeCN6

Central metal ion is Fe3+, which has Ar3d5 configuration as free ion. In strong ligand field of CN-, its splits to become t2geg05.

One unpaired electron will be there, and 

∴ It is paramagnetic.

K3CoF6 : Central metal ion is Co3+.

Free metal ion configuration Ar3d6.

In weak ligand field of F-, its splits and becomes t2g4eg2.

Four unpaired electrons, therefore paramagnetic.

In K4FeCN6, central ion is Fe2+ and in CoNH36Cl3, central ion is Co3+

In both of them, free metal ion is Ar3d6.

Ligands NH3 and CN- are of strong field, Δ0>P

So, d6 splits to t2g6eg0

No unpaired electrons, therefore diamagnetic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The complex having the highest spin only magnetic moment is
questions.solution:
The spin only magnetic moment for a complex can be calculated using formula

μ=nn+2

(a) FeCN63-

The oxidation number Fe in FeCN63- is +3 .The electronic configuration of Fe3+ is -Ar3d54s0

As CN- is a strong ligand pairing will occur.

FeCN63-

∴n=1⇒μ=11+2=3

(b) FeH2O62+

The oxidation number of Fe in

FeH2O62+ is +2 .The electronic configuration of Fe2+ is -Ar3d64s0

As H2O is weak ligand, so pairing will not occur.

FeH2O62-


n=4

μ=44+2=24

(c) MnF64-

The oxidation number of Mn is +2. It's
configuration is -Ar3d54s0

As F- is a weak field ligand, so pairing will not occur.

MnF64-


n=5

μ=55+2=35

(d) NiCl42-

In NiCl42-, the oxidation number of Ni will be +2. It's configuration is -Ar3d84s0

As Cl- is a weak field ligand, so pairing will not occur.

NiCl42-

n=2

μ=22+2=8

Thus, MnF64- will have the highest spin only magnetic moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ce</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mn>1</mn></msup><mn>5</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mn>1</mn></msup><mn>6</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mo>Nd</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mn>1</mn></msup><mn>6</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Eu</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mn>7</mn></msup><mn>6</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Dy</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">f</mi><mn>10</mn></msup><mn>6</mn><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>, the elements having highest and lowest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>rd ionisation energies, respectively are
questions.solution:
For 3rd ionisation energy, the electron must be removed from +2 oxidation state of given metals. The electronic configuration of Ce is

4f15d16s2

The electronic configuration of Ce2+ is

4f15d1

The electronic configuration of Nd is

4f46s2

The electronic configuration of Nd2+ is

4f4

The electronic configuration of Eu is

4f76s2

The electronic configuration of Eu2+ is

4f7

The electronic configuration of Dy is

4f106s2

The electronic configuration of Dy2+ is 4f10 Eu will have the maximum 3rd ionisation enthalphy, because the electron has to be removed from stable half-filled 4f7Eu2+ configuration, whereas Ce will have the lowest 3rd ionisation enthalpy as the electron has to be removed from 5d-orbital.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The following reaction takes place at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ in an electrochemical cell involving two metals $\mathrm{A}$ and B,
$\mathrm{A}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq} .)+\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{B}^{2+}($ aq $)+\mathrm{A}(\mathrm{s})$
With $\left[\mathrm{A}^{2+}\right]=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ and $\left[\mathrm{B}^{2+}\right]=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$ in the respective half-cells, the cell EMF is $1.091 \mathrm{~V}$. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is closest to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{.0591}{2} \log \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$
$1.091=\mathrm{E}^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}-\frac{.0591}{2} \log (.5)$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=1.099$
$\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=-\frac{.0591}{2} \log \mathrm{k}$
$\log \mathrm{k}=-\frac{1.099 \times 2}{.0591}=-37.22$
$\therefore \mathrm{k}=2 \times 10^{37}$

The cathode half-reaction in the process is  2H2O+2e-⟶H2+2OH-

questions.q: If $E_{\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{\circ}=-0.440 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ}=0.770 \mathrm{~V}$, then $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}}^{o}$ is-
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Fe}^{+3} / \mathrm{Fe}$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe} \quad \mathrm{E}^{0}=-0.44$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{+2} \quad \mathrm{E}^{0}=0.770$
$\overline{\mathrm{Fe}^{+3}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}} \quad \mathrm{E}^{0}=?$
$\overline{\mathrm{E}^{0}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{0}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{0}}{\mathrm{n}_{3}}}=\frac{2(-0.44)+1 \times(0.770)}{3}=\frac{-0.88+0.770}{3}=-0.037 \mathrm{volt}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, the plot that correctly represents the conductometric titration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi>OH</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
The principle of conductometric titration is based on the fact that during the titration one of the ions is replaced by other and invariably these two ions differ in the ionic conductivity.

In the conductometric titration of 0.05 M H2SO4 with 0.1 M NH4OH, initially the fast moving H+ ion get neutralised as H2O and is replaced by slow moving NH4+ ion up to neutralisation point. After neutralisation point, weak electrolyte NH4OH is added gradually, which do not affect the conductance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The equilibrium constant of a 2 electron redox reaction at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is $3.8 \times 10^{-3}$. The cell potential $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ (in $\left.\mathrm{V}\right)$ and the free energy change $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) for this equilibrium respectively, are
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{rl}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ} & =-2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 \log \left(3.8 \times 10^{-3}\right) \mathrm{J} \\ & =13809.3876 \mathrm{~J}=13.809 \mathrm{Kg} \\ \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ} & =-\mathrm{nFE}^{\circ} \\ 1380 & 9.387=-2 \times 96500 \times \mathrm{E}^{\circ} \\ \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} & =.071\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Given the standard potentials $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}\right)$ and $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+} / \mathrm{Cu}\right)$ as $0.340 \mathrm{~V}$ and $0.522 \mathrm{~V}$ respectively, the value of $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} / \mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$
$\frac{\mathrm{Cu}+2} + \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu} \Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$
$\mathrm{Cu}^{+2}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+} \Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{n}_{3} \mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\circ}-\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\circ}$
$-\mathrm{n}_{3} \mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}=-\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}+\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$
$\mathrm{E}_{3}^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{1} \mathrm{E}_{1}^{\circ}-\mathrm{n}_{2} \mathrm{E}_{2}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{n}_{3}}=\frac{2 \times 0.34-.522}{1}=.158 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the electrochemical cell shown below

Pt|H2p=1 atm|H+aq.,xM|Cu2+aq,1.0 MCus

The potential is 0.49 V at 298 K. The pH of the solution is closest to [ Given, standard reduction potential, E° for Cu2+/Cu is 0.34 VGas constant, R is 8.31 J K-1 mol-1 Faraday constant, F is 9.65×104JV-1 mol-1 ]
questions.solution:
According to Nernst equation,

Ecell=E°cell-0.0591nlogPR

For the given electrochemical cell

Pt∣H2p=1 atmH+aq·x M‖Cu2+aq·10 M/Cus

Ecell=E°cell-0.0591nlogH+2Cu2+

=0.49=0.34-0.05912logx21

0.49=0.34-0.05912×2logx

0.15=-0.0591×logx

2.53=-logx

Also ,-logH+=pH

Here, H+=x

∴  pH=-logx

⇒pH=2.53

questions.solution:
I is most basic, because the lone pair of nitrogen is not delocalised by resonance in that, but in all others, there is resonance with benzene ring. 

III is more basic than II due to +R (or +M) effect of -CH3, and IV is less basic than II because of -R (or - M) effect of -NO2, i.e. correct order is I>III>II>IV.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
X and Y


are
questions.solution:
Both X and Y are conformers of each other, where X is eclipsed and Y is staggered form.

Conformers are the spatial arrangements of atoms, which can be converted into one another by rotation around aC-C  single bond. The conformation in which the substituents are as closed together as possible is called eclipsed conformation, whereas in staggered form the substituents are far apart as possible.

options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>I</mi></math> -effect of the methyl group in ethyl anion, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>p</mi></math> -orbital conjugation in ethyl radical

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mi>I</mi></math> -effect of the methyl group in ethyl anion and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>→</mo><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></math> conjugation in ethyl radical
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>I</mi></math> effect of the methyl group in both cases

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>I</mi></math> - effect of the methyl group in ethyl anion and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>σ</mi><mo>→</mo><msup><mi>σ</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></math> conjugation in ethyl radical
questions.solution: The lower stability of ethyl anion CH3CH2- Compared to methyl anion CH3- is because of +I -effect of methyl group of ethyl anion. The higher stability of ethyl radical compared to methyl radical is due to σ-p -orbital conjugation which is known as hyper conjugation in ethyl radical.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The bond order in $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{2-}$ is-
questions.solution:
Bond order of $\mathrm{O}_{2}{ }^{2-}$
Total electron $=18$
Configuration $=\mathrm{KK} \sigma(2 \mathrm{~s})^{2} \sigma^{*}(2 \mathrm{~s})^{2} \sigma\left(2 \mathrm{p}_{z}\right)^{2} \pi\left(2 \mathrm{p}_{x}\right)^{2} \pi\left(2 p_{y}\right)^{2} \pi^{*}\left(2 \mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2} \pi^{*}\left(2 \mathrm{p}_{y}\right)^{2}$
Bond order $=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{b}}-\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2}=\frac{8-6}{2}=1.0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Strength of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> solution is labeled as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>79</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. Its strength can also be expressed as closest to
questions.solution:
2H2O2aq.⟶2H2Oℓ+O2g

General Solution :- 

It is a common trend that n-factor H2O2 is
taken as '2' 

By the definition of volume strength of H2O2 if we consume 1ℓ of 1 N H2O2 in the above equation we are using 1 gram equivalent of
H2O2≡0.5 moles of H2O2 (by using n-factor =2)

This will produce 14 moles of O2 gas at N.T.P.

≡14×22.4=5.6ℓ of O2 gas

i.e. 1ℓ,1 N H2O2 solution gives 5.6ℓ O2 at N.T.P

Hence 1 N≡5.6 'vol.' H2O2 solution

In the given question it is 1.76 N H2O2 solution Hence volume strength =5.6×1.79≃10 volumes

Suggested Solution :- 

2H2O2aq.⟶2H2Oℓ+O2g

The above equation is a classic example of
disproportionation of H2O2, hence we should take n-factor of H2O2=1

1ℓ,1 N H2O2 solution ≡1 Gm equivalent of H2O2

≡1 mole of H2O2

This will produce 12 moles of O2 gas at N.T.P.

=12×22.4=11.2ℓ of O2 gas

i.e. 1ℓ,1 N H2O2 solution gives 11.2ℓ of O2 gas at N.T.P.

Hence 1 N≡11.2 'vol' H2O2 solution 

In the given question it is 1.79 NH2O2 solution

Hence, volume strength =1.79×11.2≃10 volumes

Suggested Answer is option (A)

BaSO4⇌Ba2++SO42-                      S             S

Solubility (in mol/L) × Molecular weight

=233×10-5=2.3×10-3

CuIs⇌Cu+aq+I-aq

Ksp=Cu+I-

If its solubility =x, at equilibrium.,Cu+=x and I-=x,Ksp=x2.

Since, Ksp=4×10-12,x=2×10-6 M

Ag2CrO4s⇌2Ag+aq+CrO2-aq

If its solubility =y, at eqbm., Ag+=2y and CrO42-=y

4y3=4×10-12,

y=10-4 M

=2×10-2=0.02

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A buffer solution can be prepared by mixing equal volumes of
questions.solution:
The solutions which resist change in pH on dilution or with the addition of small amounts of acid or alkali are called buffer solution. It is a mixture of weak base with its salt or weak acid with its salt.

Thus, buffer solution can be prepared by mixing equal volumes of 0.2 M NH4OH (weak base) and 0.1 M HCl (strong acid). It results in the formation of NH4OH (weak base) +NH4Cl (salt of weak base NH4OH ), which is a basic buffer mixture.

The H +ion concentration of an aqueous solution is 0.001M or 1×10-3M

Since.  we know that pH=-logH+
Using this equation, by plugging in the values

pH=-log10-3=-(-3) log10=3
pH=3
We know that
pOH=14-pH=14-3=11

∴  pOH=11

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The tendency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>BX</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>Cl</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>OMe</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>NMe</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> to form a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi></math>-bond with boron follows the order
questions.solution:
Boron atom has an incomplete octet. It can accept a lone pair from X given in the question and can also donate electron to it, thus it can form a π-bond also known as back bonding.

The extent of back bonding depends upon the electronegativity of X and on the size of the valence shell. Lesser is the electronegativity of X and similar is the size of valence shell of X and B, more will be the extend of back bonding.

Cl and F are more electronegative than OMe and NMe group, so there extent of back bonding will be least. Among BCl3 and BF3, Cl will have least tendency to form π-bond with boron, because of large size of its 3p-orbital.

Among B(OMe)3 and BNMe23, NMe
will have the maximum tendency to form π-bond because N in NMe is less electronegative than O in OMe group. Thus, the tendency to form π-bond with boron follows the order.

BCl3<BF3<B(OMe)3<BNMe23

The hybridisation of any compound can be calculated as,

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The oxoacid of phosphorus having the strongest reducing property is
questions.solution:
The extent of reducing property is determined by the oxidation state of central atom. More is the oxidation state lesser will be the reducing property. The oxidation states of P in given oxoacids are as follows:

(i) H3PO3

3+1+x+3-2=0

3+x-6=0

x=+3

(ii) H3PO2

3+1+x+2-2=0

3+x-4=0

x=+1

(iii) H3PO4

3+1+x+4-2=0

3+x-8=0

x=+5

(iv) H4P2O7

4+1+2x+7-2=0

4+2x-14=0

x=+5

As the oxidation state of P is least in H3PO2. Thus, it has the strongest reducing property.

In NH42Cr2O7

=2+1+2x+7-2=0

2+2x-14=0

x=+6

In Cr2O3

2x+3-2==

2x-6=0

questions.q:
In a titration experiment, 10 mL of an FeCl2 solution consumed 25 mL of a standard K2Cr2O7 solution to reach the equivalent point. The standard K2Cr2O7 solution is prepared by dissolving 1.225 g of K2Cr2O7 in 250 mL water. The concentration of the FeCl2 solution is closest to

[Given : molecular weight of K2Cr2O7=294 g mol-1]
questions.solution:
In titration at equivalence point, number of equivalents of both reactants must be same.

∴ N1V1=N2V2

N (normality) of FeCl2=N×V of K2Cr2O7V of FeCl2

N (normality) of K2Cr2O7= Mass ×1000 Equivalent mass × Volume of solution prepared

Equivalent mass = Molar mass n factor 

=2946

=49 g/equi.

(K2Cr2O7 as oxidising agent is reduced to 2Cr3+).

Oxidation state of 2 Cr atoms change from +6 to +3.

Total change is3×2=6, (∴n-factor =6)

∴ N (of K2Cr2O7) =1225×100049×250=0.1N

∴ N (of FeCl2) =0.1×2510

=0.25 N

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A mineral consists of a cubic close-packed structure formed by $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions where half the octahedral voids are occupied by $\mathrm{Al}^{3+}$ and oneeighth of the tetrahedral voids are occupied by $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$. The chemical formula of the mineral is
questions.solution:
No of $\mathrm{O}^{-2}$ per unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}+6 \times \frac{1}{2}=4$
No of $\mathrm{Al}^{+3}$ per unit cell $=4 \times \frac{1}{2}=2$
No of $\mathrm{Mn}^{+2}$ per unit cell $=8 \times \frac{1}{8}=1$ 
$\mathbf{M n A l}_{2} \mathbf{O}_{4}$

The unit cell of the above pattern will consist of 8 white square and 1 black square i.e. it will form centre unit cell.
$\therefore$ No. of white square $\mathrm{Y}=8$
No. of black square $X=1$
$\therefore$ Formula $\mathbf{X Y}_{\mathbf{8}}$

$\mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{N} \times \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \mathrm{a}^{3}}$
$9.32=\frac{1 \times 209}{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times \mathrm{a}^{3}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}^{3}=37.2 \times 10^{-24 \mathrm{~S}}$
$\mathrm{a}=3.33 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$
$\cong 334 \mathrm{pm}$

$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To obtain a diffraction peak, for a crystalline solid with interplane distance equal to the wavelength of incident $X$-ray radiation, the angle of incidence should be
questions.solution:
From Bragg's equation
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{n} \lambda=2 \mathrm{~d} \sin \theta \\
\mathrm{d}=\lambda, \mathrm{n}=1 \\
1 \times \lambda=2 \times \lambda \sin \theta \\
\sin \theta=\frac{1}{-} \\
\theta=30^{\circ}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A two dimensional solid is made by alternating circles with radius a and b such that the sides of the circles touch. The packing fraction is defined as the ratio of the area under the circles to the area under the rectangle with sides of length x and y.

The ratio r=b/a for which the packing fraction is minimized is closest to:
questions.solution:
Area of rectangle=xy

=2a(2a+2b)

=4a(a+b)

Area covered by circles=πa2+πb2=πa2+b2

Packing fraction (P.F.)=πa2+b24a2+ab

=πa21+b2a24a21+ba

Putting r=ba

P.F.=π1+r24(1+r)

For minimum P.F., d(P.F.)dr=0

or π42r(1+r)-1+r2(1+r)2=0

⇒r2+2r-1=0

or r=-2+4+42=2-1=0.414

Answer is option (A)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The boiling point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> aqueous solutions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaCl</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math> should follows the order.
questions.solution:
Elevation in boiling point, ΔTb=i×Kb×m. (Kb is constant for a solvent)

If molality m is same, ΔTb∝ van't Hoff factor (i) CH3COOH only partially dissociates,

∴ i<2

NaCl,Na2SO4

and K3PO4 almost completely dissociate to produce 2,3 and 4 ions per formula respectively,

∴ i≈2,3, and 4 respectively

∴ The order of i = the order of boiling points

=CH3COOH<NaCl<Na2SO4<K3PO4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $18 \mathrm{~g}$ of glucose $\left(\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6}\right)$ dissolved in $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water is heated to boiling. The boiling point (in $\mathrm{K}$ ) measured at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure is closest to [Ebulioscopic constant, $K_{\mathrm{b}}$ for water is $0.52 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Consider absolute zero to be $\left.-273.15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right]$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{b}}=& \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{b}} \times \mathrm{m} \\=& 0.52 \times \frac{18 / 180}{1} \\ &=0.052 \\ \therefore \mathrm{B} \cdot \mathrm{P} &=373.15+0.052 \\ &=373.2 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}$

questions.q: The boiling point (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mprescripts/><none/><mo>∘</mo></mmultiscripts></math>) of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn></math> molal aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> at 1 bar is closest to: [Given: Ebullioscopic (molal boiling point elevation) constant of water, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="" close="]"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>512</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>KKgmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mo>-</mo></math>
questions.solution:
i=2

∆Tb=i·Kb·m=2×0.512×0.1=0.1024
 Tb'=Tb0+ΔTb=100+0.1024=100.10

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of oxalic acid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>·</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is dissolved in a solvent to prepare a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> solution. The density of the solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mL</mi></math>. The molality and normality of the solution, respectively, are closest to
questions.solution:
Molality = Mass of solute ×1000 Molar mass of solute × Mass of solvent 

Mass of solvent 

=250 mL×19 g/mL=475 g

∴ Mass of solvent =472 g

Molar mass of CO2H2.2H2O=126 g mol-1

∴ Molality =3×1000472×126

=0.05 mol kg-1

Normality = Number of equivalents of solute  Volume of solution l

= Mass of solute g×1000 Equivalent mass of solute × Volume of solution mL

Equivalent mass of oxalic acid

=Molarmass2

=63 g/equi.

∴ Normality =3×100063×250

=0.19 (equivalents)/l

or 0.19 N

questions.q: $2.52 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxalic acid dehydrate was disved in $100 \mathrm{ml}$ of water, $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of this ution was diluted to 500 $\mathrm{mL} .$ The normality of the final ution and the amount of oxalic acid $(\mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ ) in the ution are respectively-
questions.solution:
Initial Normality
$$
\mathrm{N}=\frac{2.52 \times 1000}{63 \times 100}=0.4
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
{ }_{\mathrm{COOH}} \\
\therefore \mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \\
0.4 \times 10=\mathrm{N}_{2} \times 500 \\
\mathrm{~N}_{2}=\frac{0.4}{50}=0.08 \mathrm{~N}
\end{array}
$$
Then final weight $\quad N=\frac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{m}}$
$$
0.08=\frac{w \times 1000}{63 \times 500}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A mixture of toluene and benzene boils at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Assuming ideal behaviour, the mole fraction of toluene in the mixture is closest to [Vapour pressures of pure toluene and pure benzene at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $0.742$ and $1.800$ bar respectively. 1 atm $=1.013$ bar $]$
questions.solution:
$1.013=0.742 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{t}}+1.8 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$1.013=0.742\left(1-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{b}}\right)+1.8 \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{b}}$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.256$
$\therefore \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{t}}=1-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{b}}=0.744$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The values of the Henry's law constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Ar</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>41</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>34</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>86</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kbar</mi></math>, respectively. The order of their solubility in water at the same temperature and pressure is
questions.solution:
By Henry's law, 

pgas ∝Xgas  (in liquid solution)

and pgas =KH×χgas 

KH= Henry's law constant.

∴ At a given partial pressure of gas, 

KH∝1χgas

∴ The greater the value of KH, the less its solubility.

Here, KH of Ar>O2>CO2>CH4.

∴ Solubility of CH4>CO2>O2>Ar.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The weight percent of sucrose (formula weight $\left.=342 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$ in an aqueous solution is $3.42$. The density of the solution is $1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$, the concentration of sucrose in the solution in $\mathrm{mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$ is
questions.solution:
$3.42 \mathrm{~gm}$ sacrose in $100 \mathrm{~gm}$ solution
$\mathrm{d}=1 \mathrm{~gm} \mathrm{~ml}^{-1}$
$\because \mathrm{d}=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}$
volume of solution $=\frac{100}{1}=100 \mathrm{~ml}$
Molarity $=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{v}} \times 1000$
Molarity $=\frac{3.42}{342 \times 100} \times 1000=0.1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In $108 \mathrm{~g}$ of water, $18 \mathrm{~g}$ of a non-volatile compound is dissolved. At $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the vapor pressure of the solution is $750 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$. Assuming that the compound does not undergo association or dissociation, the molar mass of the compound in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{N}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}}-\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{S}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}}}$
$\frac{18}{\mathrm{~m}} \times \frac{18}{108}=\frac{760-750}{760}$
$\frac{18}{6 \mathrm{~m}}=\frac{10}{760}$
$6 \mathrm{~m}=18 \times 76$
$\mathrm{~m}=228$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The plot of total vapour pressure as a function of mole fraction of the components of an ideal solution formed by mixing liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
According to Raoult's law,

p=p0χ

According to Dalton's law of partial pressure,

pT=px+py

pT=p0xχx+p0yχy

=p0xχx+py01-χx

pT=p0Y+p0x-p0yχx

Thus, total vapour pressure of solution varies linearly with mole fraction of component X.

Case I, at χX=0

pT=p0Y

Case II, at XX=1

pT=p0Y+p0X-p0Y

pT=pX0

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $300 \mathrm{~K}$ the vapour pressure of two pure liquids, $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are 100 and $500 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$, respectively. If in a mixture of $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, the vapour pressure is $300 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$, the mole fractions of $\mathrm{A}$ in the liquid and in the vapour phase, respectively, are-
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{100 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{100 \cdot \frac{1}{2}+500 \cdot \frac{1}{2}}$
$=\frac{50}{50+250}$
$=\frac{50}{300}$
$=1 / 6$
$\because \mathrm{PS}=\left(\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0}-\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0}\right) \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0}$
$300=(-400) \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}+500$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of water molecules in 250 m L of water is closest to [Given, density of water is 1.0 g m L-1; Avogadro's number =6.023×1023
questions.solution:
Given, density of water =1.0 g m L-1
volume of water =250 m L

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution (5 $\mathrm{mL}$ ) of an acid $\mathrm{X}$ is completely neutralized by y $\mathrm{mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaOH}$. The same volume (y $\mathrm{ML}$ ) of $1 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{NaOH}$ is required to neutralize $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.6 \mathrm{M}$ of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ completely. The normality (N) of the acid $\mathrm{X}$ is
questions.solution: (No. of eq. $)_{\mathrm{NaOH}}=\left(\right.$ No. of eq.) $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ $\Rightarrow(1 \times 1) \times \mathrm{y}=(0.6 \times 2) \times 10$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=12 \mathrm{ml}$ Now, $(\text { No. of eq. })_{\text {acid }}=(\mathrm{No} . \text { of eq. })_{\mathrm{NaOH}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{N} \times 5=(1 \times 1) \times 12$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{N}=\frac{12}{5}=2.4$

questions.solution: $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1.25}{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{1.68}{\mathrm{E}+8}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{E}=23.25$ $\quad \mathrm{n}-$ factor $=\frac{69.7}{23.25} \approx 3$ $\therefore \quad$ Emprical formula $=\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$2.33 \mathrm{~g}$ of compound $\mathrm{X}$ (empirical formula $\left.\mathrm{CoH}_{12} \mathrm{~N}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right)$ upon treatment with excess $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution produces $1.435 \mathrm{~g}$ of a white precipitate. The primary and secondary valences of cobalt in compound $\mathrm{X}$, respectively, are
[Given : Atomic mass : $\mathrm{Co}=59, \mathrm{Cl}=35.5, \mathrm{Ag}=108$ ]
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { M.wt }=59+12+14 \times 4+35.5 \times 3=233.5 \\
\mathrm{C}_{0} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{~N}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{3} \stackrel{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}{\longrightarrow} 1.435 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{AgCl} \\
\frac{2.33}{233.5} \mathrm{~g}=0.01 \text { mole } \quad(0.01 \mathrm{~mole})
\end{array}
$$
(i) $0.01$ mole molecule produce $0.01$ mole $\mathrm{AgCl}$ $\therefore$ one replaceable $\mathrm{Cl}^{\underline{\Theta}}$ ion so formula of complex is
$$
\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}
$$
(ii) Oxidation no. of Co is $+3$ so primary valency is 3 .
(iii) Coordination no. is 6 so sec. valency is 6 so ans. is 3,6

questions.solution:
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|}
\hline Elements & \% & moles & Simplest ratio \\
\hline \mathrm{Na} & 18.6 & \frac{18.6}{23}=0.8 & 1 \times 2 \\
\hline \mathrm{S} & 25.8 & \frac{25.8}{32}=0.8 & 1 \times 2 \\
\hline \mathrm{O} & 51.58 & \frac{51.58}{16}=3.22 & 4 \times 2 \\
\hline \mathrm{H} & 4.02 & \frac{4.02}{1}=4.02 & 5 \times 2 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$\Rightarrow$ Formula is $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

$\eta_{\mathrm{AS}}=\eta_{\mathrm{ASO}_{4}^{3}}=0.00133$

$\%$ of $\mathrm{AS}=\frac{0.0996}{1.85} \times 100=5.4 \%$

questions.q: Natural abundances of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ and ${ }^{13} \mathrm{C}$ isotopes of carbon are $99 \%$ and $1 \%$, respectively. Assuming they only contributes to the mol. wt. of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{F}_{4}$, the percentage of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{F}_{4}$ having a molecular mass of $101$ is
questions.solution:
Percentage of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{F}_{4}$ of Molar mass $100=\frac{1}{100} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 100=0.01 \%$
Percentage of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{F}_{4}$ of Molar mass $102=\frac{99}{100} \times \frac{99}{100} \times 100=98.01 \%$
Percentage of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{F}_{4}$ of Molar mass $101=100-(0.01+98.01)=1.98 \%$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, the correct statement is :
questions.solution:
There are certain rules for determining the number of significant figures. These are stated below:
(1) All non-zero digits are significant. 
(2) Zeros preceding to first non-zero digit are not significant. Such zero indicates the position of decimal point.
(3) Zeros between two non-zero digits are significant.

(4) Zeros at the end or right of a number are significant provided they are on the right side of the decimal point. 

Hence, 2.005 has four significant figures.

(ii) TC is the highest temperature at which the gas can be liquefied

(iv) At point A, a small increase in pressure condenses the whole system to a liquid

The correct statements are :
questions.solution:
Since T1>TC, the gas cannot be liquefied at T1, TC is the highest temperature at which the gas can be liquefied. 

At temperature T2, liquid starts to appear at point B, however a small increase in pressure at point A  condenses the whole system to liquid.

$P V=n R T$
$\Rightarrow \frac{V}{T}=\frac{n R}{P}=$ slope
$\Rightarrow P=\frac{n R}{\text { slope }}=\frac{2 \times 0.0821}{0.328}=0.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, the incorrect statement is
options: no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers
(b) the maximum number of electrons in the shell with principal quantum number, $n$ is equal to $n^{2}+2$
electrons in an orbital must have opposite spin
in the ground state, atomic orbitals are filled in the order of their increasing energies
questions.solution:
Consider the following statements.

(I) According to Pauli's exclusion principle, no two electrons in an atom can have the same set of four quantum numbers.

Thus, statement (a) is correct.
(II) The maximum number of electrons in the shell with principle quantum number, n=2n2

Thus, statement (b) is incorrect.
(III) Electrons in an orbital must have opposite spin, i.e. ms=+12 and -12. Thus, statement (c) is correct.

(IV) According to Aufbau principle, in the ground state of the atoms, the orbitals are filled in order of their increasing energies. Thus, statement (d) is correct.

questions.solution:
Mav $=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}$
$35.45=\frac{35 \mathrm{n}_{1}+37 \mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{n}_{1}: \mathrm{n}_{2}=3: 1$

It is irradiated first with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mi>nm</mi></math> light and later with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mi>nm</mi></math> light. Among the following, the correct statement is: [Given:Planck constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></mfenced></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>626</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>kgs</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> Speed of light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
options: Both colors of light give rise to same number of photoelectrons.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mi>nm</mi></math> light gives rise to less energetic photoelectrons than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mi>nm</mi></math> light.
questions.solution:
Work function of metal ϕ=2eV

Energy of photon λ=400nm=hcλ=3.105eV

Energy of photon λ=800nm=hcλ=1.5525eV

Hence, photon with λ=400nm will emit photoelectrons while photon with λ=800nm will not emit photoelectrons.

questions.solution: The ideal body which emits and adsorbs radiations of all frequencies is called a black body and the radiation emitted by such a body is called black body radiation. The exact frequency distribution of the emitted radiation from black body depends only on its temperature. At a given temperature intensity of radiation emitted increases with decrease of wavelength, reaches a maximum value at a given wavelength and then starts decreasing with further decrease of wavelength.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following statements about Langmuir isotherm.
I. The free gas and adsorbed gas are in dynamic equilibrium.
II. All adsorption sites are equivalent.
III. The initially adsorbed layer can act as a substrate for further adsorption.
IV. The ability of a molecule to get adsorbed at a given site is independent of the occupation of neighboring sites.

The correct statements are
questions.solution: Langmuir isotherm gives the relationship between the release of adsorbed gas and pressure. The Langmuir isotherm assumes that the gas attaches to the surface of adsorbent. The free gas and adsorbed gas are in dynamic equilibrium. The surface of the adsorbent is uniform that is, all the adsorption sites are equivalent. The ability of a molecule to get adsorbed at a given site is independent of the occupation of neighbouring sites. Thus, statements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>II</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>IV</mi></math> are correct.

moles of gas remaining $=9.7$ at constant $(T) \&(V)$
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}} \\
\frac{10}{9.7}=\frac{1}{p_{2}} \& p_{2}=0.97
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the size of a spherical nanoparticle decreases from $30 \mathrm{~nm}$ to $10 \mathrm{~nm}$, the ratio surface area/volume becomes
questions.solution:
$\frac{\text { Surfacearea }}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{2}}{\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}^{3}}{6}}=\frac{6}{\mathrm{~d}}$
$\mathrm{~d}_{1}=30 \mathrm{~nm} \quad \mathrm{~d}_{2}=10 \mathrm{~nm}$
$\left(\frac{\text { Surface area }}{\text { Volume }}\right)_{2}=\frac{\frac{6}{\mathrm{~d}_{2}}}{\frac{6}{\mathrm{~d}_{1}}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}_{1}}{\mathrm{~d}_{2}}=\frac{30}{10}=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a spontaneous process,
options: enthalpy change of the system must be negative
entropy change of the system must be positive
entropy change of the surrounding must be positive
entropy change of the system plus surrounding must be positive
questions.solution:
For a spontaneous process in an isolated system, the change in entropy is positive, i.e, ΔS>O.

However, if a system is not isolated, the entropy change of both the system and surroundings are to be taken into account because system and surroundings together constitute the isolated system thus, the total entropy change ΔStotal is sum of the change in entropy of the system ΔSsystem  and the change in entropy of the surroundings ΔSpurroundings 
i.e., ΔStotal =ΔSsystem +ΔSSurrounding  for a spontenceus process, ΔStotal  must be positive, i.e., ΔStotal  is also termed as ΔSuniverse"

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Products are favoured in a chemical reaction taking place at a constant temperature and pressure. Consider the following statements:
(i) The change in Gibbs energy for the reaction is negative.
(ii) The total change in Gibbs energy for the reaction and the surroundings is negative.
(iii) The change in entropy for the reaction is positive.
(iv) The total change in entropy for the reaction and the surroundings is positive.

The statements which are ALWAYS true are:-
questions.solution:
Since products are formed in the chemical reaction taking place at constant temperature and pressure, we can say that the reaction is spontaneous.

Hence, ΔGreaction <0
ΔStotal >0

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A system consisting of 1 mol of an ideal gas undergoes a reversible process, $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C} \rightarrow \mathrm{A}$ (schematically indicated in the figure below). If the temperature at the starting point $\mathrm{A}$ is $300 \mathrm{~K}$ and the work done in the process $\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}$ is $1 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm}$, the heat exchanged in the entire process in $\mathrm{L}$ atm is
questions.solution:
For process $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{W}=0, \Delta \mathrm{E}=0, \mathrm{q}=0$ For process $\mathrm{A} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}$ $\mathrm{W}=-1(1.5-1)=-0.5$ lit. atm $\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$ $0=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W}$ $\mathrm{q}=-\mathrm{W}=-(-0.5)=0.5$ lit. atm
For process $\mathrm{B} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{W}=1 \mathrm{~atm}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Delta \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{q}+\mathrm{W} \\
0=\mathrm{q}-\mathrm{W} \\
-\mathrm{q}=\mathrm{W}=-1 \mathrm{~atm}
\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Total heat exchanged $=1.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The maximum work (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) that can be derived from complete combustion of $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{CO}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ is
[Standard enthalpy of combustion of $\mathrm{CO}=-283.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} ;$ standard molar entropies at $298 \mathrm{~K} ; \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{O} 2}=205.1 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ $\left.\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{CO}}=197.7 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \mathrm{~S}_{\mathrm{CO} 2}=213.7 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{g})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \Delta \mathrm{N}=-283.5$
$\Delta \mathrm{S}=213.7-197.7-\frac{20501}{2}=-86.5$
$\Delta \mathrm{G}=-283-\frac{298 \times(-86.5)}{1000}=-2570 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\mathrm{~W}_{\max }=-\Delta \mathrm{G}=-(-257 \mathrm{~kJ})=257 \mathrm{~kJ}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A thermodynamic cycle in the pressure P-volume V plane is given below:

AB and CD are isothermal processes while BC and DA are adiabatic processes. The same cycle in the temperature T- entropy S plane is :
questions.solution:
AB is isothermal reversible expansion process i.e. ΔT=0 and S increases as there is increase in volume.

BC is adiabatic reversible expansion process qrev=0 i.e. temperature decreases and ΔS=0

CD is isothermal reversible compression process i.e. ΔT=0 and S decreases as there is decrease in volume.

DA is adiabatic reversible compression process qres=0 i.e. temperature decreases and ΔS=0

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The specific heat of a certain substance is 0.86 J g-1 K-1. Assuming ideal solution behavior, the energy required (in J) to heat 10 g of 1 molal of its aqueous solution from 300 K to 310 K is closest to :

[Given: molar mass of the substance =58 g mol-1; specific heat of water =4.2 J g-1 K-1]
questions.solution:
Specific heat capacity of substance =0.86 J g-1 K-1

1 molal aqueous solution 

⇒1000 gm water has 58 gm solute 

(total mass of solution =1058 gm)

If we take 10 gm solution it would have 

water =10001058×10 gm

substance =581058×10 gm

Heat required =10001058×10×4.2×10  (for water) =396.975

581058×10×0.86×10  (for substance) =4.715

=396.975+4.715=401.69≃401.7

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Nitroglycerine MW=227.1 detonates according to the following equations,

2C3H5NO33l⟶3 N2g+12O2g+6CO2g+5H2Og

The standard molar enthalpies of formation, ΔHf° for all the compounds are given below.

ΔHf°C3H5NO33=-364 kJ/mol
ΔHf°CO2g=-393.5 kJ/mol
ΔHf°H2Og=-241.8 kJ/mol
ΔHf°N2g=0 kJ/mol
ΔHf°O2g=0 kJ/mol

The enthalpy change when 10 g of nitroglycerine is detonated is
questions.solution:
ΣHReaction°=ΣHfP°-ΣHf(R)°

ΔHReaction°=3×0+12×0+6×-393.5+5×-241.8-2×-364

=-2361-1209+728

=-2842 kJ/mol

For 2 moles of nitroglycerine enthalpy
-2842 kJ/mol

∴ For 1 mole of nitroglycerine enthalpy
=-28422=-1421 kJ/mol

227.1 g of C3H5NO33 has enthalpy -1421 kJ/mol.

∴10 g of C3H5NO33 has enthalpy

=-1421227.1×10

=-62.5 kJ/mol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A container is divided into two compartments by a removable partition as shown below:

In the first compartment, n1 moles of ideal gas He is present in a volume V1. In the second compartment, n2 moles of ideal gas Ne is present in a volume V2. The temperature and pressure in both the compartments are T and P, respectively. Assuming R is the gas constant, the total change in entropy upon removing the partition when the gases mix irreversibly is:
questions.solution:
Entropy change ΔS=nCvℓn

T2 T1+nRℓnV2 V1

Since temperature is constant throughout process.

He:ΔS=n1RℓnV1+V2 V1

Ne:ΔS=n2RℓnV1+V2 V2

+n2RℓnV1+V2 V2

questions.q: Given that, the molar combustion enthalpy of benzene, cyclohexane and hydrogen are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>, respectively, the molar enthalpy of hydrogenation of benzene to cyclohexane is
questions.solution:
Hydrogenation of benzene is given by

C6H6+3H2⟶C6H12  ...(a)

Enthalpies are given for, 

C6H6+152O2⟶6CO2+3H2O; ΔH=x  ...(b)

C6H12+9O2⟶6CO2+6H2O; ΔH=y  ...(c)

H2+12O2⟶H2; ΔH=z  ...(d)

Reaction (a)=(b)-(c)+3(d)

∴ ΔH of a=x-y+3z

Given
$\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) & \Delta \mathrm{H}=-198.9 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\

\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) & \Delta \mathrm{H}=+495.0 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
\end{array}$
The entalpy change $(\Delta \mathrm{H})$ for the following reaction is
$\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})+\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \Delta \mathrm{H}=-198.9 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\mathrm{O}_{3} \longrightarrow \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \Delta \mathrm{H}=-142.3 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\mathrm{O}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \Delta \mathrm{H}=+495.0 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mole}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}$ of $\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
For this $\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{ii}-\frac{\mathrm{iii}}{2}$
$=-198.9-(-142.3)-\frac{495}{2}$
$=-304.1 \mathrm{KJ} / \mathrm{mole}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A sector is removed from a metallic disc and the remaining region is bent into the shape of a circular conical funnel with volume $2 \sqrt{3} \pi$. The least possible diameter of the disc is
questions.solution: for a cone $\mathrm{r}$ will be cone slant height $$ V=2 \sqrt{3} $$ Let $x$ radius of cone $\mathrm{h}$ be height then $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=2 \sqrt{3} \pi \\ x^{2} h=6 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{h} \end{array} $$ least Diameter $\Rightarrow$ least slant height of cone $\ell^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{h}^{2}$ $\mathrm{r}^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{h}^{2}$ $$ r^{2}=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{h}+h^{2} $$ Diff. w. to $\mathrm{x}$ $$ 2 r \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dh}}=\frac{-6 \sqrt{3}}{\mathrm{~h}^{2}}+2 \mathrm{~h} $$ for maximum \& minimum $\frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{dh}}=0$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{h}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{h}^{2}=3 \\ \mathrm{x}^{2}=\frac{6 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \mathrm{x}^{2}=6 \\ \mathrm{r}^{2}=6+3 \\ \mathrm{r}^{2}=9 \\ \mathrm{r}=3 \\ \mathrm{~d}=2 \mathrm{r} \\ \mathrm{d}=6 \end{array} $$

questions.q: The number of polynomials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>></mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> for all <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>p</mi><mrow><mo>'</mo><mo>'</mo></mrow></msup><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

px>x2,p0=0,p''0=12

Let gx=px-x2

gx>0,∀x≠0

and g0=p0-0=0

⇒x=0 should be minima.

∵g''x should be≥0 at x=0

Now, g'x=p'x-2x

g''x2=p''x-2

g''0=p''0-2=12-2=-32

But g''0≥0

∵ No polynomial exists.

questions.q:
Let R be the set of all real numbers and f(x)=sin10xcos8x+cos4x+cos2x+1
for x∈R. Let S={λ∈R∣ there exits a point c∈(0,2π) with f'(c)=λf(c)
Then
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is a finite set having more than one element
questions.solution:
Let g(x)=f(x)e-λx;x∈[0,2π]
so, g(0)=g(2π)=0 as f(0)=f(2π)=0
Thus, ∃c∈(0,2π)
such that g'(c)=0
⇒f'(c)=λf(c) ∀λ∈R
⇒S=R

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A solid hemisphere is mounted on a solid cylinder, both having equal radii. If the whole solid is to have a fixed surface area and the maximum possible volume, then the ratio of the height of the cylinder to the common radius is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{S}=2 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}+2 \pi \mathrm{Rh}+\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \\
(\mathrm{R}=\text { radius of hemisphere \& cylinder) } \\
\mathrm{V}=\frac{2}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3}+\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \mathrm{~h} \\
\mathrm{~V}=\frac{2}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3}+\pi \mathrm{R}^{2} \times\left(\frac{5-3 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}\right) \\
\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dR}}=2 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}+\frac{5}{2}-\frac{9 \pi}{2} \mathrm{R}^{2}
\end{array}
$$
for maximum \& minimum $\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{dR}}=0$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
5 \pi R^{2}=S \\
5 \pi R^{2}=3 \pi R^{2}+2 \pi R h \\
2 R=2 h \\
h: R=1: 1
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A rectangle with its sides parallel to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>-axis is inscribed in the region bounded by the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The maximum possible area of such a rectangle is closest to the integer.
questions.solution:
Equation of parabola

y=x2-4 and 2y=4-x2

Let point B=h,4-h22

A=-h,4-h22

C=h1h2-4

D=-h,h2-4

Area of rectangle

ABCD=AB×BC=2h×4-h22-h2+4

⇒A=12h-3h3⇒dAdh=12-9h2

For maxima or minima

dAdh=0⇒12-9h2=0

h=±23 maximum at h=23

∴A=1223-3233=243-83

=163=1633

A-16×1733=9.22

A=9.22=9

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let f(x)=sinx+x3-3x2+4x-2cosx for x∈(0,1). Consider the following statements
I. f has a zero in 0, 1
II. f is monotone in 0, 1
Then
options: I is true and II are false
I is false and II are true
questions.solution:
fx=sinx+x3-3x2+4x-2cosx,x∈(0,1)

x∈(0,1)

f(0)=-2>0

f(1)=sin1<0

∵f(0)·f(1)<0⇒f(x) has a zero in 0, 1

Now,

fx=sinx+(x-1)3+(x-1)cosx

⇒f'x=3(x-1)2+2cosx-sinx(x-1)3+x-1

=3(x-1)2+2cosx+(1-x)3+(1-x)sinx

>0∀x∈(0,1)

⇒f(x)is monotone in 0, 1

questions.solution: $\int_{0}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{n} \int_{0}^{1} \mathrm{~g}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{n} \quad \int_{0}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{4}$

questions.q: The range of the polynomial $p(x)=4 x^{3}-3 x$ as $x$ varies over the interval $\left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right)$ is
questions.solution:

$\mathrm{In}\left(-\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{P}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) < 0$
