Let Ravi picks a Red card from Rashmi and Rashmi picks black card from Ravi and in second time also Ravi picks a red card from Rashmi and Rashmi picks black card from Ravi, then finally Ravi have four red cards and Rashmi have four black cards, then probability of such event is

C12C14×C12C15×C11C14×C11C15=4400=1100

Similarly, the probability if at the end Ravi have four black and Rashmi have four red cards is 1100.

So, required probability =150=2%=p≤5%

⇒cosθ2sinθ2=2cosθ1sinθ1

⇒2b2+2a2=10c2

⇒a2+b2=5c2  ...(ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get 

cosθ=0

⇒θ=π2

give any C and T as above, the ratio  Area (C) Area (T) is
independent of C and T

there exist real numbers a and b such that for any circle C and triangle T as above, we must have <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mrow><mtext> Area </mtext><mo>(</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mtext> Area </mtext><mo>(</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></mfrac><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi></math>

Now, as we know that area of triangle will be maximum for given perimeter if it is an equilateral triangle, let the length of side of equilateral triangle is ' a, then
3a=l⇒a=l3
and area of equilateral triangle is

A2=34a2 

So, A2=34l29=l2123 ∵A1A2=l24πl2123=33π>1

since, as we took an equilateral triangle, which has maximum area. But we can take a triangle T such that the ratio  area (C) area (T) is greater than any positive real number α.

questions.q: Let $A_{1} A_{2} A_{3} \ldots A_{9}$ be a nine-sided regular polygon with side length 2 units. The difference between the lengths of the diagonals $\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{5}$ and $\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{4}$ equals
questions.solution: $\angle \mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{OA}_{2}=\frac{2 \pi}{9}=40^{\circ}$ $\cos \frac{2 \pi}{9}=\frac{x^{2}+x^{2}-4}{2 x^{2}}$ $\left.x^{2} \cos 40^{\circ}\right)=x^{2}-2$ $x^{2}\left(1-\cos 40^{\circ}\right)=2$ $x=\frac{1}{\sin 20^{\circ}}$ .........(i) Now $\cos \frac{8 \pi}{9}=\frac{x^{2}+x^{2}-\left(A_{1} A_{5}\right)^{2}}{2 x^{2}}$ (in $\left.\Delta A_{1} O A_{5}\right)$ $\begin{array}{l} \left(A_{1} A_{5}\right)^{2}=2 x^{2}\left(1-\cos \frac{8 \pi}{9}\right) \\ =2 x^{2}\left(1-\cos 160^{\circ}\right) \\ =4 x^{2} \sin ^{2} 80^{\circ} \end{array}$ $\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{5}=2 \mathrm{x} \sin 80^{\circ}$ .........(ii) Similarly in $\triangle \mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{OA}_{4}$ $\mathrm{~A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{4}=2 \mathrm{x} \sin 40^{\circ}$ .........(iii) (ii) $-$ (iii) $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{~A}_{5}-\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~A}_{4} &=2 \mathrm{x}\left(\sin 80^{\circ}-\sin 40^{\circ}\right) \\ &=2(\text { using }(\mathrm{i})) \end{aligned}$

questions.solution: 'E' is mid point of $A D$. $\begin{array}{l} \cos 2 \theta=\frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow 2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1=\frac{1}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \cos ^{2} \theta=\frac{2}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \end{array}$ $\frac{\text { ED }}{1}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ $\mathrm{DH}=\mathrm{ED} \sec \theta$ $=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}$ $=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose $A B C D E F$ is a hexagon such that $A B=B C=C D=1$ and $D E=E F=F A=$ 2. If the vertices $A, B, C, D, E, F$ are concyclic, the radius of the circle passing through them is.
questions.solution: $$ \theta+\phi=120^{\circ} $$ So, $$ \begin{array}{l} \angle A=120^{\circ} \quad \because \mathrm{ABCDEF} \text { are concyclic } \\ \cos \mathrm{A}=\cos 120^{\circ}=\frac{1^{2}+2^{2}-F B^{2}}{2(1)(2)} \\ \frac{-1}{2}=\frac{5-F B^{2}}{2(2)} \Rightarrow F B^{2}=7 \\ F B=\sqrt{7} \end{array} $$ Again by cosine rule $$ \begin{array}{l} \cos (\theta+\phi)=\frac{r^{2}+r^{2}-7}{2 r^{2}} \\ -\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2 r^{2}-7}{2 r^{2}} \\ 3 r^{2}=7 \\ r=\sqrt{\frac{7}{3}} \end{array} $$

questions.q: The angle bisectors $B D$ and $C E$ of a triangle $A B C$ are divided by the incentre $l$ in the rators $3: 2$ and $2: 1$ respectively. Then the ratio in which I divides the angle bisector through A is.
questions.solution: $\because \frac{A l}{I F}=\frac{b+c}{a}\quad ......\text {(1)}$ $\because \frac{B l}{I D}=\frac{a+c}{b}=\frac{3}{2}\quad ......\text {(2)}$ $\begin{array}{l} \because \frac{C l}{I E}=\frac{a+c}{c}=\frac{2}{1} \\ \Rightarrow a+b=2 c\quad ......\text {(3)} \\ \text { (2) } 2 a+2 c=3 b \quad \text { using (2)}\\ \Rightarrow 2 a+a+b=3 b \quad \text { using (3)} \\ \Rightarrow 3 a=2 b \\ \Rightarrow \quad b=\frac{3}{2} a\quad ......\text {(4)} \\ \end{array}$ Now again (3) $\Rightarrow 2 c=a+b$ $\begin{array}{l} =a+\frac{3}{2} a \\ \Rightarrow c+\frac{5}{4} a \end{array}$ Hence $\frac{A l}{I F}=\frac{b+c}{a}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} a+\frac{5}{4} a}{a}=\frac{11}{4}$

questions.q: In a triangle $A B C$ with $\angle A=90^{\circ}, P$ is a point on $B C$ such that $P A: P B=3: 4$. If $A B=\sqrt{7}$ and $A C=\sqrt{5}$, then BP : PC is
questions.solution: Equation of line $A B$ is $\frac{x}{\sqrt{7}}+\frac{y}{\sqrt{5}}=1$ Let $\mathrm{P}\left[\alpha, \sqrt{5}\left(1-\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{7}}\right)\right]$ on solving $16(\mathrm{PA}) 2=9(\mathrm{~PB}) 2$ $P\left[\frac{\sqrt{7}}{3}, \frac{2 \sqrt{5}}{3}\right]$ Let $\mathrm{BP}: \mathrm{PC}=\lambda: 1$ then $\lambda=2$ $\mathrm{BP}: \mathrm{PC}=2: 1$

questions.solution:

since, A B=15 and B C=25∴  AC=BC2-AB2=625-225

=12(AB)(AC)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $A B C$ be an acute-angled triangle and let $D$ be the midpoint of $B C$. If $A B=A D$, then $\tan (B) / \tan (C)$ equals
questions.solution: Using M - N Rule $$ \begin{array}{l} (1+1) \cot (\pi-B)=1 \cdot \cot B-\cot C \\ 3 \cot B=\cot C \\ \frac{\tan B}{\tan C}=3 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of values of $b$ for which there is an isosceles triangle with sides of length $b+5,3 b-2$ and $6-b$ is
questions.solution:
Case (I)
$b+5=3 b-2$
$\Rightarrow b=\frac{7}{2}$
So sides are $\frac{17}{2}, \frac{17}{2}, \frac{5}{2}$
Case (II)
$b+5=6-b=b=\frac{1}{2}$
Sides $\frac{11}{2}, \frac{-1}{2}, \frac{11}{2}$ Not possible
Case (III) 
$3 \mathrm{~b}-2=6-\mathrm{b}$
$4 \mathrm{~b}=8$
$\mathrm{~b}=2$
$7,4,4$
two cases are possible.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{H}$ be the orthocenter of an acute - angled triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ and $\mathrm{O}$ be its circumcenter. Then $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HA}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HC}}$
options: is not a scalar multiple of $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HO}}$ in general
questions.solution:
G is centroid
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{G}=\frac{\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{C}}{3} \\
\mathrm{G}=\frac{20+\mathrm{H}}{3} \\
2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}=3 \mathrm{G} \\
\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HA}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{HC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}} \\
\quad=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{C}}-3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}} \\
\quad=3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{G}}-3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}} \\
\quad=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{O}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}}-3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}} \\
\quad=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{O}}-2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{H}} \\
\quad=2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{HO}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$, it is known that $\mathrm{AB}=\mathrm{AC}$. Suppose $\mathrm{D}$ is the mid- point of $\mathrm{AC}$ and $\mathrm{BD}=\mathrm{BC}=2$. Then the area of the triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ is $-$
questions.solution: We know $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{AB}^{2}+\mathrm{BC}^{2}=2\left(\mathrm{CD}^{2}+\mathrm{BD}^{2}\right) \\ \mathrm{AB}^{2}+4=2\left(\frac{\mathrm{AB}^{2}}{4}+4\right) \\ \mathrm{AB}^{2}+4=\frac{\mathrm{AB}^{2}}{2}+8 \\ \frac{\mathrm{AB}^{2}}{2}=4 \\ \mathrm{AB}^{2}=8 \\ \mathrm{AB}=2 \sqrt{2} \end{array}$ Now Area $=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times \cdot \sqrt{7}=\cdot \sqrt{7}$ square unit.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In triangle $A B C$, we are given that $3 \sin A+4 \cos B=6$ and $4 \sin B+3 \cos A=1$. Then the measure of the angle $C$ is -
questions.solution:
Square \& add both equations
$\begin{array}{r}
9+16+24 \sin (\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B})=37 \\
\sin (\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B})=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{B}=\frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow C=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \text { (wrong) } \\
\Rightarrow A+B=\frac{5 \pi}{6} \Rightarrow C=\frac{\pi}{6} \\
\text { because } \mathrm{C}=\frac{5 \pi}{6}
\end{array}$
does not follow equation $3 \sin A+4 \cos B=6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{ABC}$ be a triangle with $\angle \mathrm{B}=90^{\circ}$. Let $\mathrm{AD}$ be the bisector of $\angle \mathrm{A}$ with $\mathrm{D}$ on $\mathrm{BC}$. Suppose $\mathrm{AC}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the area of the triangle $\mathrm{ADC}$ is $10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. Then the length of $\mathrm{BD}$ in $\mathrm{cm}$ is equal to
questions.solution: From angle bisector theorem $\begin{array}{l} \frac{r}{6}=\frac{p}{q} \\ q r=6 p ...(1) \end{array}$ Area of $\triangle \mathrm{ADC}=10 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ $\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{DC})(\mathrm{AB})=10 \\ \frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{q})(\mathrm{r})=10 \\ \mathrm{q} \mathrm{r}=20 \end{array}$ From (1) $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow 20=6 \mathrm{p} \\ \mathrm{p}=\frac{20}{6}=\frac{10}{3} \end{array}$

questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> be an acute-angled triangle in which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi><mo><</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo></math>. From the vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> draw the altitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> the angle bisector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and the median <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math>, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></mrow></msub><msub><mi>Q</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></mrow></msub><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> lying on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math>. Then,
questions.solution:
Given, P Q R is an acute angle triangle.

P Q<Q R

 

∠QRP<∠QPR

 

PQ3=12PR

 

PQ2:Q2R=r:p

 

PQ2=rr+pPR

But   r<p

 

PQ2<12PR

Comparison between altitude and angle bisector

∠QPQ2+∠PQ2Q+∠PQQ2=∠RQQ2 +∠QQ2R+∠QRQ2

 

∴∠PQQ2=∠RQQ2

[since, QQ2 is angle bisector of ∠Q

 

∠QPQ2+∠PQ2Q=∠QQ2R+∠QRQ2

∴PQ<QR the ∠QPQ2<∠QRQ2

Hence, ∠QQ2P<∠QQ2R

But ∠QQ2P+∠QQ2R=180°

Hence, ∠QQ2P<90° and ∠QQ2R>90°

∵ Foot from Q to side P R lie inside Δ PQQ2

⇒  PQ1<PQ2<PQ3

questions.q: Let $\mathrm{ABC}$ be an equilateral triangle, let KLMN be a rectangle with $\mathrm{K}, \mathrm{L}$ on $\mathrm{BC}, \mathrm{M}$ on $\mathrm{AC}$ and $\mathrm{N}$ on $\mathrm{AB}$. Suppose AN $/ \mathrm{NB}=2$ and the area of triangle BKN is $6 .$ The area of the triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ is-
options: not determinable with the above data
questions.solution: $y=\frac{\sqrt{3} x}{2}$ $z=\frac{x}{2}$ $\frac{1}{2} y z=6 \Rightarrow x^{2}=\frac{48}{\sqrt{3}}$ Area of $\Delta \mathrm{ABC}=6+6+2 \mathrm{xy}+\frac{1}{2}(2 \mathrm{x})(2 \mathrm{x}) \sin 60^{\circ}$ $$ =12+2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{x}}{2}+2 \mathrm{x}^{2} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ $$ 12+2 \sqrt{3} \times \frac{48}{\sqrt{3}}=108 $$

questions.q: Let a, b, c be the side-length of a triangle and l, m, n be the lengths of its medians. Put K=l+m+na+b+c Then, as a, b, c vary, K can assume every value in the interval
questions.solution:
Let Δ ABC

BC = aAC = bAB = c

and median of Δ ABC

A D=l
B E=m

 

C F=n

A D is median,

 

∴  AD<AB+BC2

 

∴  l<b+c2

Similarly,   m<a+b2 and

n<a+c2

 

∴  l+m+n<a+b+c

⇒  l+m+na+b+c<1  ... (i)

Also in Δ BGC,BG+GC>BC

 

∴  23(m+n)>a

Similarly, 23(n+l)>b and 23(m+l)>c

 

∵  43(l+m+n)>a+b+c

⇒  l+m+na+b+c>34   ... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

l+m+na+b+c∈34,1

questions.q: Let $X Y$ be the diameter of a semicircle with centre $O$. Let $A$ be a variable point on the semicircle and $B$ another point on the semicircle such that $\mathrm{AB}$ is parallel to $\mathrm{XY}$. The value of $\angle \mathrm{BOY}$ for which the inradius of triangle $A O B$ is maximum, is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{OD}=\mathrm{R} \sin \theta$ $A B=2 R \cos \theta$ $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{O} \mathrm{Ba}}=\frac{\operatorname{ar}(\Delta \mathrm{OAB})}{\text { semi- perimeter }}$ $x_{R}{0} y$ $=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{OD} \times \mathrm{AB}}{\frac{2 \mathrm{R}+\mathrm{AB}}{2}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{R} \sin \theta 2 \mathrm{R} \cos \theta}{\frac{2 \mathrm{R}+2 \mathrm{R} \cos \theta}{2}}$ $r_{O A B}=\frac{R \sin \theta \cos \theta}{(1+\cos \theta)}$ $\frac{d r_{O A B}}{d \theta}=\frac{(1+\cos \theta) \cos 2 \theta-\sin \theta \cos \theta(-\sin \theta)}{(1+\cos \theta)^{2}}=0$ at $\cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}$

Suppose $\mathrm{AD}, \mathrm{BE}$ and $\mathrm{CF}$ concur at $\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{I}, \mathrm{F}$ are concyclic, then $\angle \mathrm{IFD}$ has measure -
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} \angle \mathrm{AIC}=180^{\circ}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C}}{2}\right) \\ =180^{\circ}-\left(\frac{\mathrm{A}+\mathrm{C}}{2}\right) \\ =90+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2} \end{array}$ Here $:-90+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{2}+\mathrm{B}=180^{\circ}$ $\mathrm{B}=60^{\circ}$ This will be case of equilateral $\Delta$ $\Rightarrow \angle \mathrm{IFD}=30^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{ABC}$ be a triangle and $\mathrm{P}$ be a point inside $\mathrm{ABC}$ such that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PA}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PB}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}}=\overrightarrow{0}$. The ratio of the area of triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ to that of $\mathrm{APC}$ is-
questions.solution: $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{PA}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PB}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{PC}}=0$ $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})+2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})+3(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}})=0$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{6}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{\text { Area } \Delta \mathrm{ABC}}{\text { Area } \Delta \mathrm{APC}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|}{\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|} \\ \text { put } \overrightarrow{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}+2 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}+3 \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}}{6} \\ \text { ratio }=3 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi></math> on a semicircle with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> as diameter are such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>D</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math>. Then, the length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> lies in the interval.
questions.solution:
Let the diameter AB=x 

∵ ∠ACB=π2

and ∠ADB=π2

∴ BC=x2-1

On applying Ptolemy theorem, we get 

AB·CD+AC·BC=AD·BC

⇒2x+3=x2-1x2-9

On squaring both sides, we get 

4x2+9+12x=x4-10x2+9

⇒x4-14x2-12x=0

⇒x3-14x-12=0

Let fx=x3-14x-12

∵ f4=-4

f4.1=-0.479

f4.2=3288

∵ f4.1·f4.2<0

⇒x∈[4,1,4.2)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of cubic polynomials P(x) satisfying P(1)=2, P(2)=4, P(3)=6, P(4)=8 is
options: more than one but finitely many
questions.solution:
Let the equation of a cubic polynomial
P(x)=ax3+bx2+cx+d

Now,

P(1)=a+b+c+d=2

P(2)=8a+4b+2c+d=4

P(3)=27a+9b+3c+d=6 P(4)=64a+16b+4c+d=8…

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
7a+3b+c=2
From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get
 19 a+5 b+c=2 
From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get
 37 a+7 b+c=2 
Now, from Eqs. (v) and (vi), we get
12a+2b=0  
and from Eqs. (vi) and (vii), we get
18 a+2 b=0 
From Eqs. (viii) and (ix), we get
a=0 and b=0c=2 and d=0
So, P(x)=2 x
∴ no cubic polynomial is possible.

fa+fb+fc+fd is

⇒fx=x2-xgx+2x2-2x-1

∴ fa=2a2-2a-1

∴ Σfa=fa+fb+c+fd

=2Σa2-2Σa-Σ1

∵ a,b,c,d are roots of equation 

gx=x4-x3-x2-1=0

then Σa=1, Σab=-1

∴ Σfa=212-2-1-21-4

=6-2-4=0

questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>p</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2020</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>p</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2021</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be polynomials having two common roots <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>. questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let
$$
\mathrm{S}=\left\{\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}+\mathrm{b}^{2}+\mathrm{c}^{2}}{\mathrm{ab}+\mathrm{bc}+\mathrm{ca}}: \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c} \in \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{ab}+\mathrm{bc}+\mathrm{ca} \neq 0\right\}
$$
where $R$ is the set of real numbers. Then $S$ equals
[2017]
questions.solution:
Case-I
$\begin{array}{l}
(a-b)^{2}+(b-c)^{2}+(c-a)^{2} \geq 0 \\
a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}-a b-b c-c a \geq 0 \\
a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2} \geq a b+b c+c a \\
\text { If } a b+b c+c a>0
\end{array}$
Then $\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{a b+b c+c a} \geq 1$
Case-II
$\text { Let } a b+b c+c a < 0$
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{(a+b+c)^{2}}{a b+b c+c a} \leq 0 \\
\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+2(a b+b c+c a)}{a b+b c+c a} \leq 0 \\
\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{a b+b c+c a}+2 \leq 0 \\
\frac{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}{a b+b c+c a} \leq-2
\end{array}$
So, Range is $(-\infty,-2] \cup[1, \infty)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $a, b, c, d$ be real numbers between $-5$ and 5 such that $|a|=\sqrt{4-\sqrt{5-a}},|b|=\sqrt{4+\sqrt{5-b}}$,
$$
|c|=\sqrt{4-\sqrt{5+c}},|d|=\sqrt{4+\sqrt{5+d}}
$$
Then the product abcd is
questions.solution:
Given $|\mathrm{a}|=\sqrt{4-\sqrt{5-\mathrm{a}}}$
squaring
$\begin{array}{c}
a^{2}=4-\sqrt{5-a} \\
\Rightarrow a^{4}+16-8 a^{2}=5-a \\
\Rightarrow a^{4}-8 a^{2}+a+11=0
\end{array}$
Sirnilarly squaring other given equations
\& solving we can say that $a, b,-c,-d$ are roots of $x^{4}-8 x^{2}+x+11=0$
$\therefore$ product of roots
$a b(-c)(-d)=11$
abcd $=11$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Number of integers $\mathrm{k}$ for which the equation $\mathrm{x}^{3}-27 \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{k}=0$ has at least two distinct integer roots is -
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=x^{3}-27 x$ $f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{2}-27=3\left(x^{2}-9\right)$
As sum of the roots is zero, so if two roots are integer then $3^{\text {rd }}$ root has to be integer Now put $\mathrm{x}=6 \mathrm{t}$ $216 t^{3}-27 \times 6 t+k=0$ $54\left(4 t^{3}-3 t\right)+k=0$ Put $\mathrm{t}=\cos \theta$ $54 \cos 3 \theta=-\mathrm{k}$ Now for $3 \theta=0,2 \pi$ we get integral solution So two values of 'k'

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The polynomial equation $x^{3}-3 a x^{2}+\left(27 a^{2}+9\right) x+2016=0$ has -
options: exactly one real root for any real a
three real roots for any real a
three real roots for any $a \geq 0$, and exactly one real root for any a $ < 0$
three real roots for any a $\leq 0$, and exactly one real root for any a $>0$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{f}^{\prime}(\mathrm{x}) &=3 \mathrm{x}^{2}-6 \mathrm{ax}+27 \mathrm{a}^{2}+9 \\
&=3\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}-2 \mathrm{ax}+9 \mathrm{a}^{2}+3\right]=3\left((\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{a})^{2}+8 \mathrm{a}^{2}+3\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore f^{\prime}(x)$ is $+$ ve for $x \in R$ so $f(x)$ is monotonic $\uparrow$ for $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Suppose the quadratic polynomial $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x})=a x^{2}+\mathrm{b} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}$ has positive coefficients $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ in arithmetic progression in that order. If $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x})=0$ has integer roots $\alpha$ and $\beta$, then $\alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta$ equals
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{2}+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{x}-\alpha)(\mathrm{x}-\beta) \\
\text { and } \alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta+1-1=(\alpha+1)(\beta+1)-1 \\
=\frac{(\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c})}{\mathrm{a}}-1 \\
\Rightarrow \alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}-1=\lambda_{1}-1
\end{array}$
i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}}$ is integer $=\lambda_{1}$
If $\mathrm{b}=\mathrm{a} \lambda_{1}$
then, $\mathrm{c}=\mathrm{a}\left(2 \lambda_{1}-1\right)$ \{because $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ are in A.P.\}
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x}) &=\mathrm{ax}^{2}+\mathrm{a} \lambda_{1} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a}\left(2 \lambda_{1}-1\right) \\
&=\mathrm{a}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}+\lambda_{1} \mathrm{x}+\left(2 \lambda_{1}-1\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{D}=\lambda_{1}^{2}-4\left(2 \lambda_{1}-1\right)$ is perfect square for integral roots $\Rightarrow \mathrm{D}=\lambda_{1}{ }^{2}-8 \lambda_{1}+4$ is perfect square
Let $\mathrm{D}=\left(\lambda_{1}-4\right)^{2}-12=\mathrm{k}^{2}\{$ where $\mathrm{k} \in \mathrm{I}\}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\lambda_{1}-4-\mathrm{k}\right)\left(\lambda_{1}-4+\mathrm{k}\right)=12$
This gives $\lambda_{1}-4-\mathrm{k}=2$
$\begin{array}{c}
\Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{1}-4=4 & \& k=1 \\
\Rightarrow \alpha_{1}=8 \\
\therefore \alpha+\beta+\alpha \beta=8-1=7
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider the equation $(1+a+b)^{2}=3\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right)$, where $a, b$ are real numbers. Then
options: there is no solution pair (a, b)
there are infinitely many solution pairs (a, b)
there are exactly two solution pairs (a, b)
there is exactly one solution pair ( $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b})$
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
(1+a+b)^{2}=3\left(1+a^{2}+b^{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow  1 \cdot a+a \cdot b+1 \cdot b=1^{2}+a^{2}+b^{2} \\
\Rightarrow  1=a=b
\end{array}$
exactly one pair.

questions.q: Suppose a is a positive real number such that $a^{5}-a^{3}+a=2 .$ Then
questions.solution:
$a^{5}-a^{3}+a=2 ; a \in R^{+}$
Let $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})=\mathrm{a}^{5}-\mathrm{a}^{3}+\mathrm{a}-2 ;$ Note $\left.\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{a})>0 \forall \mathrm{a} \in \mathrm{R}\right\}$ 
for $a^{6}=3 \Rightarrow a=3^{1 / 6}=1.2$ \{use calculator $\}$
we get $\mathrm{f}(1.2) < 0$ and at $\mathrm{a}=4^{1 / 6} \Rightarrow \mathrm{f}\left(4^{1 / 6}\right)>0$ 
so one root in $\mathrm{a} \in(3,4)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Consider the quadratic equation $n x^{2}+7 \sqrt{n} x+n=0$, where $n$ is a positive integer. Which of the following statements are necessarily correct?
I. For any $\mathrm{n}$, the roots are distinct.
II. There are infinitely many values of $\mathrm{n}$ for which both roots are real.
III. The product of the roots is necessarily an integer.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{D} &=49 \mathrm{n}-4 \mathrm{n}^{2} \\
&=\mathrm{n}(49-4 \mathrm{n})
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{D} \neq 0$ for any $\mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}^{+}$. So roots are distinct For roots to be real $\mathrm{D} \geq 0$
So $\mathrm{n} \leq \frac{49}{4}$
So n can be $\{1,2,3, \ldots \ldots 12\}$
Clearly product of the roots is 1

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> be a non-zero polynomial such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for all real <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> be the largest integer such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>m</mi></msup></math> divides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> for all such <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math>. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> equals
questions.solution:
It is given that P is a non-zero polynomial such that, P1+x=P1-x

On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get 

P'1+x=-P'1-x

Now on putting x=0, we get

P'1=-P'1

⇒ P'1=0

i.e the polynomial P touches the X-axis at x=1 and P1=0 (given)

∴ Px=x-12Qx

∴ Largest integral value of 'm' such that x-1m divides Px is 2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let px=x2+ax+b have two distinct real roots, where a,b are real number. Define gx=px3 for all real number x

Then, which of the following statements are true?
I. g has exactly two distinct real roots.
II. g can have more than two distinct real roots.
III. There exists a real number α such that gx≥α for all real x
questions.solution:
Let the given quadratic polynomial px=x2+ax+b has two distinct real roots α and β, then px=x2+ax+b=x-αx-β
and since gx=px3=x3-αx3-β

let α=α13 and β=β13
then gx=x3-α13x3-β13

=x-α1x-β1x2+α1x+α12x2+β1x+β12

∵  the discriminants of quadratic equations x2+α1x+α12 and x2+β1x+β12 are
negative.

∴ gx has exactly two distinct real roots and since gx=x6+ax3+b is an even degree polynomial, so there exists a real number α ' such that gx≥α for all real x

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The number of ordered pairs $(x, y)$ of real numbers that satisfy the simultaneous equations.
$$
x+y^{2}=x^{2}+y=12
$$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y}=12 \\
\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}^{2}=\mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{y} \\
x-y=x^{2}-y^{2} \Rightarrow x=y, \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=1 \\
\text { when } \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y} \quad \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}=12 \\
\quad \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{x}-12=0 \\
\quad \mathrm{x}^{2}+4 \mathrm{x}-3 \mathrm{x}-12=0 \\
\quad(\mathrm{x}+4)(\mathrm{x}-3)=0 \\
\quad \mathrm{x}=-4,3 \\
\quad(3,3)(-4,-4) \\
\text { When } \mathrm{y}=1-\mathrm{x} \\
\quad \mathrm{x}+(1-\mathrm{x})^{2}=12 \\
\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}-11=0 \\
\quad x=\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1+44}}{2}
\end{array}
$$
So four pair.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>d</mi></math> be distinct real numbers such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> are roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>d</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>d</mi></math> are roots of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>a</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>d</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
It is given that the quadratic equation x2-5cx-6d=0 has roots a and b, then
a+b=5c and ab=-6d
and, the quadratic equation x2-5ax-6b=0 has roots C and d, then
c+d=5 a
and c d=-6 b
Now, from Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have

Now, from Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have
(a+b)−(c+d)=5c−5a

⇒(a−c)+(b−d)=−5(a−c)

⇒(b−d)=6(c−a)
∴a and c are the roots of equations. x2-5cx-6d=00$ and x2-5ax-6b=0
respectively.

∴a2−5ac−6d=0 

 and c2−5ac−6b=0

⇒a2−c2−6(d−b)=0

⇒a+c=6(d−b)a−c=36  From Eqs. (i) and (iii), we have

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x})$ be a polynomial such that $p(x)-p^{\prime}(x)=x^{n}$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is a positive integer. Then $p(0)$ equals.
questions.solution:
Let $\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{a}_{0} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-2} \ldots \ldots . \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P^{1}(x)=\sum_{r=0}^{n-1} a_{r}(n-r) x^{n-r-1} \\
P(x)-P^{1}(x)=a_{0} x^{n}+\sum_{r=1}^{n}\left\{a_{r}-a_{r+}(n-r+1)\right\} x^{n-r}=x^{n}
\end{array}
$$
So, $a_{0}=1$ and $a_{r}=a_{r-1}(n-r+1)$
$$
\frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}}}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{r}-1}}=\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}+1
$$
Now, $P(0)=a_{n}=\frac{a_{n}}{a_{n-1}} \cdot \frac{a_{n-1}}{a_{n-2}} \ldots \ldots \frac{a_{2}}{a_{1}} \frac{a_{1}}{a_{0}} \cdot a_{0}$ $=(1 \times 2 \ldots . ., \mathrm{n})=\mathrm{n} !$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $p(x)$ is a cubic polynomial with $p(1)=3, p(0)=2$ and $p(-1)=4$, then $\int_{-1}^{1} p(x) d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{p}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{3}+\mathrm{b} \mathrm{x}^{2}+\mathrm{cx}+\mathrm{d}$
$\mathrm{p}(0)=2$
$\mathrm{~d}=2$
$\mathrm{p}(1)=3$
$\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}+2=3$
$\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=1$
$\mathrm{p}(-1)=4$
$-a+b-c+2=4$
$-a+b-c=2$
$2 b=3 \Rightarrow b=\frac{3}{2}$
$P(x)=a x^{3}+\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+c x+2$
$\int_{-1}^{1} p(x) d x=\int_{-1}^{1}\left(a x^{2}+\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+c x+2\right) d x=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
options: $x$ is a rational number, but not an integer $
questions.solution:
$(\sqrt{50}+7)^{\frac{1}{2}}=\mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}$
$\mathrm{x}^{3}=\mathrm{t}^{3}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}^{3}}-3\left(\mathrm{t}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}}\right)$
$\mathrm{x}^{3}+3 \mathrm{x}=\sqrt{50}+7-(\sqrt{50}-7)$
$\mathrm{x}^{3}+3 \mathrm{x}=14$
$\mathrm{x}^{3}+3 \mathrm{x}-14=0$
$(\mathrm{x}-2)\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+2 \mathrm{x}+7\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sum of non-real roots of the polynomial equation $x^{3}+3 x^{2}+3 x+3=0$.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
f(x)=x^{3}+3 x^{2}+3 x+3=0 \\
f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{2}+6 x+3=3(x+1)^{2} \geq 0 \\
f(x) \text { is lncreasing function } \\
\text { Now } \begin{array}{l}
f(-3)=-6 < 0 \\
f(-2)=1>0
\end{array}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ real root $\gamma$ lies between $-3$ and $-2$
$$
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=-3 ; \quad-3 < \gamma < -2
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\alpha+\beta-3 < \alpha+\beta+\gamma < \alpha+\beta-2 \\
\alpha+\beta-3 < -3 < \alpha+\beta-2 \\
-1 < \alpha+\beta < 0
\end{array}
$$

$f(x)$ has at least two real roots
At least one of the coefficients of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is bigger than 50.

Coefficient of highest degree term must be negative becuase ifit is positive, then $\mathrm{x} \rightarrow \infty, \mathrm{y} \rightarrow \infty$ and it is not possible, since $f(x) \leq 100$. Now, graph will be like
at least two real roots will be there, \& if $x \neq \frac{1}{2}$, then $f(x) < 100$, it is not always true, as the graph can be like. this also
Now, let the highest coefficients, it can have is 49 then, $f\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=49+\frac{49}{2}+\frac{49}{2^{2}}+\ldots$ But the sum cannot be equal to 100 .

options: One positive and one negative root

$\begin{array}{ll}D_{1}>0 & D_{2}>0 \\ 4 a^{2}+b^{2}>0 & 4 b^{2}-4 c^{2}>0 \\ a^{2}>b^{2} \ldots \ldots \ldots .(1) & b^{2}>c^{2} \ldots \ldots \ldots . .(2)\end{array}$
From $(1)$ and $(2)$
$a^{2}>b^{2}>c^{2} \Rightarrow a^{2}>c^{2} \Rightarrow c^{2}-a^{2} < 0$
$x^{2}+2 c x+a^{2}=0$
$D=4 c^{2}-4 a^{2} < 0 \quad$ No real roots

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Two workers A and B are engaged to do a piece of work. Working alone, A takes 8 hours more to complete the work than if both worked together. On the other hand, working alone, B would need $4 \frac{1}{2}$ hours more to complete the work than if both worked together. How much time would they take to complete the job working together?
questions.solution:
If A \& B work together take time t hr. If A work alone takes $t+8 \mathrm{hr}$. to complete the work Work $/ \mathrm{hr}$ by $\mathrm{A}$ is $\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}+8}$. Similarly for B work/hr is $\frac{1}{\mathrm{t}+9 / 2}$
When A \& B work together there work/hr $\frac{1}{t+8}+\frac{1}{t+9 / 2}$
Work will be done in t hr.
$\mathrm{t}\left(\frac{1}{t+8}+\frac{1}{t+9 / 2}\right)=1$
By solving
$\mathrm{t}=6$

questions.q: Consider the cubic equation $x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c=0$, where $a, b, c$ are real numbers. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: If $a^{2}-2 b < 0$, then the equation has one real and two imaginary roots
If $a^{2}-2 b \geq 0$, then the equation has all real roots
If $a^{2}-2 b>0$, then the equation has all real and distinct roots
If $4 a^{3}-27 b^{2}>0$, then the equation has real and distinct roots
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l} f(x)=x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c \\ f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^{2}+2 a x+b \\ D=4 a^{2}-4.3 \quad b=4\left(a^{2}-3 b\right) \end{array}$ If $a^{2} < 2 b \Rightarrow a^{2} < 3 b \Rightarrow D < 0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=0$ has non real roots
Hence $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=0$ has 1 real and two imaginary roots

$\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is always positive

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$, where $a, b, c$ are integers. Suppose $f(1)=0,40 < f(6) < 50,60 < f(7) < 70$, and $1000 \mathrm{t} < \mathrm{f}(50) < 1000(\mathrm{t}+1)$ for some integer $\mathrm{t}$. Then the value of $\mathrm{t}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{ax}^{2}+\mathrm{bx}+\mathrm{c} \\
\text { given } \mathrm{f}(1)=0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}=0 \\
\text { and } 40 < \mathrm{f}(6) < 50 \\
\Rightarrow 40 < 36 \mathrm{a}+6 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c} < 50 \\
\Rightarrow 40 < 35 \mathrm{a}+5 \mathrm{~b} < 50 \\
\Rightarrow 8 < 7 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} < 10 \\
7 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=\text { integer }=9 \\
\text { and } 60 < \mathrm{f}(7) < 70 \\
\Rightarrow 60 < 49 \mathrm{a}+7 \mathrm{~b}+\mathrm{c} < 70 \\
\Rightarrow 60 < 48 \mathrm{a}+6 \mathrm{~b} < 70 \\
\Rightarrow 10 < 8 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} < 11.6 \\
8 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=\text { integer }=11
\end{array}$
Solving $(1) \&(2)$
$\begin{array}{l}
a=2, b=-5, c=3 \\
\therefore \quad f(x)=2 x^{2}-5 x+3 \\
\quad f(50)=4753 \\
1000 t < f(50) < 1000(t+1) \\
(1000 \times 4) < 4753 < 1000(4+1) \\
\therefore \quad t=4
\end{array}$

questions.q: Let $a_{i}=i+\frac{1}{i}$ for $i=1,2, \ldots \ldots ., 20 .$ Put $\mathrm{p}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\mathrm{a}_{1}+\mathrm{a}_{2}+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{a}_{20}\right)$ and $\mathrm{q}=\frac{1}{20}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}_{2}}+\ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{\mathrm{a}_{20}}\right) .$ Then
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{q}>0$, try to the contra prove that $\mathrm{q} < \frac{22-\mathrm{p}}{21}$
$\mathrm{q}+\frac{\mathrm{p}}{21} < \frac{22}{21}$
$\mathrm{q}+\frac{\mathrm{p}}{21}=\frac{1}{20}\left[\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20} \frac{1}{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}}+\frac{1}{21} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}\right]$
$=\frac{1}{20}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{20}\left(\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}^{2}+1}\right)+\frac{1}{21} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20}\left(\mathrm{i}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{i}}\right)\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20}\left[\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}^{2}+1}+\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20}\left(\frac{1}{21 \mathrm{i}}\right)\right]$
$=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\sum_{\mathrm{i}=2}^{20} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}^{2}+1}+\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20} \frac{1}{21 \mathrm{i}}\right]$
$\quad < \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{5} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=2}^{20} 1+\frac{1}{21} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{20} 1\right]$
$ < \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20}\left[\frac{1}{2}+\frac{2}{5} \times 19+\frac{1}{21} \times 20\right]$
$ < \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{20}(1+8+1)$
$ < \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}$
$ < \frac{22}{21}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $n \geq 4$ be a positive integer and let $\ell_{1}, \ell_{2}, \ldots . ., \ell_{\mathrm{n}}$ be the lengths of the sides of arbitrary $n$ - sided non-degenerate polygon P. Suppose
$$
\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}}+\frac{\ell_{2}}{\ell_{3}}+\ldots \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{n}-1}}{\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}+\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}{\ell_{1}}=\mathrm{n}
$$
Consider the following statements:
I. The lengths of the sides of $P$ are equal.
II. The angles of $P$ are equal.
III. $\mathrm{P}$ is a regular polygon if it is cyclic.
Then
options: $I$ is true and I implies II
questions.solution:
given : $\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}}+\frac{\ell_{2}}{\ell_{3}} \ldots \ldots .+\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}{\ell_{1}}=\mathrm{n}$ .........(i)
$\therefore$ Use A.M $\geq$ G.M
We get
$\frac{\left(\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}}+\frac{\ell_{2}}{\ell_{3}} \ldots \ldots+\frac{\ell_{n}}{\ell_{1}}\right)}{\mathrm{n}} \geq \sqrt[n]{\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}} \cdot \frac{\ell_{2}}{\ell_{3}} \ldots \cdots \frac{\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}{\ell_{1}}}$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}} \geq 1$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}$ So $\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{G} \mathrm{M}$
Hence $\frac{\ell_{1}}{\ell_{2}}=\frac{\ell_{2}}{\ell_{3}} \ldots \ldots \ldots .=\frac{\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}{\ell_{1}}=\mathrm{k}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{\ell_{1}+\ell_{2} \ldots \ldots+\ell_{\mathrm{n}}}{\ell_{2}+\ell_{3} \ldots \ldots+\ell_{\mathrm{n}}+\ell_{1}}=1 \\
\Rightarrow \ell_{1}=\ell_{2} \ldots \ldots=\ell_{\mathrm{n}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of triples $(x, y, z)$ of real numbers satisfying the equation $x^{4}+y^{4}+z^{4}+1=4 x y z$ is
questions.solution:
$(x, y, z)$ are real & $x^{4}, y^{4}, z^{4}$ are positive real numbers
$$
\begin{array}{l}

questions.q: The sum of all positive integers $n$ for which $\frac{1^{3}+2^{3}+\ldots . .+(2 n)^{3}}{1^{2}+2^{2}+\ldots . .+n^{2}}$ is also an integer is.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1^{3}+2^{3}+\ldots . .+(2 n)^{3}}{1^{2}+2^{2}+\ldots \ldots . .+n^{2}} &=\left(\frac{2 n(2 n+1)}{2}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{6}{n(n+1)(2 n+1)} \\
&=\frac{6 n(2 n+1)}{n+1} \\
&=\frac{12 n^{2}+6 n}{n+1}=\frac{12\left(n^{2}-1\right)+6(n+1)+6}{n+1} \\
&=1+\frac{6}{n+1}
\end{aligned}$
If the given terms is an intiger, then $\frac{6}{n+1}$ must be an integer
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=1,2,5$
$\text { Sum }=8$

Let $\mathrm{S}$ be the infinite sum given by $\mathrm{S}=\sum_{\mathrm{n}=0}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}}{10^{2 \mathrm{n}}}$
where $\left\{a_{n}\right\}_{n \geq 0}$ is a sequence defined by $a_{0}=a_{1}=1$ and $a_{j}=20 a_{j-1}-108 a_{j-2}$ for $j \geq 2$.
If $S$ is expressed in the form $\frac{a}{b}$, where $a, b$ are coprime positive integers, then a equals
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
a_{n}=20 a_{n-1}-108 a_{n-2} \\
\frac{a_{n}}{10^{2 n}}=\frac{20 a_{n-1}}{10^{2 n}}-\frac{108 a_{n-2}}{10^{2 n}} \\
\frac{a_{n}}{10^{2 n}}=\frac{20}{100} \frac{a_{n-1}}{10^{2(n-1)}}-\frac{108}{10000} \frac{a_{n-2}}{10^{2(n-2)}}
\end{array}$
apply summation
$\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{\mathrm{n}=2}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}}{10^{2 \mathrm{n}}}=\frac{1}{5} \sum_{\mathrm{n}=2}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-1}}{10^{2(\mathrm{n}-1)}}-\frac{27}{2500} \sum_{\mathrm{n}=2}^{\infty} \frac{\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-2}}{10^{2(\mathrm{n}-2)}} \\
\mathrm{S}-1-\frac{1}{100}=\frac{1}{5}(\mathrm{~S}-1)-\frac{27}{2500} \mathrm{~S} \\
\mathrm{~S}-1-\frac{1}{100}=\frac{1}{5} \mathrm{~S}-\frac{1}{5}-\frac{27}{2500} \mathrm{~S} \\
\mathrm{~S}\left(1-\frac{1}{5}+\frac{27}{2500}\right)=-\frac{1}{5}+1+\frac{1}{100}
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{~S}\left(\frac{2500-500+27}{2500}\right)=\frac{81}{100}$
$\mathrm{~S}=\frac{81 \times 25}{2027}$
$\mathrm{~S}=\frac{2025}{2027}$

questions.q: The number of ordered pairs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> of positive integers such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mi>a</mi></mfrac></math> are both integers is
questions.solution:
For positive integers 'a' and 'b' the numbers 2a-1b and 2b-1a are integers if 'a' and 'b' and odd integers because '2a-1' and '2b-1' are odd integers. 

Now, let 2a-1b=α and 2b-1a=β, where α,β are integers.

then 2a-1=αb and 2b-1=βa

so 4a-2=αβa+1

⇒ a=α+24-αβ

∵ a is an integer, then 0<αβ<4

∴ Possible value of α=1, 2 or 3 and β=1, 2 or 3

such that 0<αβ<4

Now, when α,β=1,1, then a,b=1,1

When α,β=1,2, then a,b have no value

When (α,β)=(1,3), then a,b=3,5 and similarly when α,β=3,1, then a,b=5,3 

So, number of ordered pairs a,b is 3.

It is given that for n∈N

Similarly,

13n+22/3<fn+1<13n+12/3

∴  fn+1<13n+12/3<fn,∀n∈N

So, set A=N

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For each positive real number $\lambda$, let $A_{\lambda}$ be the set of all natural numbers $n$ such that $\mid \sin (\sqrt{n+1}-\sin (\sqrt{n}) \mid < \lambda$ Let $A_{\lambda}^{\mathrm{c}}$ be the complement of $\mathrm{A}_{\lambda}$ in the set of all natural numbers. Then -
options: $A_{\frac{1}{3}}$ is a finite set but $A_{\frac{1}{2}}, A_{\frac{2}{5}}$ are infinite sets
$A_{\frac{1}{3}}, A_{\frac{2}{5}}$ are finite sets and $A_{\frac{1}{2}}$ is an infinite set
questions.solution:
As $\mathrm{n} \rightarrow \infty$
$$
|\sin \sqrt{x+1}-\sin \sqrt{x}| \rightarrow 0
$$
$\therefore$ There exist infinite natural numbers for which $|\sin \sqrt{x+1}-\sin \sqrt{x}| < \lambda \forall \lambda>0$
Hence $A_{\frac{1}{2}}, A_{\frac{1}{3}}, A_{\frac{2}{5}}$ are all infinite sets

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $A_{1}, A_{2} \ldots, A_{m}$ be non-empty subsets of $\{1,2,3, \ldots \ldots, 100\}$ satisfying the following conditions:
(1) the numbers $\left|\mathrm{A}_{1}\right|,\left|\mathrm{A}_{2}\right|, \ldots,\left|\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}\right|$ are distinct;
(2) $\mathrm{A}_{1}, \mathrm{~A}_{2}, \ldots, \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{m}}$ are pairwise disjoint.
(Here $\mid \mathrm{A}$ denotes the number of elements in the set $\mathrm{A}$.) Then the maximum possible value of $\mathrm{m}$ is
questions.solution:
The possibility is
$\begin{array}{l}
\left|\mathrm{A}_{1}\right|=1 ;\left|\mathrm{A}_{2}\right|=2 ;\left|\mathrm{A}_{3}\right|=3 ; \ldots \ldots .,\left|\mathrm{A}_{m}\right|=\mathrm{m} \\
1+2+3+\ldots \ldots+\mathrm{m} \leq 100 \quad\{\text { Because all are disjoint }\} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{m}+1)}{2} \leq 100 \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{m} < 14
\end{array}$
$14^{\text {th }}$ set will have the same size as that of one of the previous sets 
So, $\mathrm{m}=13$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For each positive integer n, let $A_{n}=\max \left\{\left(\begin{array}{l}n \\ r\end{array}\right) \mid 0 \leq r \leq n\right\}$. Then the number of elements $n$ is $\{1,2,3, \ldots \ldots . .20\}$ for which $1.9 \leq \frac{A_{n}}{A_{n-1}} \leq 2$ is :
questions.solution:
$\quad A_{n}=\left({ }^{n} C_{r}\right) \max$
case-I $n=2 k($ even $) \Rightarrow A_{n}={ }^{2 k} C_{k}$
$\Rightarrow n-1=2 k-1$ (odd) $\Rightarrow A_{n-1}={ }^{2 k-1} C_{k-1}$ or ${ }^{2 k-1} C_{k}$
$\Rightarrow 1.9 \leq \frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}-1}} \leq 2$
$\Rightarrow 1.9 \leq \frac{{ }^{2 \mathrm{k}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{k}}}{{ }^{2 \mathrm{k}-1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{k}-1}} \leq 2$
$\Rightarrow 1.9 \leq 2 \leq 2$ which is true for
every $n=$ even
$n=2,4,6, \ldots \ldots \ldots . .20 \Rightarrow 10$ numbers
case-II $n=(2 k-1)$ (odd)
$A_{n}={ }^{2 k-1} C_{k}$ or $^{2 k-1} C_{k-1}$
$\Rightarrow A_{n-1}={ }^{n}{k}-2 C_{k-1}$
$\because 1.9 \leq \frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}-1}} \leq 2$
$1.9 \leq \frac{{ }^{2 \mathrm{k}-1} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{k}}}{{ }^{2 \mathrm{k}-2} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{k}-1}} \leq 2.0$
$1.9 \leq \frac{2 k-1}{k} \leq 2$
$1.9 \mathrm{k} \leq 2 \mathrm{k}-1 \leq 2 \mathrm{k}$
$10 \leq \mathrm{k}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n} \geq 19$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow n &=19 \text { only } \\ \text { total } &=11 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a cinema hall, the charge per person is Rs. 200 . On the first day, only $60 \%$ of the seats were filled. The owner decided to reduce the price by $20 \%$ and there was in increase of $50 \%$ in the number of spectators on the next day. The percentage increase in the revenue on the second day was
questions.solution:
Let total seats $=100$
on first day,
Ticket price $=200$
$=\frac{60}{100} \times 100=60$
$\therefore$ Revenue $=60 \times 200$
$\mathrm{R}_{1}=12000$
On second day
Tricked price $=200-20 \%$ of 200
$=200-\frac{20}{100} \times 200$
$=200-40=160$
Scents full $60+50 \%$ of 60
$=60+\frac{50}{100} \times 60$
$\begin{array}{l}
=\begin{aligned}
60+30=90 \\
\text { Revenue }=160 \times 90 \\
\mathrm{R}_{2}=14400 \\
\% & \text { Increase is Revenue }=\frac{R_{2}-R_{1}}{R_{1}} \times 100 \\
&=\frac{14400-12000}{12000} \times 100 \\
&=\frac{2400}{1200} \times 100 \\
=& 20 \%
\end{aligned}
\end{array}$

questions.q: The sum of $\left(1^{2}-1+1\right)(1 !)+\left(2^{2}-2+1\right)(2 !)+\ldots .+\left(n^{2}-n+1\right)(n !)$ is -
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=\left(\mathrm{n}^{2}-\mathrm{n}+1\right) \mathrm{n} !$
$\quad=\left(\mathrm{n}^{2}-1\right) \mathrm{n} !-(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{n} !$
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{n}}=(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}+1) !-(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{n} !$
$\mathrm{Sum}=1+(\mathrm{n}-1)(\mathrm{n}+1) !$

x6+x62=m( Let )

∵  m<x6 as x5<x5+x62<x6

questions.q: In a city, the total income of all people with salary below $Rs. 10000$ per annum is less than the total income of all people with salary above $Rs. 10000$ per annum. If the salaries of people in the first group increases by $5 \%$ and the salaries of people in the second group decreases by $5 \%$ then the average income of all people
options: cannot be determined from the data
questions.solution:
Let total number of people with salary below $\mathrm{Rs. 10000}$ per annum is $x$ and salary is $A$. Let total number of people with salary above $\mathrm{Rs. 10000}$ per annum is $y$ and salary is $B$ then.
$\mathrm{xA}-\mathrm{yB} < 0$
$\frac{\text { average after }}{\text { average before }}=\frac{x\left(\frac{105}{100} A\right)+y\left(\frac{95}{100} B\right)}{x A+y B}$
$=1+\frac{5}{100}\left(\frac{x A-y B}{x A+y B}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $n \geq 3$. A list of numbers $0 < x_{1} < x_{2} < \ldots < x_{n}$ has mean $\mu$ and standard deviatiion $\sigma$. A new list of numbers is made as follows : $y_{1}=0, y_{2}=x_{2}, \ldots, y_{n-1}=x_{n-1}, y_{n}=x_{1}+x_{n}$. The mean and the standard deviation of the new list are $\hat{\mu}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$. Which of the following is necessarily true?
options: $\mu$ may or may not be equal to $\hat{\mu}$
questions.solution:
$\hat{\mu}=\frac{0+x_{2}+x_{3} \ldots \ldots x_{n-1}+\left(x_{1}+x_{n}\right)}{n}=\mu$
$\hat{\sigma}^{2}=\frac{1}{n} \Sigma y_{i}^{2}-\hat{\mu}^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{n}\left[0^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+x_{3}^{2} \ldots\left(x_{n-1}\right)^{2}+\left(x_{1}+x_{n}\right)^{2}\right]-\mu^{2}$
$\hat{\sigma}^{2}-\sigma^{2}=\frac{2 x_{1} x_{n}}{n}>0$
so $\hat{\sigma}>\sigma$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $n \geq 3$.A list of numbers $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots x_{n}$ has mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma .$ A new list of numbers $y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots \ldots \ldots . y_{n}$ is made as follows: $y_{1}=\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}, y 2=\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}$ and $y_{j}=x$ for $j=3,4, \ldots \ldots . n$. The mean and the standard deviation of the list are $\hat{\mu}$ and $\hat{\sigma}$. Then which of the following is necessarily true?
questions.solution:
$\mu=\frac{\sum x_{i}}{n} \quad n \geq 3$
$\sigma=\sqrt{\frac{\sum x_{i}^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum x_{i}}{n}\right)^{2}}$
$\hat{\mu}=\frac{y_{1}+y_{2}+y_{3}+\ldots \ldots+y_{n}}{n}$
$=\frac{\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}+\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}+x_{3}+x_{4}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+x_{n}}{n}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}+\ldots \ldots . .+\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{n}}=\mu$
$\hat{\sigma}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}}{2}\right)^{2}+x_{3}^{2}+\ldots \ldots+x_{n}^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum y_{1}}{n}\right)^{2}}$
$\begin{array}{l}=\sqrt{\frac{\frac{1}{4}\left(2 x_{1}^{2}+2 x_{2}^{2}+4 x_{1} x_{2}\right)+x_{3}^{2}+\ldots \ldots . .+x_{n}^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum y_{1}}{n}\right)^{2}} \\ =\sqrt{\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}\right)+x_{1} x_{2}+x_{3}^{2}+\ldots \ldots+x_{n}^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum x_{i}}{n}\right)^{2}} \\ \leq \sqrt{\frac{x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\ldots \ldots+x_{2}^{2}}{n}-\left(\frac{\sum x_{i}}{n}\right)^{2}} \\ \hat{\sigma} \leq \sigma & \ldots \ldots \ldots(2) \\ \text { by }(1) \text { and }(2)\end{array}$

questions.q: Consider the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> of points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>n</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math> are integers. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> be the set of all lines passing through at least two distinct points from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Suppose we choose a line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> at random from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>S</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> be the probability that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mi>n</mi></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> Then, the limit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>∞</mo></mrow></munder><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> is
questions.solution:
Equation of line passing through two points x1,y1 and x2,y2 is

y-y1=y2-y1x2-x1x-x1

⇒x2-x1y+y1-y2x+y1x1-x2+x1y2-y1=0

⇒ax+by+c=0, where a,b,c∈I

a=y1-y2,b=x2-x1

c∈yx1-x2+x1y2-y1

Perpendicular distance from origin to line

ax+by+c=0 is ca2+b2 and c2a2+b2 is rational.

Case I n is not perfect square and square f radius =n211-1n2= rational

⇒r2≠c2a2+b2

⇒ax+by+c=0 never be tangent to give n circle

⇒limn→∞Pn=0

Case II If n is perfect square. In this case number of tangents passing through two points from given set are few, but total number of lines are in much quantity when n approaches to infinite.
⇒limn→∞Pn=0

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
In a circle with centre $\mathrm{O}$, suppose A, P, B are three points on its circumference such that $\mathrm{P}$ is the mid-point of minor arc $\mathrm{AB}$. Suppose when $\angle \mathrm{AOB}=\theta$,
$\frac{\operatorname{area}(\Delta \mathrm{AOB})}{\text { area }(\Delta \mathrm{APB})}=\sqrt{5}+2$
If $\angle \mathrm{AOB}$ is doubled to $2 \theta$, then the ratio $\frac{\text { area }(\Delta \mathrm{AOB})}{\text { area }(\triangle \mathrm{APB})}$ is -
questions.solution: $\frac{\Delta(\mathrm{AOB})}{\Delta \mathrm{APB}}=2+\sqrt{5}$ $\frac{\cos \frac{\theta}{2}}{1-\cos \frac{\theta}{2}}=2+\sqrt{5} \quad \Rightarrow \cos \frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{4}$ So $\cos \theta=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{4}$ If $\theta \rightarrow 2 \theta$ $\frac{\Delta \mathrm{AOB}}{\Delta \mathrm{APB}}=\frac{\cos \theta}{1-\cos \theta}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$

questions.solution: $$ \mathrm{PQ} \perp \mathrm{RS} \Rightarrow $$ $$ c-a=b-d $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} P Q=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4} \\ P Q^{2}=\frac{27}{16} \\ 1+(a-c)^{2}=\frac{27}{16} \\ R S=\sqrt{(b-d)^{2}+1} \end{array} $$ By equation $(1),(2)$ and (3) $$ R S=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4} $$

questions.q:
In the figure given below, a rectangle of perimeter 76 units is divided into 7 congruent rectangles.
What is the perimeter of each of the smaller rectangles?
questions.solution:
Let Sides of Rectangle are $x$ \& y Then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
5 x+6 y=76 \\
4 y=3 x
\end{array}
$$
After solving we get $y=6, x=8$ Perimeter $=2(x+y)=28$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathrm{BC}$ be a fixed line segment in the plane. The locus of a point $A$ such that the triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ is isosceles, is (with finitely many possible exceptional points)
options: the union of a circle and a line
the union of two circles and a line
questions.solution:
Case (i):
If $\angle \mathrm{B}=\angle \mathrm{C}$ locus of $\mathrm{A}$ is $\perp$ bisector of $\mathrm{BC}$ So it is straight line Case (ii):
If $\angle \mathrm{A}=\angle \mathrm{C}$ BC fixed $\mathrm{B}(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{C}(0, \mathrm{a})$ $\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{AB}$ So, $(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}=2 a^{2}$ Circle Case (iii): $\begin{array}{l} \angle \mathrm{A}=\angle \mathrm{B} \\ \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC} \\ \sqrt{\mathrm{h}^{2}+(\mathrm{k}-\mathrm{a})^{2}}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{a}^{2}} \\ \mathrm{x}^{2}+(\mathrm{y}-\mathrm{a})^{2}=2 \mathrm{a}^{2} \end{array}$ also a circle So union of two circle and a line.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Lets $S=\{(a, b)\} \mid a, b \in Z, 0 \leq a, b, \leq 18\}$. The number of lines in $R^{2}$ passing through $(0,0)$ and exactly one other point in $S$ is
questions.solution:
Line passing through origin will be of the form $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{mx}$ and any general point on that line can be given by $(\mathrm{h}, \mathrm{mh})$.
Now if we take the point as $(a, b)$ with restrictions that a and b both are integers between o and 18 , both included, then there will be $18 \times 18=324$ such integral pairs in the complete $18 \times 18$ square.
We need to select those pairs, which do not have any factor i.e coprime pairs in the given range, such as $(1,10),(1,11)$, etc. Number of lines will be equal to number of such points in the plane.
Number of such pairs on $\mathrm{R}^{2}$ is equal to 16 . Therefore, there will be 16 such lines

using $A M \geq G \cdot M$
$\frac{a+b}{2} \geq \sqrt{a b} \Rightarrow a+b \geq 12$

questions.q: Consider a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ in the $\mathrm{xy}$-plane with vertices $\mathrm{A}=(0,0), \mathrm{B}=(1,1)$ and $\mathrm{C}=(9,1)$. If the line $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}$ divides the triangle into two parts of equal area, then a equals
questions.solution: area of $P Q C=\frac{1}{2}$ area of $A B C$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{2}(9-a)\left(1-\frac{a}{9}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}(8 \times 1) \\ (9-a)^{2}=36 \Rightarrow a=3 \end{array} $$

questions.solution:
$x^{2}+2 x \sin (x y)+1=0$ $2 \sin (x y)=-\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)$
$\begin{array}{l} \sin (-y)=1 \quad \sin y=-1 \\ \sin y=-1 \\ y=2 n \pi-\frac{\pi}{2}, n \in I \end{array}$ Hence pair of straight lines.

questions.solution: Slope of $\mathrm{AB}=\frac{4}{2}=2$ slope of $\mathrm{BC}=-\frac{1}{2}$ $\ell(\mathrm{AB})=\sqrt{4+16}=2 \cdot 5$ distance between $2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}+4=0 \quad \& \quad 2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0 \Rightarrow \frac{4}{\sqrt{5}}$ Area $=2 \sqrt{5} \cdot \frac{8}{\sqrt{5}}=16$

r=|0+20-1|1+4=15

questions.q: Let $A=(4,0), B=(0,12)$ be two points in the plane. The locus of a point $C$ such that the area of triangle $A B C$ is 18 sq. units is -
questions.solution: $\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & x & y \\ 1 & 0 & 12 \\ 1 & 4 & 0\end{array}\right|=\pm 18$ $1(-48)-x(-12)+y(4)=\pm 36$ $12 x+4 y-48=\pm 36$ $3 x+y-12=\pm 9$ $(3 x+y-12)^{2}=81$

questions.solution: $|x+y|+|x-y|=4$ represent a square $x^{2}+y^{2}-4 x-6 y=(x-2)^{2}+(y-3)^{2}-13$ $=$ (distance point on square from $(2,3))^{2}-13$ Maximum $=(-2-2)^{2}+(-2-3)^{2}-13=28$

questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> be planes passing through the origin. Assume that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is perpendicular to the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is perpendicular to a vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is perpendicular to the vector <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. What are all the positive values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> so that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>∩</mo><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>∩</mo><msub><mi>σ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is a single point?
options: Any positive value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> other than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math>

Any positive values of a,b and c where either a≠b,b≠c
or a≠c
Any three distinct positive values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math>

There exist no such positive real numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math>
questions.solution:
According to given information equation of planes 

σ1;x+y+z=d1

σ2:ax+by+cz=d2

and σ3:a2x+b2y+c2z=d3

Now, for unique solution Δ≠0.

and Δ=111abca2b2c2

On applying c2→c2-c1 and c3→c3-c1

Δ=100ab-ac-aa2b2-a2c2-a2=b-ac-a

100a11a2b+ac+a

On applying c3→c3-c2

b-ac-a100a10a2b+ac-b

=a-bb-cc-a

So, if a,b and c has any three distinct values then Δ≠0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real solutions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sec</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>x</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
We have,

cos2xsin2x+11+x2=cos2x+sec2x

LHS=cos2xsin2x+11+x2

cos2xsin2x+11+x2≤2

RHS=cos2x+sec2x≥2

LHS=RHS

∴ cos2x+sec2x=2 at x=0

Hence, only one solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions of the equation $\sin \theta+\cos \theta=\sin 2 \theta$ in the interval $[-\pi, \pi]$ is
questions.solution:
Squaring $\sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta=\sin ^{2} 2 \theta$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} 2 \theta-\sin 2 \theta-1=0 \\
\sin 2 \theta=-\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right), \sin 2 \theta=\frac{\sqrt{5}+1}{2}(\text { reject })
\end{array}
$$
two solution exist between
$\frac{-\pi}{2}$ to $\frac{-\pi}{4} \& \frac{\pi}{4}$ to 0 (2 solution)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
The given equation 

sinπsin2θ+sinπcos2θ=2cosπ2cosθ

⇒2sinπsin2θ+cos2θ2

cosπsin2θ-cos2θ2=2cosπ2cosθ

⇒cosπ2cos2θ=cosπ2cosθ

⇒π2cos2θ=2nπ±π2cosθ, n∈ Integers

⇒cos2θ=4n±cosθ,n∈I.

Case I

If cos2θ=4n+cosθ⇒cos2θ-cosθ=4n

The above equation will hold only if n=0, 

so cos2θ=cosθ

⇒2cos2θ-cosθ-1=0⇒cosθ=1,-12

⇒θ=2kπ,2kπ±2π3,k∈I

∵ θ∈0,2π

So, θ=0,2π,2π3,4π3

Case II

If cos2θ=4n-cosθ

⇒cos2θ+cosθ=4n

The above equation will hold only if n=0,

so cos2θ+cosθ=0

⇒2cos2θ+cosθ-1=0

⇒cosθ=-1,12

⇒θ=2m+1π,2mπ±π3,m∈I.

∵ θ∈0,2π

So, θ=π,π3,5π3

∴ There are total 7 solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real numbers $\lambda$ for which the equality $\frac{\sin (\lambda \alpha)}{\sin \alpha}-\frac{\cos (\lambda \alpha)}{\cos \alpha}=\lambda-1$, holds for all real $\alpha$ which are not integral multiples of $\pi / 2$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{\sin (\lambda \alpha)}{\sin \alpha}-\frac{\cos (\lambda \alpha)}{\cos \alpha}=\lambda-1$
$\sin \lambda \alpha \cos \alpha-\cos \lambda \alpha \sin \alpha=(\lambda-1) \sin \alpha \cos \alpha$
$\sin (\lambda \alpha-\alpha)=\frac{(\lambda-1)}{2} \sin 2 \alpha$
so $=\lambda=1 ; 3$

questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
6 \sin \theta-2 \sin 3 \theta-7 \sin \theta+\sqrt{3} \cos \theta=0 \\
\sqrt{3} \cos \theta-\sin \theta=2 \sin 3 \theta
\end{array}$
It can be written as
$\begin{array}{l}

\sin \left(60^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\sin 3 \theta \\
60^{\circ}=4 \theta
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \theta=15^{\circ}$ is one of the value

Given trigonometric equation is sin9x+sin3x=0

∴ either sin6x=0

or cos3x=0

for x∈0,2π

sin6x=0

⇒ x=0, π6, π3, π2, 2π3, 5π6, π, 7π6, 4π3, 3π2, 5π3, 2π

and 3x=0

⇒ x=π6, π2, 5π6, 7π6, 3π2, 11π6

So, number of solution of given equation is 13.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>2</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>∩</mo><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> has more than two points
questions.solution:
Given trigonometric relation is 

13sinθ+23cosθ2=13sin2θ+23cos2θ

⇒ 19sin2θ+49cos2θ+49sinθcosθ

=13sin2θ+23cos2θ

⇒ 29sin2θ+29cos2θ-49sinθcosθ=0

⇒ sin2θ+cos2θ-2sinθcosθ=0

⇒ sin2θ=1

⇒ 2θ=2nπ+π2, n∈I

⇒ θ=nπ+π4, n∈I

∴ A=θ∈R:θ=nπ+π4,n∈I

∴  A∩0,π=π4

∴  A∩0,π has exactly one point.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The sum of all $x \in[0, \pi]$ which satisfy the equation $\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x=\sin ^{2}\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is -
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l}\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x=\sin ^{2}\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ \sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x=\frac{1}{2}\left(1-\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+2 x\right)\right. \\ \sin x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x=\frac{1}{2}(1+\sin 2 x) \\ 2 \sin x+\cos x=1+\sin x \cos x \\ 2 \sin x \cos x-2 \sin x+1-\cos x=0 \\ (1-\cos x)-2 \sin x(1-\cos x)=0 \\ (1-\cos x)(1-2 \sin x)=0 \\ 1-\cos x=0 & 1-2 \sin x=0 \\ \cos x=1 \quad \sin x=\frac{1}{2} \\ x=0, \quad x=\frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5 \pi}{6} \\ \text { sum }=0+\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{5 \pi}{6}=\pi\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></math> in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is
questions.solution:
Given,

sinx+x2-sinx2=sinx

⇒sinx+x2=sinx2+sinx

⇒2sinx+x22cosx+x22=2sinx2+x2cosx2-x2

sinx+x22=0

or cosx2+x2-cosx2-x2=0

x2+x2=xπ or 2sinx22sinx2=0

⇒x2+x2=0,π,2π or x22=0,π,n2=0,π,2π

⇒x2+x=2π or x2=2π

⇒x2+x-2π=0 or x=2π

⇒x=-1±1+8π2 or 2<2π<3

⇒1+8π≈25.14

∴x=5.2-12=422=2.1

∵ Total numbers of solution lies between 2,3=2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions to the equation $\cos ^{4} x+\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}=\sin ^{4} x+\frac{1}{\sin ^{2} x}$ in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$ is :
questions.solution:
$\cos ^{4} x-\sin ^{4} x=\frac{1}{\sin ^{2} x}-\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}$
$\left(\cos ^{2} x-\sin ^{2} x\right)=\frac{\left(\cos ^{2} x-\sin ^{2} x\right)}{\sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x}$
$\cos 2 x=\frac{4 \cos 2 x}{\sin ^{2} 2 x}$
$\cos 2 x\left(1-4 \operatorname{cosec}^{2} 2 x\right)=0$
$\cos 2 x=0$
$2 x=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{2}$
$x=n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{4}$
At $n=0, x=\frac{\pi}{4}$
$n=1 ; \quad x=\frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}$
$n=2, x=\frac{7 \pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $X=\{x \in R: \cos (\sin x)=\sin (\cos x)\}$. The number of elements in $X$ is -
questions.solution:
$\cos (\sin x)=\sin (\cos x)$
$\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2} \pm \sin x\right)=\sin (\cos x)$
$\cos x=n \pi+(-1)^{n}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\sin x\right), n \in I$
$$
\Rightarrow \cos x \pm \sin x=n \pi+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \frac{\pi}{2}, \mathrm{n} \in \mathrm{I}
$$
As LHS $\in[-\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{2}]$, and it does not satisfies RHS So No solution possible

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of real solutions of the equation 2sin $3 x+\sin 7 x-3=0$ which lie in the interval $[-2 \pi, 2 \pi]$ is
questions.solution:
only possible when $\sin 3 x=1 \& \sin 7 x=1$ $\sin 3 x=1$
$\begin{array}{l}
\sin 3 x=\sin (4 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, n \in I \\
3 x=(4 n+1) \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow x=(4 n+1) \frac{\pi}{6} \\
\sin 7 x=\sin (4 m+1) \frac{\pi}{2}, m \in I \\
x=(4 m+1) \frac{\pi}{14}
\end{array}$
for common solution
$(4 n+1) \frac{\pi}{6}=(4 m+1) \frac{\pi}{14}$
Solving these $1=3 \mathrm{~m}-7 \mathrm{n}$
First solution is $m=5, n=2$
Second solution is $\mathrm{m}=12, \mathrm{n}=5$
So two solutions are possible

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The integer part of the number $\sum_{\mathrm{k}=0}^{44} \frac{1}{\cos \mathrm{k}^{\circ} \cos (\mathrm{k}+1)^{\circ}} \mathrm{is}$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{\cos 0^{\circ} \cos 1^{\circ}}+\frac{1}{\cos 1^{\circ} \cos 2^{\circ}}+\frac{1}{\cos 2^{\circ} \cos 3^{\circ}}+\ldots \\
\ldots \frac{1}{\cos 44^{\circ} \cos 45^{\circ}}
\end{array}
$$
multiply \& divided by $\sin 1^{\circ}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{1}{\sin 1^{\circ}}\left[\frac{\sin 1^{\circ}}{\cos 0^{\circ} \cos 1^{\circ}}+\frac{\sin 1^{\circ}}{\cos 1^{\circ} \cos 2^{\circ}}+\ldots \frac{\sin 1^{\circ}}{\cos 44^{\circ} \cos 45^{\circ}}\right] \\
\frac{1}{\sin 1^{\circ}}\left[\frac{\sin \left(1^{\circ}-0^{\circ}\right)}{\cos 0^{\circ} \cos 1^{\circ}}+\frac{\sin (2-1)^{\circ}}{\cos 1^{\circ} \cos 2^{\circ}}+\ldots \frac{\sin \left(45^{\circ}-44^{\circ}\right)}{\cos 44^{\circ} \cos 45^{\circ}}\right] \\
\frac{1}{\sin 1^{\circ}}\left[\tan 1^{\circ}-\tan 0^{\circ}+\tan 2^{\circ}-\tan 1^{\circ}+\ldots \tan 45^{\circ}-\tan 44^{\circ}\right] \\
=\frac{1}{\sin 1^{\circ}}\left[\tan 45^{\circ}\right] \\
=\frac{1}{0.0174524}=57.2987 \\
\text { Integral part }=57
\end{array}
$$

$\cot 9^{\circ}-\cot 27^{\circ}-\tan 27^{\circ}+\tan 9^{\circ}$

$=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The product $\left(1+\tan 1^{\circ}\right)\left(1+\tan 2^{\circ}\right)\left(1+\tan 3^{\circ}\right) \ldots\left(1+\tan 45^{\circ}\right)$ equals-
questions.solution: $(1+\tan \theta)(1+\tan (45-\theta))=(1+\tan \theta)\left(1+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right)=2$ $\left(1+\tan 1^{\circ}\right)\left(1+\tan 44^{\circ}\right)=2$ etc so product $=2^{22}\left(1+\tan 45^{\circ}\right)=2^{23}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ of a triangle satisfy the equations $2 \sin \alpha+3 \cos \beta=3 \sqrt{2}$ and $3 \sin \beta+2 \cos \alpha=1$. Then angle $\gamma$ equals
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
2 \sin \alpha+3 \cos \beta=3 \sqrt{2}...(1) \\
3 \sin \beta+2 \cos \alpha=1...(2)
\end{array}
$$
sum of squares of equation (1) and (2)
$4+9+12 \sin (\alpha+\beta)=19$
$\sin (\alpha+\beta)=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \alpha+\beta=150^{\circ}$ or $30^{\circ}$
If $\alpha+\beta=30^{\circ} \Rightarrow \beta=30-\alpha$
put in equation (1) and (2)
we get $7 \sin \alpha+3 \sqrt{3} \cos \alpha=6 \sqrt{2}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
7 \cos \alpha-3 \sqrt{3} \sin \alpha=2 \\
\cos \alpha=\frac{7+9 \sqrt{6}}{37}=.8 < \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\
\cos \alpha < \cos 30^{\circ} \quad \because \alpha>30^{\circ} \quad \therefore \alpha+\beta \neq 30^{\circ}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\vec{a}=6 \vec{i}-3 \vec{j}-6 \vec{k}$ and $\vec{d}=\vec{i}+\vec{j}+\vec{k}$. Suppose that $\vec{a}=\vec{b}+\vec{c}$ where $\vec{b}$ is parallel to $\vec{d}$ and $\vec{c}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{d}$. Then $\vec{c}$ is
questions.solution:
$\vec{a}=6 \vec{i}-3 \vec{j}-6 \vec{k}$ 
$\vec{d}=\vec{i}+\vec{j}+\vec{k}$ $\vec{a}=\vec{b}+\vec{c}$ 
$\vec{b}=\lambda \vec{d} \quad \& \quad \vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=0$ $\vec{b}=\lambda \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}$ From $(1)$ $6 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}=\lambda \hat{i}+\lambda \hat{j}+\lambda \hat{k}+\vec{c}$ $\vec{c}=(6-\lambda) \hat{i}-(3+\lambda) \hat{j}-(6+\lambda) \hat{k}$ $\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=0$ $\Rightarrow \lambda=-1$ $\vec{c}=7 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-5 \hat{k}$

$(x, y)=\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right)$
$V=\frac{3 i+3 j}{2}$
$|\mathrm{v}|=\frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \in(2,3)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> be three vectors. The area of the region formed by the set of points whose position vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>r</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> satisfy the equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>·</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>+</mo><mo>|</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is closest to the integer.
questions.solution:
Since, |r-b|+|r-c|=4 is equation of ellipsoid in plane r,a=5 or x+y+z=5.

∴ Distance between foci =|b-c|

=|3i^-j^-2k^|=14=2ae

and 2a=4

∴ e=144⇒e2=1-b2a2

⇒1416=1-b24⇒b24=216

b2=12

∴ Area of ellipsoid 

=πab=π212=2π

And from the given options the closest integer to 2π is 4.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
In a triangle $A B C$, points $X$ and $Y$ are on $A B$ and $A C$, respectively, such that $X Y$ is parallel to BC, Which of the two following always hold? (Here [PQR] denotes the area of triangle PQR.)
(I) $[\mathrm{BCX}]=[\mathrm{BCY}]$
(II) $[\mathrm{ACX}] \cdot[\mathrm{ABY}]=[\mathrm{AXY}] \cdot[\mathrm{ABC}]$.
questions.solution: $\Delta \mathrm{BCX}=\Delta \mathrm{BCY}$ (Obvious) Same base and same height Now Let $\mathrm{A}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{O}}), \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ So $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AX}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AY}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}$ $$ \Delta \mathrm{ACX}=\frac{1}{2} \lambda|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \Delta \mathrm{ABY}=\frac{1}{2} \lambda|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \\ \Delta \mathrm{AXY}=\frac{1}{2} \lambda^{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \\ \Delta \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \end{array} $$

Then the maximum possible value of $|(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{u}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}) \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{w}}|$ is-

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a triangle $A B C$, let $G$ denote its centroid and let $M, N$ be points in the interiors of the segments $A B, A C$, respectively, such that $\mathrm{M}, \mathrm{G}, \mathrm{N}$ are collinear. If $\mathrm{r}$ denotes the ratio of the area of triangle AMN to the area of ABC then
questions.solution: Let $\overrightarrow{A B}=\vec{b}, \overrightarrow{A C}=\vec{c}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \overrightarrow{A M}=\lambda \vec{b} \\ \overrightarrow{A N}=m \vec{c} \end{array} $$ Let $G$ divides $\mathrm{MN}$ in the ratio $\mathrm{K}: 1$ So $\frac{k \mu \vec{c}+\lambda \vec{b}}{k+1}=\frac{\vec{b}+\vec{c}}{3}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{k} \mu}{\mathrm{k}+1}=\frac{1}{3} \quad \frac{\lambda}{\mathrm{k}+1}=\frac{1}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{k}=\frac{\lambda}{\mu} \\ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda}+\frac{1}{\mu}=3 \\ \mathrm{AM} \geq \mathrm{GM} \\ \frac{1}{\lambda}+\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{1}{2} \geq \frac{2}{\sqrt{\lambda \mu}} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} \leq \lambda \mu...(1) \end{array} $$ Now, $\frac{\text { area of } \triangle \mathrm{AMN}}{\text { area of } \mathrm{ABC}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \lambda \mu|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|}{\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|}$ $=\lambda \mu$ using $\frac{1}{\lambda}+\frac{1}{\mu}=3 \Rightarrow$ Ratio $=\frac{\lambda}{3 \lambda-1} \lambda \in[0,1]$ maximum value of ratio $=\frac{\lambda^{2}}{3 \lambda-1}$ attain when $\lambda=1$ using derivative but $\lambda$ is not 1 becuase $M$ is an interior point. so $\frac{4}{9} \leq$ ratio $ < \frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the R-L-C circuit given below. The circuit is driven by a $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ AC source with peak voltage $220 \mathrm{~V} .$ If $\mathrm{R}=400 \Omega, \mathrm{C}=200 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{L}=6 \mathrm{H}$, the maximum current in the circuit is closest to
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\omega \mathrm{L}=2 \pi \times 50 \times 6=600 \pi \Omega$
$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi 80 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}=\frac{100}{2 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi} \Omega$
$\mathrm{I}_{\max }=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{z}}=\frac{220}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^{2}}}$
$=\frac{220}{\sqrt{400^{2}+\left(600 \pi-\frac{50}{\pi}\right)^{2}}}$
$\mathrm{I}_{\max }=0.120 \mathrm{~A}$
