questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The output voltage (taken across the resistance) of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></math> series resonant circuit falls to half its peak value at a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> and again reaches the same value at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The bandwidth of this circuit is:
questions.solution:
VOutput =VR

=irmsR

=V0RZ=V0RR2+XL−XC2

For peak XL=XC⇒Vpeak =V0
For VOutput =V02
V02=V0RR2+XL-XC2
R2+XL-XC2=4R2
XL-XC=±3R
ωL-1ωC=±3R
ω2LC∓3RωC-1=0

ω=±3RC±3R2C2+4LC2LC
ω1=-3RC+3R2C2+4LC2LC=200×2π
ω2=+3RC+3R2C2+4LC2LC=800×2π
ω2-ω1=600×2π=3RL
Bandwidth =RL=2π×6003
Δf=12πRL=6003=2003

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An initially uncharged capacitor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is being charged by a battery of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> through a resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> upto the instant when the capacitor is charged to the potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the ratio of the work done by the battery to the heat dissipated by the resistor is given by:-
questions.solution:
i=ERe-t/RC,Q=CE1-e-t/RC
Capacitor is charged to E2
So Q=CE2
∴CE2=CE1-e-t/RC
12=e-t/RC
t=RCln2
Work done by battery =Qflown (ΔV)

=CE2(E)=CE22

Heat dissipated =∫0RCℓn2i2Rdt
=E2R∫0RCln2e-2t/RC·dt
=34CE22
 Work done  Heat dissipated =CE2/234CE22=43

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circuit consists of a coil with inductance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> and an uncharged capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. The coil is in a constant uniform magnetic field such that the flux through the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ϕ</mi></math>. At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>, the magnetic field is abruptly switched OFF. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><msqrt><mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi></msqrt></math> and ignore the resistance of the circuit. Then,
options: magnitude of the charge on the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>Q</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>sin</mi><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
initial current in the circuit is infinite
initial charge on the capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><msub><mi>ω</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>ϕ</mi></math>
questions.solution:
At t=0 capacitor is uncharged and flux of inductor is ϕ.

Now, using ϕ=LI, at t=0 current in circuit is

I0=ϕL

Instantaneous current in circuit is dqdt.

where, q is solution of Ld2qdt2+qC=0

or d2qdt2+qLC=0

At t=0, current in circuit is non-zero, so instantaneous current is given by 

=I0cosωt=ϕL·cosωt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider the infinite ladder circuit shown below.
For which angular frequency $\omega$ will the circuit behave like a pure inductance?
questions.solution:
Let the equivalent impedance of the circuit be $Z$
So $Z=\omega L+Z^{\prime}$
now $Z^{\prime}=\frac{Z X_{C}}{Z+X_{C}}$
$$
\therefore Z=\omega L+\left(\frac{Z \times \frac{1}{\omega C}}{Z+\frac{1}{\omega C}}\right)
$$
on ving we get $Z=\frac{\omega L C \pm \sqrt{(\omega L C)^{2}-4 L C}}{2 C}$
for $Z$ to be purely inductive
$$
\omega^{2} L^{2} C^{2}-4 L C=0 \text { or } \omega=\frac{2}{\sqrt{L C}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a muonic atom, a muon of mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn></math> times of that of electron and same charge is bound to the proton. The wavelengths of its Balmer series are in the range of
questions.solution:
A muon is an unstable elementary particle of mass nearly 200 me and charge ±e.

Here, a negative muon is given bound to a proton. 

So, m=200 me and M=1836 me

(as mass of a proton is 1836 times of mass of electron)

So, reduced mass of system, 

m'=mMm+M=200me×1836me200me+1836me≈180me

As mass of muon is comparable to mass of proton, we have to take account of motion of nucleus. That's way we are calculating reduced mass.

Now, as energy of an electron in nth orbit is of H-atom,

En=me48ε02h2n2

So, energy of a muon of muonic atom is 

En'=m'e48ε02h2n2⇒En'=180En

Now, considering a Balmer transition, n=3 to n=2.

∴ ΔE'=hcλ

or ΔE'=En=3'-En=2'=hcλ

⇒ 180En=3-En=2=hcλ

⇒180-13.632--13.622=hcλ

⇒180×189 eV=1240eV·nmλnm

⇒λnm=3.6 nm

This emitted radiation is X-rays.

Note X-rays have a  wavelength range of 0.01 nm to 10 nm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The emission series of hydrogen atom is given by
$$
\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{n_{2}^{2}}\right)
$$
where $R$ is the Rydberg constant. For a transition from $\mathrm{n}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{n}_{1}$, the relative change $\Delta \lambda / \lambda$ in the emission wavelength if hydrogen is replaced by deuterium (assume that the mass of proton and neutron are the same and approximately 2000 times larger than that of electrons) is
questions.solution:
$$
\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}} \mathrm{e}^{4}}{8 \varepsilon_{0}^{2} \mathrm{ch}^{3}}
$$
where $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=$ mass of electron
When we consider mass of nucleus also then we replace $\mathrm{m}$ with reduced mass
$$
\mu=\frac{m_{e} m}{m_{e}+m}
$$
where $\mathrm{m}=$ mass of nucleus
In case of hydrogen atom
$$
\mu_{1}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)\left(2000 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}+(2000) \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{2000}{2001} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}
$$
In case of deuterium
$$
\mu_{2}=\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)\left(4000 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}+(4000) \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{4000}{4001} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}
$$
Hence,
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}} &=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}} \\
&=\left(\frac{4000 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}}{4001}\right) \frac{(2001)}{\left(2000 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)} \\
&=\frac{4002}{4001} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_{2}}{}^{-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1}}=& \frac{1}{4001} \\
\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda_{1}} \times 100 &=\frac{1}{4001} \times 100 \\
&=0.025 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{r} \omega$
$\mathrm{mr}^{2} \omega=\mathrm{n} \frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$
$\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{nh}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{m} \omega}}$
$\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}^{2}}{2 \pi \mathrm{m} \omega}}$
$\mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \mathrm{a}$

$r \propto \frac{1}{z} \& v \propto z$
$\therefore$ according $\left(\frac{v^{2}}{r}\right) \propto z^{2} \times \frac{1}{(1 / z)}=z^{3}$
$\frac{a_{H}}{a_{H e}}=\left(\frac{z_{H}}{z_{\mathrm{He}}}\right)^{3}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3}=\frac{1}{8}$

questions.solution:
Fe=mv2r⇒mv2r=k(Ze)(e)r2
12mv2=KZe22r…i (Kinetic energy)

∴r∝n
As kinetic energy =KZe22r⇒KE∝1n

L=mvr
L∝vr

L∝1n(n)∝n

$\begin{array}{l}
Q=\frac{C}{2} E \\
C e q=\frac{C \cdot K C}{C+K C}=\frac{C^{2} K}{C(1+k)}=\frac{C K}{1+K} \\
Q=\frac{C K}{1+k} E \\

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A smaller cube with side b (depicted by dashed lines) is excised from a bigger uniform cube with side a as shown below such that both cubes have a common vertex $\mathrm{P}$. Let $\mathrm{X}=\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$. If the centre of mass of the remaining solid is at the vertex $\mathrm{O}$ of smaller cube then $\mathrm{X}$ satisfies.
questions.solution:
Centre of mass of remaining cube $x$ coordinate $=b$
$$ \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{a}^{3} \times \frac{\mathrm{a}}{2}-\rho \mathrm{b}^{3} \times \frac{\mathrm{b}}{2}}{\rho \mathrm{a}^{3}-\rho \mathrm{b}^{3}} $$ We will consider removed mass as a negative mass $$ \begin{array}{l} b=\frac{\frac{\rho a^{4}}{2}-\frac{\rho b^{4}}{2}}{\rho a^{3}-\rho b^{3}} \\ a^{3} b-b^{4}=\frac{a^{4}}{2}-\frac{b^{4}}{2} \\ 2 a^{3} b-2 b^{4}=a^{4}-b^{4} \\ \text { put a }=b x \Rightarrow 2 b^{4} x^{3} b-2 b^{4}=b^{4} x^{4}-b^{4} \\ 2 x^{3}-1==x^{4} \\ 2 x^{3}-2+1=x^{4} \\ 2\left[x^{3}-1\right]=\left(x^{2}-1\right)\left(x^{2}+1\right) \\ 2[x-1]\left[x^{2}+1+x\right]=[x-1][x+1]\left[x^{2}+1\right] \\ 2 x^{2}+2+2 x=x^{3}+x+x^{2}+1 \\ x^{3}-x^{2}-x-1=0 \end{array} $$

Collision is elastic, so both linear momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
We have following situation,

12MV2=12MV2+12mv2
(kinetic energy conservation)

MV-V'=mv

and MV2-V2=mv2
Dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii), we have
MV2-V2MV-V'=mv2mv
or V+V'=v

questions.q:
Figure below shows a shampoo bottle in a perfect cylindrical shape

In a simple experiment, the stability of the bottle filled with different amount of shampoo volume is observed. The bottle is tilted from one side and then released. base Area =A=πr2

density of shampoo =ρ

mass of shampoo =ρfAL

Center of mass of system

y=m0L2+ρfALfL2m0+ρfAL

for critical angular displacement, mg will pass through tilted side.

From the diagram tanθ=ry

tanθ=rm0+ρALfL2m0+ρALf2

at f=0 & f=1, tipping angle θ will be same.

for very small values of f, we can neglect f2 terms

⇒tanθ=rL2m0+ρALfm0

θ=tan-1rL2m0+ρALfm0

So if f increases θ will increase

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A point mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving with a certain velocity collides with a stationary point mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The collision is elastic and in one-dimension. Let the ratio of the final velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
As collision is elastic both linear momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. 

We have, following given condition, 

Momentum conservation gives, 

Mu1=Mv1+M2v2⇒2u1=2v1+v2   ...(i)

Energy conservation gives, 

12Mu12=12Mv12+12M2v22

⇒2u12=2v12+v22   ...(ii)

Now, substituting for u1 from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we get 

22v1+v222=2v12+v22

⇒4u12+v22+4v1v2=4v12+2v22

⇒v22=4v1v2 or v1v2=14

∴ Ratio of final velocities of M and M2 is 14.

questions.solution: $\mathrm{CM}$ will go downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q:
Two identical uniform rectangular blocks (with longest side $\mathrm{L}$ ) and a solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ are to be balanced at the edge of a heavy table such that the centre of the sphere remains at the maximum possible horizontal distance from the vertical edge of the table without toppling as indicated in the figure.
If the mass of each block is $\mathrm{M}$ and of the sphere is $\mathrm{M/2}$, then the maximum distance $\mathrm{x}$ that can be achieved is
questions.solution: $2+\mathrm{S}$ System lie above edge of 1 . $\begin{array}{l} \frac{M}{2} y-M\left(\frac{L}{2}-y\right)=0 \\ \frac{y}{2}+y=\frac{L}{2} \\ y=\frac{L}{3} \end{array}$ Now $1+2+\mathrm{S}$ centre of mass wili lie above the table $\frac{3 M}{2}\left(x-\frac{L}{3}\right)+M\left(x-\frac{L}{3}-\frac{L}{2}\right)=0$ $\frac{3 x}{2}-\frac{L}{2}+x-\frac{L}{3}-\frac{L}{2}=0$ $\frac{5 x}{2}=\frac{4 L}{3} \quad \quad x=\frac{8 L}{15}$

questions.q: A particle is acted upon by a force given by $F=-\alpha x^{3}-\beta x^{4}$ where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. At the point $\mathrm{x}=0$, the particle is $-$
questions.solution: $\frac{-\mathrm{d} \mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{F},\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)_{\mathrm{x}=0}=0 \text { and }\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}\right)_{\mathrm{x}=0}=0$

questions.solution: Using energy conservation and law of restitution and momentum conservation

questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A narrow but tall cabin is falling freely near the earth's surface. Inside the cabin, two small stones $A$ and $B$ are released from rest (relative to the cabin). Initially $A$ is much above the centre of mass and $B$ much below the centre of mass of the cabin. A close observation of the motion of $A$ and $B$ will reveal that -
options: both $A$ and $B$ continue to be exactly at rest relative to the cabin
A moves slowly upward and $B$ moves slowly downward relative to the cabin
both $A$ and $B$ fall to the bottom of the cabin with constant acceleration due to gravity
$A$ and $B$ move slightly towards each other vertically
questions.solution: $a_{B}>a_{C}>a_{A}$ $a_{B}=g$ $\vec{a}_{\Lambda / C M}=\vec{a}_{\Lambda}-\vec{a}_{C M}(\uparrow)$ $\vec{a}_{B / C M}=\vec{a}_{B}-\vec{a}_{C M}(\downarrow)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two masses $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ are connected by a massless spring of spring constant $\mathrm{k}$ and unstreched length $\ell$. The masses are placed on a frictionless straight channel - which we consider our $x$-axis. They are initially at rest at $\mathrm{x}=0$ and $\mathrm{x}=\ell$, respectively. At $\mathrm{t}=0$, a velocity of $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ is suddenly imparted to the first particle. At a later time to, the centre of mass of the two masses is at-
questions.solution: $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{0}+0}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}, \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{iCOM}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}(0)+\mathrm{m}_{2}(\ell)}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}} \\ \text { also } \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{COM}}+\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{Com}} \mathrm{t} \\ \therefore \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{COM}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}_{2} \ell}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{v}_{0} \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: An electron with kinetic energy $E$ collides with a hydrogen atom in the ground state. The collision will be elastic
questions.solution:
When $\mathrm{e}^{-}$collide with atom which is massive in comparison to $\mathrm{e}^{-} .$Max possible loss of $\mathrm{KE}=\mathrm{KE}$ of $\mathrm{e}^{-}($initial $\mathrm{KE})=\mathrm{E}$ if this $\mathrm{E}$ is less than min excitation energy then collision is elastic
$\therefore \mathrm{E} < 10.2 \mathrm{eV}$ (Minimum excitation energy)

questions.solution: $$ \begin{array}{l} m_{1} \times \frac{h}{3}=m_{2} \times \frac{b}{2} \\ \ell b=\frac{1}{2} \times \ell h \end{array} $$ From (1) and (2) $$ \frac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\frac{3}{4} $$

questions.q:
A uniform square wooden sheet of side a has its center of mass located at point $\mathrm{O}$ as shown in the figure on the left. A square portion of side $b$ of this sheet is cut out to produce and L-shaped sheet as shown in the figure on the right.

The center of mass of the L-shaped sheet lies at the point $\mathrm{P}$ (in the diagram) when
questions.solution: Finaly com at $p$ $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{am}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}_{1} \mathrm{X}_{1}+\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}}{\mathrm{~A}_{1}+\mathrm{A}_{2}}$ $(a-b)=\frac{a(a-b) \frac{(a-b)}{2}+b(a-b)(a-b+b / 2)}{a(a-b)+(a-b) b}$ $\begin{array}{l} \therefore\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)-1=0 \\ \frac{a}{b}=\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.q: A clay ball of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and speed $\mathrm{v}$ strikes another metal ball. of same mass $\mathrm{m}$, which is at rest. They stick together after collision. The kinetic energy of the system after collision is -
questions.solution:
Applying the law of conservation of momentum, $\mathrm{mv}+0=(2 \mathrm{~m}) \mathrm{v}^{\prime}$
$\mathrm{v}^{\prime}=\mathrm{v} / 2$
$\mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2}(2 \mathrm{~m}) \mathrm{v}^{\prime 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: An electron collides with a free molecules initially in its ground state. The collision leaves the molecules in an excited state that is metastable and does not decay to the ground state by radiation. Let $\mathrm{K}$ be the sum of the initial kinetic energies of the electron and the molecule, and $\vec{P}$ the sum of their initial momenta. Let $\mathrm{K}^{\prime}$ and $\vec{P}$ 'represent the same physical quantities after the collision. Then -
questions.solution:
Collision of e $^{-}$lead to excitation of molecules so Collision is inelastic
$\therefore \mathrm{K}^{\prime} < \mathrm{K}$ and loss of kinetic energy go for excitation of molecules. Momentum remain conserved during collision.
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}^{\prime}}$

The cylinder topples over when the water reaches a certain height $h$, given by.
questions.solution: $\tan \theta=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2}}{\mathrm{a} / 2}$ $\mathrm{~h}=\mathrm{a} \tan \theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the circuit shown below (on the left) the resistance and the emf source are both variable. The graph of seven readings of the voltmeter and the ammeter (V and I, respectively) for different setting of resistance and the emf, taken at equal intervals of time $\Delta t$, are shown (on the right) by the dots connected by the curve EFGH. Consider the interval resistance of the battery to be negligible and the voltmeter and ammeter to be ideal devices. Take $R_{0}=V_{0} I_{0}$
Then the plot of the resistance as a function of time corresponding to the curve EFGH is given by
questions.solution:
From $\mathrm{E} \rightarrow \mathrm{F}$
Slope is constant
$V=\mid R$
Thus $R$ must be constant
$\mathrm{R}_{0}=\mathrm{V}_{0} / \mathrm{I}_{0}$
Frorm $\mathrm{F} \rightarrow \mathrm{G}$
$\mathrm{V}=$ constant
$\mathrm{I}$ is increase thus $\mathrm{R}$ must be decrease.
at $G, R=\frac{V_{0}}{2 I_{0}}=\frac{R_{0}}{2}$
From $\mathbf{G} \rightarrow \mathbf{H}$
$\mathrm{I}=$ constant
$\mathrm{V}=$ increase, thus $\mathrm{R}$ must be increase
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{l}_{0}}=\mathrm{R}_{0}$

If a current of magnitude $1 \mathrm{~mA}$ flows through the resistor marked $\mathrm{X}$, what is the potential difference measured between point $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ ?
questions.solution: Using KCL At point $A$ Current is $3 \mathrm{~mA}$ At point $C$ Current is $8 \mathrm{~mA}$ At point $E$ Current is $21 \mathrm{~mA}$ At point $G$ Current through $\mathrm{GH}$ is 34 ma $\begin{array}{l} \therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{GH}}=\mathrm{i} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{GH}} \\ =34 \mathrm{~V} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A student uses the resistance of a known resistor (1 Ω ) to calibrate a voltmeter and an ammeter using the circuits shown below. The student measures the ratio of the voltage to current to be 1×103Ω in circuit
(a) and 0.999Ω in circuit (b). From these measurements, the resistance (in Ω ) of the voltmeter and ammeter are found to be close to
questions.solution:
When a voltmeter put in series, it still reads potential drop and when an ammeter is connected in parallel, it still shows current through it.
Case a

Let I= current through cell, then potential drop read by voltmeter is V=I·RV (this is reading of voltmeter) Where, RV is the resistance of voltmeter In loopA B, VAB=I1×1=I2×RA and I=I1+I2
Where, RA is the resistance of ammeter We substitute for I1 from above equation to get ⇒I=I2RA+I2=I2RA+1
⇒I2=IRA+1
(this is reading of ammeter)

Now given,  voltmeter readin'g  ammeter reading =1×103=IRVIRA+1
So,RVRA+1=10001000$
Case b

Let I= current through cell, then ammeter reading in this case is I.
Also, in loop A B,
VAB=I1×1=I2×RV

As, I=I1+I2=I2RV+I2

=I2RV+1
So, I2=IRV+1
Hence, voltmeter reading is V=I2RV =IRVRV+1   (this is reading of voltmeter)
Now given, voltmeter reading ÷ ammeter reading =0.999Ω.
So,0.999=IRVRV+1I

⇒0.999=RVRV+1

So,RV=999Ω
≈103Ω
Substituting RV in Eq (i), we get
RA=1999
or RA=10-3Ω

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steady current I is set up in a wire whose cross-sectional area decreases in the direction of the flow of the current. Then, as we examine the narrowing region,
options: the magnitude of the electric field increases
the average speed of the moving charges remains constant
questions.solution:
When current flows through a conductor of tapered cross-section, current flow through every section remains constant.

⇒  I1=I2

⇒  j1 A1=j2 A2

⇒  j1j2=A2A1<1

⇒  j1<j2

Current density increases in the narrow region. Also,
=nevd

⇒  nevd1<nevd2

⇒  vd1<vd2

Drift velocity increases in the narrow region.

and   j=Eρ

where, ρ= resistivity of material.

⇒  E1ρ<E2ρ⇒E1<E2

Electric field magnitude increases in the narrow region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A student was trying to construct the circuit shown in the figure below marked (a), but ended up constructing the circuit marked (b). Realizing her mistake, she corrected the circuit, but to her surprise, the output voltage (across R) did not change.

The value of resistance R is :-
questions.solution:
For circuit (a),

iR=10300R300+R↑Current through cell+300×300300+R

[Note : 300 Ω & R are in parallel which is in series with 100 & 200 Ω]

∴ VRa=10×300R300R+3002+300R

[VRa is potential difference across resistance R]

For circuit (b),

iR=10(200+R)(300)200+R+300↑Current through cell+100×300300+200+R

[Note: R & 200 Ω are in series which is in parallel with 300 Ω & again the combination is in series with 100 Ω]

∴ VRb=100×300R300×200+300R+100×500+100R

[VRb is potential difference across resistance R]

According to given situation

VRa=VRb

∴ 300R+9×104+300R=6×104+400R+5×104

⇒200R=2×104⇒R=100 Ω

Consider the circuit shown in the figure below :
All the resistors are identical. The ratio $\mathrm{I} / \mathrm{I} ^{\prime}$ is
questions.solution: $\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=8$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $10.0 \mathrm{~W}$ electrical heater is used to heat a container filled with $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water. It is found that the temperature of the water and the container rise by $3 \mathrm{K}$ in $15 \mathrm{~minutes}$. The container is then emptied, dried, and filled with $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of an oil. It is now observed that the same heater raises the temperature of the container-oil system by $2 \mathrm{K}$ in $20 \mathrm{~minutes}$. Assuming no other heat losses in any of the processes, the specific heat capacity of the oil is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Pt}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{w}} \mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{w}} \Delta \mathrm{T}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{c}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $10 \times 15 \times 60=0.5 \times 4200 \times 3+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{c}} \times 3$ $9000=6300+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{c}} 3$ 
$\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{s}_{\mathrm{c}}=900 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{k}$. 
Now, for oil 
$10 \times 20 \times 60=2 \times \mathrm{S}_{0} \times 2+900 \times 2$ 
$12000-1800=4 \mathrm{~S}_{0}$ 
$\mathrm{~S}_{0}=\frac{10200}{4}=2.51 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{k}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of $0.1$ A flows through a $25 ~\Omega$ resistor represented by the circuit diagram. The current in the $80 ~\Omega$ resistor is
questions.solution: $0.1 \times\left(25+\frac{20 \times 60}{20+60}\right)=i_{2} \times 20$ $I_{2}=0.2 \mathrm{~A}$ Hence, $\mathrm{i}$ through $80 \Omega$ $0.1+0.2=0.3 \mathrm{~A}$

options: Electrons always flow from lower to higher potential
Electrons always flow from higher to lower potential

questions.solution: No Solution

Resistance between point $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is-

questions.q: Following figures show different combinations of identical bulb(s) connected to identical battery(ies). Which option is correct regarding the total power dissipated in the circuit-
questions.solution: Let $\mathrm{R}=$ resistance of each bulb.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the circuit shown, $\mathrm{n}$ identical resistors $\mathrm{R}$ are connected in parallel $(\mathrm{n}>1$ ) and the combination is connected in series to another resistor $\mathrm{R}_{0}$. In the adjoining circuit $\mathrm{n}$ resistors of resistance $\mathrm{R}$ are all connected in series along with $\mathrm{R}_{0}-$
The batteries in both circuits are identical and net power dissipated in the n resistors in both circuits is same. The ratio $\mathrm{R}_{0} / \mathrm{R}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{i}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}}{\mathrm{n} R_{0}+\mathrm{R}}, \mathrm{i}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\left(\mathrm{R}_{0}+\mathrm{n} \mathrm{R}\right)} \\
\mathrm{P}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}^{2} \mathrm{R}}{\left(\mathrm{nR}_{0}+\mathrm{R}\right)^{2}}, \mathrm{P}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{nE}^{2} \mathrm{R}}{\left(\mathrm{R}_{0}+\mathrm{nR}\right)^{2}} \\
\because \mathrm{P}_{1}=\mathrm{P}_{2} \\
\text { Hence } \mathrm{R}_{0} / \mathrm{R}=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit shown below the resistance are given in ohms and the battery is assumed ideal with emf equal to $3.0$ volts. The resistor that dissipates the most power is -
questions.solution: Power dissipate in $\mathrm{R}_{1}$ is maximum as its current is maximum and its resistance is also $40 \Omega$ which is higher than $\mathrm{R}_{5} \mathrm{R}_{4}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two batteries $\mathrm{V}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2}$ are connected to three resistors as shown below.
If $V_{1}=2 V$ and $V_{2}=0 \mathrm{~V}$, the current $I=3 \mathrm{~mA}$. If $V_{1}=0 \mathrm{~V}$ and $V_{2}=4 V$, the current $I=4 \mathrm{~mA}$. Now, if $\mathrm{V}_{1}=10 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\mathrm{V}_{2}=10 \mathrm{~V}$, the current I will be-
questions.solution: $\mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{eq}}}{\mathrm{R}+\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}$ In each case $R_{e q} \& R$ is same only $V_{1} \& V_{2}$ is changing $\therefore V_{\text {eq }}$ is changing $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{R}_{2}+0 \times \mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}} \quad\left[\mathrm{~V}_{1}=2, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=0\right]$ $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}$ Case $-2 \quad \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{4 \mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}} \quad\left[\mathrm{~V}_{1}=0, \mathrm{~V}_{2}=4\right]$ $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\frac{3}{4}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{4 \mathrm{R}_{1}} \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\frac{3}{2}$ Case - $3 \quad \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{10 \mathrm{R}_{1}+10 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}}$ $\frac{3}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{10\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \times 1.5 \mathrm{R}_{1}}{10\left(2.5 \mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}$ or $\mathrm{I}^{\prime}=25 \mathrm{~mA}$ $\frac{3}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{2}}{10\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{2}\right)} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{\mathrm{I}^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \times 1.5 \mathrm{R}_{1}}{10\left(2.5 \mathrm{R}_{1}\right)}$ or $\mathrm{I}^{\prime}=25 \mathrm{~mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the following circuit, the $1 \Omega$ resistor dissipates power $P$. If the resistor is replaced by $9 \Omega$, the power dissipated in it is
questions.solution: $10=4 \mathrm{i}$ $\mathrm{i}=\frac{5}{2}$ $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{i}^{2} \mathrm{R}=\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^{2} \times 1=\frac{25}{4}$ $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}=\left(\frac{10}{12}\right)^{2} \times 9=\frac{100}{12 \times 12} \times 9$ $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathbf{P}_{\mathrm{i}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure below shows a portion of an electric circuit with the currents in amperes and their directions. The magnitude and direction of the current in the portion PQ is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A student in a town in India, where the price per unit (1 unit =1kWh ) of electricity is ₹ 5.00, purchases a 1kVA UPS (uninterrupted power supply) battery. A day before the exam, 10 friends arrive to the student's home with their laptops and all connect their laptops to the UPS. Assume that each laptop has a constant power requirement of 90 W. Consider the following statements.
I. All the 10 laptops can be powered by the UPS, if connected directly.
II. All the 10 laptops can be powered, if connected using an extension box with a 3 A fuse.
III. If all the 10 friends use the laptop for 5 h, then the cost of the consumed electricity is about ₹ 22.50 .
Select the correct option with the true statements.
questions.solution:
Power requirement for 1 laptop, P1=90 W
So, power r quirement for 10 laptops,
P=10×P1=10×90-900 W=0.9 kW
In 5 h, electrical energy used by all laptops,

E=P×t=0.9×5

=4.5kWh

cost of electrical energy used is
cost =E× Unit cost

=4.5×5
=₹ 22.50

So, statement III is correct.
For laptop charger, input voltage is 220 V
So, current when all 10 laptops are connected through an extension,

I=PV=900220≈4.1 A

As, line current exceeds current rating of fuse, therefore 3 A fuse cannot be used. So, statement II is incorrect.

questions.q: In the circuit, wire 1 is of negligible resistance, Then

Current will flow through wire 1 if $\varepsilon_{1} / \mathrm{R}_{1} \neq \varepsilon_{2} / \mathrm{R}_{2}$
Current will flow through wire 1 if $\left(\varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{2}\right) /\left(R_{1}+R_{2}\right) \neq\left(\varepsilon_{1}-\varepsilon_{2}\right) /\left(R_{1}-R_{2}\right)$
No current will flow through wire 1 .
questions.solution: current through wire 1 = 0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light bulb of resistance $R=16 \Omega$ is attached in series with an infinite resistor network. with identical resistances $\mathrm{r}$ as shown below. A $10 \mathrm{~V}$ battery derives current in the circuit. What should be the value of $r$ such that the bulb dissipated about $1 \mathrm{~W}$ of power.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{P}_{\text {bulb }}=\frac{v^{2}}{R}=i^{2} R \\
1=\frac{v^{2}}{16} \\
\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{B}}=4 \mathrm{~V} \\
1=i^{2} \times 16 \\
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{Amp} . \\
6=\frac{1}{4} \times r_{e q}(\text { equivalent of groups of } \mathrm{r})
\end{array}
$$
Where
$$
r_{e q}=r+\frac{r_{e q} \cdot r}{r_{e q}+r}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two rods of copper and iron with the same cross-sectional area are joined at S and a steady current I flows through the rods as shown in the figure.

Choose the most appropriate representation of charges accumulated near the junction S.
questions.solution:
When a current flows through a junction or a contact between two metals of different conductivities a charge is accumulated in the vicinity of contact. The charge accumulation is directly proportional to the current. Charge accumulation occurs in a region whose thickness is nearly equals to the mean free path in the metal.

In case current flows from a metal of large conductivity to a metal of small conductivity, charge accumulation is of positive type and vise-versa.

or ΔV∝ε

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical copper rod has length $\mathrm{L}$ and resistance $\mathrm{R}$. If it is melted and formed into another rod of length $2 \mathrm{~L}$, the resistance will be
questions.solution:
Applying volume conservation
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{A}^{\prime} \times 2 \mathrm{~L} \\
\mathrm{~A}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2} \\
\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{A}} \\
\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\frac{\rho \times 2 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~A}^{\prime}}=\frac{\rho \times 4 \mathrm{~L}}{\mathrm{~A}} \\
\mathrm{R}^{\prime}=4 \mathrm{R}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform metallic wire of lenght $L$ is mounted in two configurations. In configuration I (triangle), it is an equilateral triangle and a voltage $V$ is applied to corners A and B. In configuration 2 (circle), it is bent in the form of a circle, and the potential $v$ is applied at diameterically opposite points $P$ and $Q$. The ratio of the power dissipated in configuration 1 to configuration 2 is.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}}$ $\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}=\frac{\rho \frac{\ell}{3}}{\mathrm{~A}}\right)$ $\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ep}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}} \Rightarrow 2$ $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}_{1}}{3}=\frac{2 \rho \frac{\ell}{3}}{3 \mathrm{~A}}$ $\mathrm{i}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{2 \mathrm{e} \ell} 9 \mathrm{~A}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{R}}{2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\rho . \ell / 2}{\mathrm{~A}}\right)$ $\mathrm{i}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{V}}{\rho \ell} 4 \mathrm{~A}$ $\frac{\mathrm{i}_{1}}{\mathrm{i}_{2}}=\frac{9}{8}$

questions.q:
Consider the following statements regarding the photoelectric effect experiment:
(I) Photoelectrons are emitted as soon as the metal is exposed to light
(II) There is a minimum frequency below which no photo-current is observed
(III) The stopping potential is proportional to the frequency of light
(IV)The photo-current varies linearly with the intensity of the light
Which of the above statements indicate that light consists of quanta (photons) with energy proportional to frequency?
questions.solution: Statement I, II \& III are correct

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Using the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, arrange the following particles in the order of increasing lowest energy possible.
(I) An electron in H2 molecule
(II) A hydrogen atom in a H2, molecule
(III) A proton in the carbon nucleus
(IV) A H2 molecule within a nanotube
questions.solution:
H2 molecule in a nanotube is bound only by adhesive forces. So, its energy is least.

A hydrogen atom in a H2 molecule is bound by interatomic forces. So, its energy is less than the energy of an electron in H2 molecule.

Proton in a carbon nucleus is bound because of strong nuclear forces. So, its energy is largest.

∴ Correct order is IV<II<I<III

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photon of wavelength $\lambda$ is absorbed by an electron confined to a box of length $\sqrt{35 \mathrm{~h} \lambda / 8 \mathrm{mc}}$. As a result, the electron makes a transition from state $\mathrm{k}=1$ to the state $\mathrm{n}$. Subsequently the electron transits from the state $\mathrm{n}$ to the state $\mathrm{m}$ by emitting a photon of wavelength $\lambda^{\prime}=1.85 \lambda$. Then
questions.solution: $\mathrm{KE}$ of $\mathrm{e}^{-}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \lambda_{\text {de-broglie }}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mKE}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \times \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}}}=\frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{h} \lambda}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mc}}} \\ \frac{\mathrm{m}_{0} \lambda_{\text {debroglie }}}{2}=\ell \\ \frac{\mathrm{m}_{0}}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{h} \lambda}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mc}}}=\frac{\sqrt{35 \mathrm{~h} \lambda}}{\sqrt{8 \mathrm{mc}}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{m}_{0}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{35}}{2 \sqrt{2}} \\ \mathrm{~m}_{0}=\sqrt{35}=5.8 \approx 6 \end{array} $$ i.e. $\mathrm{e}^{-}$get excite to state 6 . $$ \therefore \mathrm{n}=6 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Photons of wavelength $\lambda$ are incident on a metal. The most energetic electrons ejected from the metal are bent into a circular arc of radius $\mathrm{R}$ by a perpendicular magnetic field having a magnitude $\mathrm{B}$. The work function of the metal is (Where symbols have their usual meanings) -
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{R} &=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}} \\ \mathrm{V} &=\frac{\mathrm{qBR}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{eBR}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}} \\ \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi=\mathrm{KE}_{\max } \text { (Einstein photo electric equation) } \\ \phi &=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\mathrm{KE}_{\max } \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{eBR}}{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right)^{2} \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{eBR}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{e}}}\right)^{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four electrons, each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> are in a one dimensional box of size <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. Assume that, the electrons are non-interacting, obey the Pauli exclusion principle and are described by standing de Broglie waves confined within the box. Define <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>h</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>/</mo><mn>8</mn><msub><mi>m</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>U</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> to be the ground state energy. Then,
options: the energy of the highest occupied state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mi>α</mi></math>
the total energy of the first excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>U</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>α</mi></math>
the total energy of the second excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>U</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>α</mi></math>
questions.solution:
For an electron described by standing waves confined within a box of length L, the wave can occupy only states for which 

L=nλ2, where n=1,2,3…

Now, by de Broglie wavelength of this electron, 

λ=hp=h2mE

From above two equations, we have 

En=h29mL2n2

(where, n=1,2,3…)

Now, ground state energy is obtained by substituting n=1.

∴ U0=E1=h28mL2

Energy of first excited state, 

E2=h28mL2×22

=4U0=U0+3α

Also, energy for second excited state, 

E3=h28mL2×32

=9U0=U0+8α

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron in an electron microscope with initial velocity $v_{0} \hat{i}$ enters a region of a stray transverse electric field $\mathrm{E}_{0} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$. The time taken for the change in its de-Broglie wavelength from the initial value of $\lambda$ to $\lambda / 3$ is proportional to
questions.solution: $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}$ $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}}$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{t}$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=0+\frac{\mathrm{qE}_{0}}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{t}$ $\left(3 \mathrm{v}_{0}\right)=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}=8 \mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{qE}_{0}}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{t}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{v}_{0}$ $\quad \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}_{0}} \mathrm{v}_{0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{E}_{0}}$

Energy of photon $=\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}$
Momentum of photon $=\mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda}$
$\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{PC} \quad \therefore \frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{C}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.solution: $\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}} & \mathrm{~N}=\frac{160 \times 6200 \times 10^{-10}}{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\ & =\frac{5 \times 10^{19} \times 10}{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.24} & =\frac{16 \times 62 \times 10^{-7}}{20 \times 10^{-34} \times 10^{8}} \\ & =\frac{5 \times 10^{19} \times 10}{4 \times 3.14 \times 3.24} & =\frac{496}{10} \times 10^{19} \\ & =1.25 \times 10^{19} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting bar of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and length $\ell$ moves on two frictionless parallel rails in the presence of a constant uniform magnetic field of magnitude B directed into the page as shown in the figure . The bar is given an initial velocity $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ towards the right at $\mathrm{t}=0$. Then the
options: Induced current in the circuit is in the clockwise direction
Velocity of the bar decreases linearly with time
Distance the bar travels before it comes to a complete stop is proportional to $\mathrm{R}$
Power generated across the resistance is proportional to $\ell$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{iB} \ell$
$\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{iB} \ell}{\mathrm{m}}$
$\phi=\mathrm{B} . \mathrm{A}$
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{B} \cdot \ell \cdot\left(\frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)$
$\varepsilon=(\mathrm{B} \cdot \ell \mathrm{v})$
$\mathrm{i}=\varepsilon / \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{B} \cdot \ell \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\mathrm{a}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{B} \ell \mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \frac{\mathrm{B} \ell}{\mathrm{m}}$
$\mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mathrm{Rm}} \cdot \mathrm{v}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{v} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{v} . \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mathrm{Rm}} \cdot \mathrm{v}$
$\Rightarrow \int \mathrm{dv}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mathrm{Rm}} \cdot \int \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$
$\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \ell^{2}}{\mathrm{Rm}} \cdot{ }^{\prime} \mathrm{X}^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{X}=\frac{\mathrm{vRM}}{\mathrm{B}^{\prime} \ell^{2}}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A square-shaped wire loop of mass $\mathrm{m}$, resistance $R$ and side 'a' moving with speed $v_{0}$, parallel to the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, enters a region of uniform magnetic field $\mathrm{B}$, which is perpendicular to the plane of the loop. The speed of the loop changes with distance $x(x < a)$ in the filed, as
questions.solution: $\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{i} \ell \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{vBa} \cdot \mathrm{Ba}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\mathrm{ma}=\frac{\mathrm{vB}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\frac{-\mathrm{mdv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{vB}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\Rightarrow-\mathrm{m} \frac{\mathrm{vdv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $-\mathrm{m} \int_{\mathrm{v}_{0}} \mathrm{dv}=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}} \int_{0}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}$ $-\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{v}-\mathrm{v}_{0}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\mathrm{v}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{a}^{2}}{\mathrm{mR}} \mathrm{x}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short solenoid (length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> and radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> turns per unit length) lies well inside and on the axis of a very long, coaxial solenoid (length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>, radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> turns per unit length, with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>></mo><mi>r</mi></math>). Current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> follows in the short solenoid. Choose the correct statement.
options: There is uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>n</mi><mi>I</mi></math> in the long solenoid.

Mutual inductance of the solenoids is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>n</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>l</mi></math>.

Flux through outer solenoid due to current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> in the inner solenoid is proportional to the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>r</mi></math>.

Mutual inductance of the solenoids is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>r</mi><mi>R</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>N</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>L</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
questions.solution:
We use reciprocatory theorem to calculate mutüal inductance of the system of solenoids.  

Let outer solenoid carries a current I1. Flux linked with small solenoid 2 is proportional to the current in large solenoid 1.

⇒ϕ21∝I1⇒nlμ0NI1πr2∝I1

Proportionality constant is the coefficient of mutual inductance M of system.

Note that M12=M21 in all cases.

So, n/μ0Nl1πr2=Ml1 or M=πr0r2nNI

questions.q: The intensity of light from a continuously emitting laser source operating at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>638</mn><mi>nm</mi></math> wavelength is modulated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>GHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The modulation is done by momentarily cutting the intensity off with a frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi>GHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the farthest distance apart two detectors can be placed in the line of the laser light, so that they can see the portions of the same pulse simultaneously? (Consider the speed of light in air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Time period between two flashes =1f
Distance travelled by laser in this interval
=cf=3×108109=0.3 m=30 cm

So this is the maximum distance between two detectors, so that they can see the same pulse simultaneously.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Imagine a world where free magnetic charges exist. In this world, a circuit is made with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math> shape wire and a rod free to slide on it. A current carried by free magnetic charges can flow in the circuit. When the circuit is placed in a uniform electric field, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> perpendicular to the plane (inward) of the circuit and the rod is pulled to the right with a constant speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the "magnetic EMF" in the current and the direction of the corresponding current, arising because of changing electric flux will be (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is the length of the rod and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is speed of light ).
questions.solution:
∮B→·dℓ→=μ0I+ε0dϕEdt

dϕEdt=vEℓ

∴∮B→·dℓ→=μ0ε0vEℓ⇒vEℓC2

Direction of electric field is not given in the question therefore both options are possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> carry positive charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>, respectively. They are dropped to the floor in a laboratory set up from the same height, where there is a constant electric field vertically upwards. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> hits the floor before <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Then,
questions.solution: \(\mathrm{Q}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~g}-\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}, \mathrm{Q}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~g}-\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}\) as \(\mathrm{S}=\mathrm{ut}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{at}^{2}\) So, \(\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{t}_{1}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}\right) \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2} \ldots \ldots \ldots(1)\) \(\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{a} \times \mathrm{t}_{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}\right) \mathrm{t}_{2}^{2} \ldots \ldots . .(2)\) given \(\mathrm{t}_{1}<\mathrm{t}_{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}<\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}\) \(\mathrm{So}, \frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}}<\frac{2 \mathrm{~h}}{\mathrm{~g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}}\) \(\mathrm{~g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}>\mathrm{g}-\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}\) \(\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}<\frac{\mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{M}_{2}}\) \(\Rightarrow \mathrm{Q}_{2} \mathrm{M}_{1}>\mathrm{Q}_{1} \mathrm{M}_{2}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electrostatic field line leaves at an angle α from point charge q1 and connects with point charge -q2 at an angle β(q1and q2 are positive) see figure below. If q2=32q1 and α=30°, then
questions.solution:
Consider another symmetric field line below line joining centres of charges q1 and q2

In given situation, flux (field lines) leaving charge q1 at a solid angle 2α= flux terminating over charge q2 at a solid angle 2β.
Clearly, in given situation

We can say that, flux leaving charge q1 through a cone of semi-vertical angle α= flux terminating on charge q2 through a cone of semi-vertical angle β.

To calculate flux, we first find flux through an elemental ring of base of cone and then we integrate to get total flux.

Area of elemental ring,
dA=2πrds=2πRsinα·Rdα

Flux through elemental ring E‖dA

dϕ=kQR2·2πR2sinαdα

Total flux through base of cone

ϕ=∫0αkQR2·2πR2sinαdα

=kQ2π∫0αsinαdα

ϕ=Q2ε0·1-cosα

So, equating flux of both cones, we get

q12ε01-cosα=q22ε01-cosβ

⇒q11-cosα=32q11-cosβ

Substituting α=30° in above equation, we get

⇒231-cos30°=1-cosβ

⇒231-32=1-cosβ

⇒cosβ=1-231-32

⇒cosβ≈0.9

So, angle β is not more than 30°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two mutually perpendicular infinitely long straight conductors carrying uniformly distributed charges of linear densities λ1 and λ2 are positioned at a distance r from each other.

Force between the conductors depends on r as
questions.solution:
Consider the arrangement given,

Force on a differentiable length element dl of conductor 2 due to field of conductor 1 is
dF=E1dq

where, E1= field of wire 1 at location of dl

This force can be resolved into components dFcosθ and dFsinθ

net force is sum of all dFcosθ components, whereas ΣdFsinθ=0.
So, net force on conductor 2 is
F=∫dF=∫E1dq

Here,
E1=2kλ1R=2kλ1

dq=λ2dl

lr=tanθ and rR=cosθ

⇒dl=rsec2θdθ

Also, θ varies from -π2 to π2 for conductor 2.

So, F=∫-π/2π/22kλ1rsecθ·λ2·rsec2θdθ
=2kλ1λ2∫-π/2π/2secθdθ

As, ∫-π/2π/2secθdθ=a constant

We can say that,

Fnet=C·λ1λ2, where C= some constant.

∴Fnet∝r0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of gravitational force and electrostatic repulsive force between two electrons is approximately (gravitational constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>11</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>kg</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>, mass of an electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>31</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge on an electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>)
questions.solution:
Gravitational force FG=Gm1m2r2

Electrostatic force Fe=14πε0q1q2r2

∴ FGFe=Gm1m2·4πϵ0q1q2

=6.7×10-11×9.1×10-31×9.1×10-311.6×10-19×1.6×10-19×9×109

=24×10-44

Consider a sphere of radius R with unform charged density and total charge Q. The electrostatic potential distribution inside the sphere is given by
ϕ(r)=Q4πε0Ra+b(r/R)c. Note that the zero of potential is at infinity The values of (a, b, c) are:

Compare with given formula, i.e,

a=32, b=-12,c=2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If some charge is given to a solid metallic sphere, the field inside remains zero and by Gauss's law all the charge resides on the surface. Suppose now that Colomb's force between two charges varies as $1 / \mathrm{r}^{3}$. Then, for a charged solid metallic sphere
options: field inside will be zero and charge density inside will be zero.
field inside will not be zero and charge density inside will not be zero.
field inside will not be zero and charge density inside will be zero.
field inside will be zero and charge density inside will not be zero.
questions.solution:
If coloumb's force $\propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}$ gauss's law is not valid $\therefore \phi \neq \frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
For static condition $\mathrm{E}=0$ in both of conductor $\therefore \phi$ through a Gaussian surface just under the surface of conductor $=0$ but as $\phi=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$ is not valid
So $\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{en}}=0$ is not correct statement. Some charge will present insider bulk of conductor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Positive point charges are placed at the vertices of a star shape as shown in the figure. Direction of the electrostatic force on a negative point charge at the centre $\mathrm{O}$ of the star is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Let the electrostatic field $\mathrm{E}$ at distance $\mathrm{r}$ from a point charge $q$ not be an inverse square but, instead an inverse cubic, e.g. $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=\mathrm{k} \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}} \hat{\mathrm{r}}$
Here $\mathrm{k}$ is a constant. Consider the following two statements
(i) Flux through a spherical surface enclosing the charge is $\phi=q_{\text {enclosed }} / \in_{0}$
(ii) A charge placed inside uniformly charged shell will experience a force.
Choose the correct option.
questions.solution:
$|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}|=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}}$ $\therefore \mathrm{d} \phi=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}$ $\phi=\int \mathrm{d} \phi=\int \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{r}^{3}} \cdot 4 \pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$ $\phi=\frac{\mathrm{kq} 4 \pi}{\mathrm{r}} \neq \frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{en}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
force on $q^{\prime}$ is zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A total charge $q$ is divided as $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ which are kept at two of the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a. The magnitude of the electric field $E$ at the third vertex of the triangle is to be depicted schematically as a function of $x=q_{1} / q$. Choose the correct figure.
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l}E_{n e t}=\vec{E}_{1}+\vec{E}_{2} \\ q=q_{1}+q_{2} \\ \left|\vec{E}_{\text {net }}\right|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{k q_{1}}{a}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{k q_{2}}{a}\right)^{2}+\frac{2 k^{2} q_{1} q_{2}}{a^{2}} \cdot \cos 60^{\circ}} \\ E_{n e t}=\frac{k}{a} \sqrt{q_{1}^{2}+q_{2}^{2}+q_{1} q_{2}} \\ E_{n e t}=\frac{k}{a} \sqrt{q_{1}^{2}+\left(q-q_{1}\right)^{2}+q_{1}\left(q-q_{1}\right)} \\ \end{array}$ $\begin{aligned} &=\frac{k}{a q} \times \sqrt{\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q}\right)^{2}+1^{2}+\left(\frac{q_{1}}{q}\right)^{2}-\frac{2 q_{1}}{q}+\frac{q_{1}}{q}-\frac{q_{1}^{2}}{q^{2}}} \\ &=\frac{k}{a q} \sqrt{x^{2}+1-x} \\ E_{n e t} &=\frac{k}{a q}\left(\sqrt{(x-1 / 2)^{2}+3 / 4}\right) \end{aligned}$ Minima must be at $|\mathrm{x}=1 / 2|$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four identical pendulums are made by made by attaching a small ball of mass $100 \mathrm{~g}$ on a $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ long thread and suspended from the same point. Now each ball is given charge $Q$ so that balls move away from each other with each thread making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ from the vertical. The value of $Q$ is close to $\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=9 \times 10^{9}\right.$ in SI units).
questions.solution: From the diagram, \(\mathrm{OA}=\mathrm{L} / \sqrt{2}\) $\Longrightarrow \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{BC}=\mathrm{L}$ $\mathrm{AD}=\mathrm{L} \sqrt{2}$ Net electrical force on charge \(A\) is: $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AD}}+\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AB}} \cos 45^{\circ}+\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{AC}} \cos 45^{\circ} \\ \mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}} \times 2 \mathrm{~L}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{L}^{2} \sqrt{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{Q}^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{L}^{2} \sqrt{2}} \\ \mathrm{~F}=\frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{Q}^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}} \mathrm{L}^{2}}(2 \sqrt{2}+1) \end{array}$ Perpendicular to the string, net force is zero. \(\mathrm{F} \sin 45^{\circ}=\mathrm{mg} \sin 45^{\circ}\) $\begin{array}{l} \frac{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{Q}^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~L}^{2}}(2 \sqrt{2}+1)=\mathrm{mg} \\ \mathrm{Q}=1.61 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics

questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A} \Rightarrow$ If stream lines intersect then there will be two direction of fluid flow at a point, which is absurd.
$\mathrm{B} \Rightarrow$ Lines of forces in electrostatic never intersect
$\mathrm{D} \Rightarrow$ Line of force in magnetism never intersect each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a cube of uniform charge density $\rho$. The ratio of electrostatic potential at the centre of the cube to that at one of the corners of the cube is
questions.solution:
Let at the corner of cube potential $=\mathrm{V}_{0}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \text { Potential } \propto \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\text { Side of cube }} \\ \mathrm{Q}=\rho \times \mathrm{a}^{3} \end{array} $$ So potential $\propto \frac{\rho \mathrm{a}^{3}}{\mathrm{a}}$ Potential $\propto \mathrm{a}^{2}$
Big cube consist of 8 cube At centre of big cube of side $2 \mathrm{a}$, potential is $8 \mathrm{~V}_{0}$ Potential at corner of big cube $=\mathrm{V}_{0} \times(2)^{2}=4 \mathrm{~V}_{0}$ Required ratio $=\frac{8 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}{4 \mathrm{~V}_{0}}=2: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius of a nucleus is given by $\mathrm{r}_{0} \mathrm{~A}^{1 / 3}$ where $\mathrm{r}_{0}=1.3 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{A}$ is the mass number of the nucleus, the Lead nucleus has $\mathrm{A}=206$. the electrostatic force between two protons in this nucleus is approximately
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r}_{0} \mathrm{~A}^{1 / 3} \quad & \mathrm{r}_{0}=1.3 \times 10^{-15} \\ & \mathrm{~F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \in_{0}} \times \frac{\mathrm{q}_{1} \mathrm{q}_{2}}{\mathrm{r}^{2}} \end{aligned}$
$F=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{\mathrm{r}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{~A}^{2 / 3}}$ $\mathrm{~F}=\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{(1.3)^{2} \times 10^{-30} \times(206)^{2 / 3}}$ $=\frac{23.04 \times 10^{39} \times 10^{-38}}{(1.69) \times 34.81}$ $=\frac{23.04 \times 10}{1.69 \times 34.81}$ $=3.91 \mathrm{Newton}$ $=0.039 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{Ans} .(\mathrm{A})$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric field due to a positively charged long straight wire at a distance $\mathrm{r}$ from it is proportional to $\mathrm{r}^{-1}$ in magnitude. Two electrons are orbiting such a long straight wire in circular orbits of radii $1 Å$ and $2 Å$. The ratio of their respective time periods is
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} E=\frac{K}{r} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qE} \end{array}$ for $1^{\text {s }}$ electron. $\begin{array}{l} \text { (q) } \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}_{1}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}_{1}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{q}=\mathrm{mv}_{1}^{2} \\ \mathrm{v}_{1}{ }^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{M}} \\ \mathrm{v}_{1}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{M}}} \end{array}$ $\therefore \quad$ Similarly $\mathrm{v}_{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{M}}}$ $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{v}_{1}=\mathrm{v}_{2} \\ \frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}=\frac{\frac{2 \mathrm{nR}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}}}{\frac{2 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{R}_{2}}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{nR}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}} \times \frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{2 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{R}_{2}} \\ =\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{1}} \times \frac{\mathrm{v}_{2}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point charge $Q=\left(=3 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}\right)$ rotates uniformly in a vertical circle of radius $\mathrm{R}=1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The axis of the circle is aligned along the magnetic axis of the earth. At what value of the angular speed $\omega$, the effective magnetic field at the center of the circle will be reduced to zero? (Horizontal component of Earth's magnetic field is 30 micro Tesla)
questions.solution:
$$
\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 R} \cdot\left(\frac{\omega \cdot q}{2 \pi}\right)=30 \times 10^{-6}
$$
So $\omega=10^{11} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a spherical shell of radius $\mathrm{R}$ with a total charge $+\mathrm{q}$ uniformly spread on its surface (center of the chell lies at the origin $x=0)$. Two point charges, $+q$ and $-q$ are brought, one after the other, from far away and placed at $x=-a / 2$ and $x=+a / 2(a < R)$, respectively. Magnitude of the work done in this process is :
questions.solution: $\mathrm{W}_{1}=\mathrm{q}\left(\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)$ $\mathrm{W}_{2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{KQ}}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{a}}\right)(-\mathrm{q})$ $\mathrm{W}=\frac{-\mathrm{kq}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two possitively charged spheres of masses $m_{1}$, and $m_{2}$, are suspended from a common point at the ceiling by identical insulating massless strings of length I. Charges on the two spheres are $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$, respectively. At equilivrium both strings make the same angle $\theta$ with the vertical. Then
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} \tan \theta=\frac{F}{m g} \quad(F \rightarrow \text { same }) \\ \tan \theta \alpha \frac{1}{m} \\ \therefore m_{1}=m_{2} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential due to an electrostatic charge distribution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>q</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is positive. The net charge within a sphere centred at the origin and of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mi>α</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Electric field due to given charge distribution is

E=-dVdr=-ddrqe-αr4πε0r

=-q4πε0ddre-αrr

=-q4πε0-αre-αr-e-αrr2

=q4πε0·e-αr·αr+1r2

Electric field at r=1α is

Er=1α=q4πε0·e-α×1αα×1α+11/α2

=q/e4πε0·2α2

Flux through a sphere of radius 1α is

ϕ=∫E·dA

For spherical distribution,
E·dA=EdAcos0°=EdA
and E= uniform.
So, we have

∴ϕ=E∫dA

=q/e4πε0·2α2·4π1α2

or ϕ=2qε0e

From Gauss' law, we have ϕ=qenclosed ε0

So, charge enclosed in given sphere is 2qe

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a particle accelerator, a current of 500 μA is carried by a proton beam in which each proton has a speed of 3×107 m/s. The cross-sectional area of the beam is 1.50 mm2. The charge density in this beam (in C/m3 is close to
questions.solution:
If Q is charge contained in L

length of beam of area A, then

L×A×ρ=Q

where, ρ= charge density of beam.

 

So,  ρ=QL×A=Q/tL/t×A=Iv×A

 

=500×10-63×107×1.50×10-6

 

=53×1.5×10-5=11×10-5 Cm-3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One can define an alpha-volt (α-V) to be the energy acquired by an α -particle when it is accelerated by a potential of 1 V. For this problem, you may take a proton to be 2000 times heavier than an electron. Then,
questions.solution:
An alpha-volt (α-V) is the energy acquired by an α -particle (charge2 units) when accelerated by a potential difference of 1 V.

∴  1α-V=q (ΔV).

=2e×1 V=2 eV

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>q</mi></math> is distributed over a thin ring of radius $r$ with line charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>q</mi><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>/</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. Note that the ring is in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mi>Y</mi></math>-plane and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is the angle made by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> with the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis. The work done by the electric force in displacing a point charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>Q</mi></math> from the centre of the ring to infinity is
options: equal to zero only, if the path is a straight line perpendicular to the plane of the ring
questions.solution:
As charge distribution is over a circle, potential due to charge over ring is 

V=kr.qtotal

where, qtotal =∫02πqsin2θπrrdθ

=qπ∫02π1-cos2θ2.dθ

=qπθ2-sin2θ402π=q

So, potential at centre of ring =V

=kqtotalr

⇒V=kqr

Also, work done in taking a charge Q from centre of ring to infinity = potential energy of system

=V·Q=kqQr=qQ4πε0r

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider an initially neutral hollow conducting spherical shell with inner radius r and outer radius $2 \mathrm{r}$. A point charge $+\mathrm{Q}$ is now placed inside the shell at a distance $r / 2$ from the centre. The shell is then grounded by connecting the outer surface to the earth. $P$ is an external point at a distance $2 r$ from the point charge $+Q$ on the line passing through the centre and the point charge $+Q$ as shown in the figure.
The magnitude of the force on a test charge $+q$ placed at $\mathrm{P}$ will be
questions.solution: Charge on outer most surface is zero Hence force on $\mathrm{q}$ is also ' 0 '

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron with an initial speed of $4.0 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ is brought to rest by an electric field. The mass and charge of an electron are $9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$, respectively. Identify the correct statement
options: The electron moves from a region of lower potential to higher potential through a potential difference of $11.4 ~\mu \mathrm{V}$.
The electron moves from a region of higher potential to lower potential through a potential difference of $11.4 ~\mu \mathrm{V}$.
The electron moves from a region of lower potential to higher potential through a potential difference of $45\mathrm{~V}$.
The electron moves from a region of higher potential to lower potential through a potential difference of $45\mathrm{~V}$.
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
q V=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} \\
V=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m v^{2}}{q} \\
V=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9 \times 10^{-31} \times\left(4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=45 V
\end{array}$
$45 \mathrm{~V}$ from higher to lower potential.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> carry positive charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. They are dropped to the floor in a laboratory set up from the same height, where there is a constant electric field vertically upwards. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> hits the floor before <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Then,
questions.solution:
Time of fall =2haret 

Net acceleration of charged masses is
anet=g-qEm
As, M1 hits the floor before M2

⇒  2ha1>2ha2

⇒  1a1>1a2

⇒  a2>a1

When reciprocal is taken in equality sign is reversed, then.
g-Q1EM1>g-Q2EM2

⇒  -Q1EM1>-Q2EM2

⇒  Q1EM1<Q2EM2

Here, multiplication with - 1reverse sign of inequality.

  So,   Q1M1<Q2M2

or M2Q1<M1Q2

⇒  M1Q2>M2Q1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given below are three schematic graphs of potential energy $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{r})$ versus distance $\mathrm{r}$ for three atomic particles: electron $\left(\mathrm{e}^{-}\right)$, proton $\left(\mathrm{p}^{+}\right)$and neutron $(\mathrm{n})$, in the presence of a nucleus at the origin $\mathrm{O}$. The radius of the nucleus is $\mathrm{r}_{0}$. The scale on the V-axis may not be the same for all figures. The correct pairing of each graph with the corresponding atomic particle is -
questions.solution: outside the nucleus electric potential decreases $\mathrm{e}^{-}$is negativity charged $\therefore$ its $\mathrm{PE}$ is negative even outside the nucleus where nuclear attractive force is negligible $(3) \rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{-}$ outside the nucleus neutron will not experience electric force as it is neutral. So no potential energy associated with it outside nucleus. $1 \rightarrow$ neutron

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An isolated sphere of radius $R$ contains uniform volume distribution of positive charge. Which of the curves shown below correctly illustrates the dependence of the magnitude of the electric field of the sphere as a function of the distance $r$ from its centre ?
questions.solution: When $\mathrm{r} < \mathrm{R}$ $\mathrm{E}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{r}}{3 \in_{0}}$ When $r>R$ $\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{4 \pi \in_{0} \mathrm{r}^{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider three concentric metallic spheres A, B and C of radii a, b, c respectively where a $ < \mathrm{b} < \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are connected whereas $\mathrm{C}$ is grounded. The potential of the middle sphere $\mathrm{B}$ is raised to $\mathrm{V}$ then the charge on the sphere C is-
questions.solution: $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{b}}+\frac{\mathrm{k}(-\mathrm{q})}{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{V} \text { (Given) }$ $\mathrm{q}=\frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \cdot \mathrm{bc}}{\mathrm{c}-\mathrm{b}} \cdot \mathrm{V}$ Charge on $\mathrm{C}=-\mathrm{q}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At a distance $\ell$ from a uniformly charged long wire, a charged particle is thrown radially outward with a velocity $\mathrm{u}$ in the direction perpendicular to the wire. When the particle reaches a distance $2 \ell$ from the wire its speed is found to be $\sqrt{2} \mathrm{u}$. The magnitude of the velocity, when it is a distance $4 \ell$ away from the wire, is (ignore gravity)
questions.solution: energy conservation at A \& B $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{qV}_{\Lambda}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\mathrm{qV}_{\mathrm{B}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \times 2 \mathrm{u}^{2} \\ \mathrm{q}\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}\right]=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2} \\ \mathrm{q} \times \frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \in_{0}} \ln 2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2} \end{array}$ energy conservation at $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{C}$ $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{qV}_{\mathrm{A}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\mathrm{q} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \\ \mathrm{q}\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right]+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \\ \frac{\mathrm{q} \lambda}{2 \pi \epsilon_{0}} \ln 4+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \\ \frac{2 \mathrm{q} \lambda}{2 \pi \in_{0}} \ln 2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \\ \mathrm{mu}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mu}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2} \\ \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{u}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{v}^{2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{u} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of charge $\mathrm{q}$ and mass $\mathrm{m}$ enters a region of a transverse electric field of $\mathrm{E}_{0} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ with initial velocity $v_{0} \hat{i}$. The time taken for the change in the de Broglie wavelength of the charge from the initial value of $\lambda_{0}$ to $\lambda_{0} / 3$ is proportional to
questions.solution: $\lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}$ $\mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}}$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{y}}+\mathrm{a}_{y} \mathrm{t}$ $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}=0+\frac{\mathrm{qE}_{0}}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{t}$ $\left(3 \mathrm{v}_{0}\right)=\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}+\mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}^{2}=8 \mathrm{v}_{0}^{2}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{t}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{v}_{0}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~m}}{\mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}_{0}} \mathrm{v}_{0}$ $\mathrm{t} \propto \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{q}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical particles of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' and charge $\mathrm{q}$ are shot at each other from a very great distance with an initial speed $\mathrm{v}$. The distance of closest approach of these charges is-
questions.solution:
Using law of conservation of mechanical energy
Initial K.E. = Final P.E.
$$
\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{q}^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{mv}^{2}}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three equal charges $+\mathrm{q}$ are placed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle centered at the origin. They are held in equilibrium by a restoring force of magnitude $F(r)=k r$ directed towards the origin, where $k$ is a constant. What is the distance of the three charges from the origin?
questions.solution: $$ F(r)=k T $$ now $F_{\text {net }}$ on a particle is $2 F_{q} \cos 30^{\circ}$ due to the other two charges $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{kq}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} $$ also $r=\frac{2}{3}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a\right)$ $\therefore \mathrm{a}=\sqrt{3} \mathrm{r}$ replacing it in $\mathrm{F}_{\text {nat }}$ we get $$ \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{kq}^{2}}{(\sqrt{3} \mathrm{r})^{2}} \times\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{kq}^{2}}{\sqrt{3} \mathrm{r}^{2}} $$ this is balanced by $\mathrm{F}(\mathrm{r})$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \therefore F(r)=F_{n e t} \Rightarrow k r=\frac{1 \times q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} \times \sqrt{3} r^{2}} \\ \therefore r=\left(\frac{\sqrt{3} q^{2}}{12 \pi \varepsilon_{0} k}\right)^{1 / 3} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A specially designed Vernier calliper has the main scale least count of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. On the Vernier scale, there are 10 equal divisions and they match with 11 main scale divisions. Then, the least count of the Vernier calliper is
questions.solution:
Here, 10 divisions of vernier scall
=11 main scale divisions So, 1 vernier scale division =1110 main scale divisions

Now, we use formula for least count, Least count =1 main scale division -1 vernier scale division.


⇒LC=1MSD−1VSD

=1−1110MSD

=−110MSD

=−110×1mm

=−01mm

So, magnitude of least count is 0.1 mm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A star of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is made up of gases. The average gravitational pressure compressing the star due to gravitational pull of the gases making up the star depends on $R$ as
questions.solution:
Consider a spherical shell of radius $\mathrm{r}$ and radial thickness dr. $P \& P+d P$ are pressure at its inner and outer surface. Let $\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{r}}=$ gravitational acceleration at distance $\mathrm{r}( < \mathrm{R})$ $\sum_{\downarrow} \mathrm{P}_{0}=$ Atmospheric pressure
For equilibrium of this shell - $$ \begin{array}{l} (P+d P)\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)+\left(4 \pi r^{2} d r\right) \rho g_{r}=(P)\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right) \\ \left\{\rho=\frac{3 M}{4 \pi R^{3}}=\right.\text { Density of sphere } \\ \Rightarrow \quad d P=-\rho g_{r} d r \\ \because \quad g_{r}=\frac{4}{3} \pi \text { Gpr } \\ \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{dP}=-\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{G} \rho^{2} r d r \end{array} $$ $\{(-)$ ve sign indicates that pressure is decreasing with radius $\}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \int_{P}^{P_{0}} d P=\int_{r}^{R}-\frac{4}{3} \pi G \rho^{2} r d r \\ \Rightarrow & P_{0}-P=-\frac{4}{3} \pi G \rho^{2}\left(\frac{R^{2}}{2}-\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & P=P_{0}+\frac{4}{3} \pi G \rho^{2}\left(\frac{R^{2}}{2}-\frac{r^{2}}{2}\right) \\ & P=P_{0}+\frac{3 G M^{2}}{8 \pi R^{4}}\left(1-\frac{r^{2}}{R^{2}}\right) \end{array} $$ Hence $P \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{4}}$ $\Rightarrow$ Average pressure will also be proportional to $\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{4}}$ $\Rightarrow$ correct answer is $[\mathrm{A}]$
