questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photon falls through a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> through the earth's gravitational field. To calculate the change in its frequency, take its mass to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mi>v</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The fractional change in frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> is close to
questions.solution:
A photon of mass m and frequency v falls through a height H through the earth's gravitational field as shown in figure below. 

When the photon falls through the earth's gravitational field, then it is gains extra energy. Therefore,

Final photon energy = Initial photon energy + Increase in energy 

⇒hv'=hv+mgH

=hv+hvc2.gH=hv1+gHc2

So, f'-ff= fractional change in frequency =gHc2=10×10003×1082=109×10-13.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles of identical mass are moving in circular orbits under a potential given by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{r})=\mathrm{Kr}^{\mathrm{n}}$, where $\mathrm{K}$ is a constant. If the radii of their orbits are $r_{1}, r_{2}$ and their speeds are $v_{1}, v_{2}$, respectively, then
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{r})=\mathrm{Kr}^{-\mathrm{n}} \\
\text { gravitational field }=\mathrm{E}=-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{dr}} \\
=(-\mathrm{K}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dr}}\left(\mathrm{r}^{-\mathrm{n}}\right) \\
=(-\mathrm{K})(-\mathrm{n}) \mathrm{r}^{-\mathrm{n}-1} \\
=\frac{\mathrm{Kn}}{\mathrm{r}^{\mathrm{n}+1}}
\end{array}$
force on mass $=\mathrm{E} \times \mathrm{M}$, where $\mathrm{M}=$ mass of body
$\begin{array}{l}
\therefore \quad M E_{1}=\frac{M V_{1}^{2}}{r_{1}} \quad M E_{2}=\frac{M V_{2}^{2}}{r_{2}} \\
\therefore \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{V_{2}^{2}}=\frac{r_{1} E_{1}}{r_{2} E_{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{V_{2}^{2}}=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}} \frac{K n}{r_{1}^{n+1}} \frac{r_{2}^{n+1}}{K n} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{V_{2}^{2}}=\frac{r_{2}^{n}}{r_{1}^{n}} \\
\Rightarrow V_{1}^{2} r_{1}^{n}=V_{2}^{2} r_{2}^{n}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two satellites $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are revolving around a planet in the opposite sense in coplanar circular concentric orbits. At time $\mathrm{t}=0$, the satellites are farthest apart. The periods of revolution of $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are $3 \mathrm{~h}$ and $24 \mathrm{~h}$ respectively. The radius of the orbit of $S_{1}$ is $3 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km}$. Then the orbital speed of $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ as observed from
options: the planet is $4 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ when $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ is closest from $\mathrm{S}_{1}$.
the planet is $2 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$ when $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ is closest from $\mathrm{S}_{1}$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{T}^{2} \propto \mathrm{r}^{3}$
$\begin{array}{l} \frac{9}{24^{2}}=\left(\frac{3 \times 10^{4}}{r^{3}}\right)^{3} \\ \frac{3 \times 3}{24 \times 24}=\left(\frac{3 \times 10^{4}}{r}\right)^{3} \\ \frac{1}{4}=\frac{3 \times 10^{4}}{r} \\ r=12 \times 10^{4} \end{array}$ orbital speed of $S_{2}$ seen from planet $=\omega_{2} r$ $\begin{array}{l} =\frac{2 \pi}{24} \times 12 \times 10^{4} \\ =\pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1} \\ \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\omega_{1} \mathrm{r}_{1}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \times 3 \times 10^{4} \\ \Rightarrow 2 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1} \end{array}$
$\left(\frac{2 \pi}{3}+\frac{2 \pi}{24}\right) \mathrm{t}=\pi$ $\frac{9 \mathrm{t}}{24}=\frac{1}{2}$ $\mathrm{t}=\frac{12}{9} \mathrm{hr}$ Angle rotate by both satellite $\begin{array}{l} \theta_{1}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \times \frac{12}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{8 \pi}{9} \\ \theta_{2}=\frac{2 \pi}{24} \times \frac{12}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{\pi}{9} \end{array}$ velocity of $S_{2}$ seen from $S_{1}=V_{1}+V_{2}$ $$ =3 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is launched from the top of Mt. Everest which is at elevation of $9000 \mathrm{~m}$. The ball moves in circular orbit around earth. Acceleration due to gravity near the earth's surface is $g$. The magnitude of the ball's acceleration while in orbit is
questions.solution: $\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=m g^{\prime}$ (where $\mathrm{g}$ ' is nearly equal to $\mathrm{g}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spiral galaxy can be approximated as an infinitesimally thin disc of a uniform surface mass density (mass per unit area) located at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> Two stars <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> start from rest from heights <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo><</mo><mo><</mo></math> radial extent of the disc), respectively and fall towards the disc, cross over to the other side and execute periodic oscillations. The ratio of time periods of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Spiral galaxies consists of flat rotating disc containing stars, gas and dust.

Now, we thought of spiral galaxy in form of a disc.

Gravitational intensity at distance r$r$ over axis is

Ig=2GMa21-rr2+a21/2

=2GMa21-11+a2r21/2

=2GMa21-1+a2r2-12

Using binomial approximation, we have
Ig≈2GMa21-1+12a2r2≈2GMr2

So, forces on planet A and B are
FA=2GMmA4z02,FB=2GMmBz02

Accelerations of A and B respectively, are
aA=GM2z02 and aB=2GMz02

If TA and TB are time periods of oscillations of A and B, then

TATB=aBaA [we use T∝1a as in case of a linear oscillator]

=41⇒TATB=2

questions.q: A comet (assumed to be in an elliptical orbit around the sun) is at a distance of $0.4 \mathrm{AU}$ from the sun at the per ihelion. If the time period of the comet is 125 years, what is the aphelion distance ? AU : Astronomical Unit.
questions.solution: $$ \therefore r=\frac{0.4+y}{2} $$ also $\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_{1}}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}_{1}}{\mathrm{r}_{2}}\right)^{3}$ by Kepler's law of time- periods $\left(\mathrm{T}_{1}, \mathrm{r}_{1}\right.$ are taken for earth) $$ \therefore\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{y}}{125 \mathrm{y}}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1 \mathrm{AU}}{\left(\frac{0.4+\mathrm{y}}{2}\right) \mathrm{AU}}\right)^{3} $$ ving we get $\mathrm{y}=49.6 \mathrm{AU}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A star of mass $M$ (equal to the solar mass) with a planet (much smaller than the star) revolves around the star in a circular orbit. The velocity of the star with respect to the center of mass of the star-planet system is shown below:
The radius of the planet's orbit is closest to (1 A. U. Earth-Sun distance)
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{T}^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{\mathrm{GM}} \mathrm{a}^{2}$
$\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{\mathrm{GM}}=1$
$\mathrm{~T}=\mathrm{in}$ year
$\mathrm{a}=$ radius in A.U.
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=3$ days $=\frac{3}{365}$ year
$\therefore \mathrm{a}=\left(\frac{3}{365}\right)^{2 / 3}$
$\mathrm{a}=0.04 \mathrm{A.U}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A planet is orbiting the sun in an elliptical orbit. Let $\mathrm{U}$ denote the potential energy and $\mathrm{K}$ denote the kinetic energy of the planet at an arbitrary point on the orbit. Choose the correct statement.
options: $K=U \mid$ for two positions of the planet in the orbit.
questions.solution: Total energy must be less than zero and as potential energy is negative so answer is $\mathbf{A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> above the surface of earth, the value of acceleration due to gravity is the same as that of a particular depth below the surface of earth. Assuming uniform mass density of the earth, the depth is,
questions.solution:
At a height h

gh=g01-2hR

when h(10 km)<R(6400 km)

at a depth d

gd=g01-dR

here g0=GMR2

Now gh=gd

⇒d=2h

d=20 km

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Six objects are placed at the vertices of a regular hexagon. The geometric center of the hexagon is at the origin with objects 1 and 4 on the $x$-axis (see figure). The mass of the $k^{\text {th }}$ object is $m k=k M\left|\cos q_{k}\right|$ where i is an integer, $M$ is a constant with dimension of mass, and $q_{k}$ is the angular position of the kth verted measured from the positive $x$-axis in the counter-clockwise sense. If the net gravitational force on a body at the centroid vanishes, the value of $i$ is
questions.solution:
For Gravitational equilibrium $\left(\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{Net}}\right) \Rightarrow 0$ All Diagonal opposite should have equal mass $2 i^{\circ} \mathrm{M} \cos 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{i}^{\circ} \mathrm{M} \cos \left(60^{\circ}+180^{\circ}\right)$
Thus i $=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the axis of rotation of the earth were extended into space then it would pass close to
options: the centre of mass of all the planets in the solar system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small asteroid is orbiting around the sun in a circular orbit of radius $\mathrm{r}_{0}$ with speed $\mathrm{V}_{0}$. A rocket is launched from the asteroid with speed $\mathrm{V}=\alpha \mathrm{V}_{0}$, where $\mathrm{V}$ is the speed relative to the sun. The highest value of $\alpha$ for which the rocket will remain bound to the solar system is (ignoring gravity due to the asteroid and effects of other planets) -
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { B.E }=\frac{G m M}{2 R} \\
\text { B.E }=\frac{1}{2} m v_{0}^{2} \\
\text { so, } \alpha=1
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A certain planet completes one rotation about its axis in time $T$. The weight of an object placed at the equator on the planet's surface is a fraction $f\left(f\right.$ is close to unity) of its weight recorded at a latitude of $60^{\circ}$. The density of the planet (assumed to be a uniform perfect sphere is given by-
questions.solution:
$\quad \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{r}}}$ also $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{v}}$ or $\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{r}^{3}}{\mathrm{GM}}}$ when $\mathrm{v}$ is replaced by $\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{r}}}$ now $g_{e f f}=g-\omega^{2} R_{e} \cos ^{2} \phi$
now $f=\frac{g-\omega^{2} R_{e} \cos 0^{\circ}}{g-\omega^{2} R_{e} \cos 60^{\circ}}$
ving we get $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{4 g(\mathrm{f}-1)}{\omega^{2}(\mathrm{f}-4)}$ or $\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}^{2}}(\mathrm{f}-1)=\frac{\omega^{2} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}}{4}(\mathrm{f}-4)$
$$
\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e}}{ }^{3}=\frac{4 \mathrm{GM}}{\omega^{2}} \frac{(\mathrm{f}-1)}{(\mathrm{f}-4)}
$$
now $\rho=\frac{M}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_{e}^{3}}=\frac{3}{16} \frac{\omega^{2}}{\pi G} \frac{(f-4)}{(f-1)}$
also $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega} \quad \therefore \mathrm{p}=\frac{3 \pi(\mathrm{f}-4)}{4 \mathrm{~T}^{2} \mathrm{G}(\mathrm{f}-1)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The state of an ideal gas was changed isobarically. The graph depicts three such isobaric lines. Which of the following is true about the pressures of the gas?
questions.solution:
For constant temperature $P \propto \frac{1}{V}$
$$
\mathrm{V}_{3}>\mathbf{V}_{2}>\mathbf{V}_{1}
$$
so $\mathrm{P}_{3} < \mathrm{P}_{2} < \mathrm{P}_{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider the following statements for air molecules in an air tight container.
(I) the average speed of molecules is larger than root mean square speed
(II) mean free path of molecules is larger than the mean distance between molecules
(III) mean free path of molecules increases with temperature
(IV) the rms speed of nitrogen molecule is smaller than oxygen molecule The true statements are :
questions.solution: For ideal gas mean free path of molecules is larger than mean distance between molecules

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long cylindrical pipe of radius $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ is closed at its upper end and has an airtight piston of negligible mass as shown. When a $50 \mathrm{Kg}$ mass is attached to the other end of the pistion, it moves down by a distance $\Delta /$ before coming to equilibrium. Assuming air to be an ideal gas, $\Delta / \mathrm{L}$ (see figure) is close to $\left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{2}\right.$, atmospheric pressure is $10^{5}$ Pascal),
questions.solution: Process is isothermal $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}=\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2} \\ \left(\mathrm{P}_{0}\right)(\mathrm{A})(\ell) \Rightarrow \mathrm{PA}(\ell+\Delta \ell) \\ \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{final}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}_{0} \mathrm{~A} \ell}{\mathrm{A}(\ell+\Delta \ell)} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{P}_{0} \ell}{\ell+\Delta \ell} \end{array}$ By force equilibrium $\begin{array}{l} \left(P_{0}-P\right) A \Rightarrow m g \\ \left(P_{0}-\frac{P_{0} \ell}{\ell+\Delta \ell}\right) A=m g \\ \frac{\Delta \ell}{\ell} \approx 0.04 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas undergoes a circular cycle centered at $4 \mathrm{~atm}, 4$ lit as shown in the diagram. The maximum temperature attained in this process is close to
questions.solution:
T Will be maximum when product of P.V will be maximum
$$
\mathrm{T}=\frac{P V}{n R}=\frac{\left(4+2 \sin 45^{\circ}\right)\left(4+2 \cos 45^{\circ}\right)}{n R}
$$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{30}{n R} \times 100$ so either A or Bonus

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas filled in a cylinder occupies volume $\mathrm{V}$. The gas is compressed isothermally to the volume $\mathrm{V} / 3 .$ Now the cylinder valve is opened and the gas is allowed to leak keeping temperature same. What percentage of the number of molecules escape to bring the pressure in the cylinder back to its original value.
questions.solution:
$\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{n_{1}}=\frac{P_{2} V_{2}}{n_{2}}$
$\frac{P_{1} V}{n_{1}}=\frac{P_{1} \cdot \frac{V}{3}}{n_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}=\frac{n_{1}}{3}$
Now, $\frac{2}{3}$ of Gas will come out to make the presence $\mathrm{P}_{1}$
Hence $66.66\%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A container is divided into two equal part I and II by a partition with a small hole of diameter d. The two partitions are filled with same ideal gas, but held at temperature $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{I}}=150 \mathrm{~K}$ and $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{II}}=300 \mathrm{~K}$ by connecting to heat reservoirs. Let $\lambda_{\mathrm{I}}$ and $\lambda_{\mathrm{II}}$ be the mean free paths of the gas particles in the two parts such that $\mathrm{d}>\lambda_{\mathrm{I}}$ and $\mathrm{d}>\lambda_{\mathrm{II}}$. Then $\lambda_{\mathrm{I}} / \lambda_{\mathrm{II}}$ is close to $-$
questions.solution:
As dimension of hole is very small than mean path, then at equilibrium effusion rate of gas in both direction must be equal.
For this $\quad \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{1}}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{T}_{2}}}$ Mean free path $\propto \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{P}}$ $$ \begin{array}{c} \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \times \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \\ \frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \times \frac{\sqrt{T_{2}}}{\sqrt{T_{1}}} \\ \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{150}{300}}=0.7 \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The average distance between molecules of an ideal gas at STP is approximately of the order of
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{RT}}=\frac{10^{5} \times 1}{\frac{25}{3} \times 300}=40$
$\mathrm{~N}=40 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}=24 \times 10^{24}$
Average sep. $=\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~N}}\right)^{1 / 3}=1 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The number of gas molecules striking per second per square meter of the top surface of a table placed in a room at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atmospheric pressure is of the order of $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{B}}=1.4 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}\right.$, and the average mass of an air molecules is $5 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{M}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \mathrm{kT}}{\mathrm{M}_{0}}}$
$\mathrm{P}=\dot{\mathrm{N}} \times 2 \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{rms}}$
$1.01 \times 10^{5}=\dot{\mathrm{N}} \times 2 \times 5 \times 10^{-27} \times \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}$
$\begin{aligned} \dot{N} &=\frac{1.01 \times 10^{5} \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-27}}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-27} \sqrt{3 \times 1.4 \times 10^{-23} \times 293}} \\ &=6.43 \times 10^{27} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the speed of sound to the average speed of an air molecule at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ and 1 atmospheric pressure is close to-
questions.solution: $\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {sound }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{av}}}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma \mathrm{kT}}{\mathrm{m}} \times \frac{\pi \mathrm{M}}{8 \mathrm{kT}}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bulbs of identical volumes connected by a small capillary are initially filled with an ideal gas at temperature T. Bulb 2 is heated to maintain a temperature $2 \mathrm{~T}$ while bulb 1 remains at temperature $\mathrm{T}$. Assume throughout that the heat conduction by the capillary is negligible. Then the ratio of final mass of the gas in bulb 2 to the initial mass of the gas in the same bulb is close to $-$
questions.solution:
Mole conservation $\mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}=\mathrm{n}$
Initial no. of moles $=\mathrm{n}_{1}=\mathrm{n}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}$ finally when temp of 1 vessel is $T \&$ another is $2 T$ $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{n}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{RT}} \\ \mathrm{n}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{PV}}{\mathrm{R} 2 \mathrm{~T}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{n}_{1}}{\mathrm{n}_{2}}=\frac{2}{1} \\ \mathrm{n}_{1}+\mathrm{n}_{2}=\mathrm{n} \\ \mathrm{n}_{1}=\frac{2 \mathrm{n}}{3} ; \mathrm{n}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{3} \end{array}$ mass of gas $\propto \mathrm{n}_{1}$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{M}_{2}}{\mathrm{M}_{1}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{n}}{3}}{\frac{\mathrm{n}}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Jet aircrafts fly at altitudes above $30,000 \mathrm{ft}$ where the air is very cold at $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the pressure is $0.28 \mathrm{~atm}$. The cabin is maintained at 1 atm pressure by means of a compressor which exchanges air from outside adiabatically. In order to have a comfortable cabin temperature of $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, we will require in addition -
options: a heater to warm the air injected into the cabin
an air-conditioner to cool the air injected into the cabin
neither a heater nor an air-conditioner; the compressor is sufficient
alternatively heating and cooling in the two halves of the compressor cycle
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{PV}^{\gamma}=\mathrm{C}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{P}^{1-\gamma} \mathrm{T}^{\gamma}=\mathrm{C} \\
(0.28)^{1-\gamma} \times(233)^{\gamma}=1^{1-\gamma} \times \mathrm{T}^{\gamma} \\
\gamma=\frac{7}{5} \\
(0.28)^{1-7 / 5} \times(233)^{7 / 5}=1^{1-7 / 5} \times \mathrm{T}^{7 / 5} \\
\mathrm{~T}^{7 / 5}=233^{7 / 5} \times(0.28)^{-2 / 5} \\
\mathrm{~T}=233(0.28)^{-2 / 7} \\
\mathrm{~T}=\frac{233}{(0.28)^{2 / 7}}
\end{array}$
$\mathrm{T}$ is coming
more than $298 \mathrm{~K}$ or $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}$ is more than $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
so to cool it an extra ac is required.

questions.q: The number of air molecules in a $(5 \mathrm{~m} \times 5 \mathrm{~m} \times 4 \mathrm{~m})$ room at standard temperature and pressure is of the order of

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A van der Waal's gas obeys the equation of state $\left(\mathrm{P}+\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}^{2}}\right)(\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb})=\mathrm{nRT}$. Its internal energy is given by $\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{CT}-\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}} .$ The equation of a quasistatic ad iabat for this gas is given by-
questions.solution:
For adiabatic process
$\mathrm{dQ}=0$ and $-\mathrm{dU}=\mathrm{dW} \Rightarrow-\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{V}} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{P} \Delta \mathrm{V}$ or $-\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{dT}=\mathrm{PdV}$
when change is very small
now given $\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{CT}-\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{CdT}+\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}^{2}} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{~V}$
put this value of $\mathrm{dU} \mathrm{in}-\mathrm{dU}=\mathrm{d} \mathrm{W}$
$$
\therefore-\left(\mathrm{CdT}+\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}^{2}} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{~V}\right)=\mathrm{PdV}
$$
also $\mathrm{P}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb}}\right)-\frac{\mathrm{n}^{2} \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{V}^{2}}$ replace it in (1)
$$
-\left(C d T+\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}} d V\right)=\left(\left(\frac{n R T}{V-n b}\right)-\frac{n^{2} a}{V^{2}}\right) d V
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\therefore-\mathrm{CdT}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{nRT}}{\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb}}\right) \mathrm{dV} \\
\therefore-\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{nR}} \frac{\mathrm{dT}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\mathrm{dV}}{\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb}}
\end{array}
$$
Integrating we get
$-\ell \mathrm{n} \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{C} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{R}}=\ell \mathrm{n}(\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb})+\mathrm{k} \quad(\mathrm{k} \rightarrow$ constant of integration)
$\therefore \ln \left(\mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{nR}}\right)(\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb})=-\mathrm{k}$
or $\left(\mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{Tn} \mathrm{R}}}\right)(\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{nb})=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of ideal gas undergoes a linear process as shown in figure below. Its temperature expressed as a function of volume $\mathrm{V}$ is.
questions.solution:
$P=\frac{-P_{0}}{V_{0}} V+P_{0}$ $\&$ $P V=n R T$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
P V=n R T \\
\therefore T=\frac{P_{0} V}{R}\left[1-\frac{V}{V_{0}}\right]
\end{array}
$$

questions.q: Velocity of sound measured at a given temperature in oxygen and hydrogen is in the ratio-

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal monatomic gas expands to twice its volume. If the process is isothermal, the magnitude of work done by the gas is $W_{1}$. If the process is adiabatic, the magnitude of work done by the gas is $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{a}}$. Which of the following is true?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{i}}>\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{a}}>0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas follows aprocess described by $P V^{2}=C$ from $\left(P_{1}, V_{1}, T_{1}\right)$ to $\left(P_{2}, V_{2}, T_{2}\right)(C$ is a constant). Then

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long cylindrical pipe of radius $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ is closed atits upper end and has an airtight piston of negligible mass as shown. When a $50 \mathrm{Kg}$ mass is attached to the other end of the pistion, it moves down. If the air in the enclosure is cooled from temperature $T$ to $T-\Delta T$, the piston moves back to its original position. Then $\Delta T / T$ is close to (Assuming air to be an ideal gas, $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, atmospheric pressure is $10^{5}$ Pascal),
questions.solution:
Initially pressure $=P_{0}$ When $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is suspended
$\begin{array}{l}
\text { pressure }=P_{0}-\frac{m g}{A} \\
\text { (temp }=\text { constant }) \\
P_{0} V_{1}=\left(V_{0}-\frac{m g}{A}\right) V_{f} \\
\left(P_{0}-\frac{m g}{A}\right) V_{f}=n R T \\
\left(P_{0}-\frac{m g}{A}\right) V_{i}=n R(T-\Delta T) \\
=\frac{V_{f}}{V_{i}}=\frac{T}{T-\Delta T} \\
\frac{T-\Delta T}{T}=\frac{V_{i}}{V_{f}} \\
=\frac{\Delta T}{T}=\left(P_{0}-\frac{m g}{A}\right) \frac{1}{P_{0}} \\
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}} &=\frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{AP}_{0}} \\ &=\frac{50 \times 10}{3.14 \times(0.2)^{2} \times 10^{5}} \\ &=\frac{500}{12.56 \times 10^{3}} \\ &=\frac{5}{125.6}=\frac{1}{25}=0.04 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One mole of an ideal gas at initial temperature $\mathrm{T}$, undergoes a quasi-static process during which the volume $\mathrm{V}$ is doubled. During the process the internal energy $\mathrm{U}$ obeys the equation $\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{aV}^{3}$, where $a$ is a constant. The work done during this process is-
questions.solution:
$U=a V^{3}$
$\frac{f n R T}{2}=a V^{3}$
$P V=R T$
$\frac{P V}{R}=a V^{3}$
$P=C V^{2}$
$W=\int_{V}^{2 V} P d V=\int_{V}^{2 V} C V^{2} d V=\frac{C}{3}\left(8 V^{3}-V^{3}\right)=\frac{7 V^{3} C}{3}$
$\frac{f R T}{2}=a V^{3}$
$P V=R T$
$\frac{f P V}{2}=a V^{3}$
$P=\frac{2 a}{f} V^{2}$
$C=\frac{2 a}{f}$
$W=\frac{7}{3} \times V^{3} \times \frac{2 a}{f}=\frac{7}{3 f}$ f $n R T=\frac{7 R T}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a thermally isolated system, two boxes filled with an ideal gas are connected by a valve. When the valve is in closed position, states of the box 1 and 2 , respectively, are (1 atm, V, T) and (0.5 atm, $4 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{~T}$ ). When the valve is opened, the final pressure of the system is approximately
questions.solution: After opening of at equilibrium temperature and pressure of whole gas is $\mathrm{T}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{1}$ $\begin{array}{l} n_{1}=\frac{1 \times V}{R T}, n_{2}=\frac{0.5 \times V \times 4}{R T} \\ n_{1}+n_{2}=n \\ \frac{V}{R T}+\frac{V \times 4}{2 R T}=\frac{5 V P_{1}}{R T_{1}} \\ \frac{3 V}{R T}=\frac{5 V P_{1}}{R T_{1}} \\ \frac{P_{1}}{T_{1}}=\frac{0.6}{T} \\ \Delta Q=0, \quad \Delta W=0 \\ \therefore \Delta U=0 \\ n_{1} C_{V} T+n_{2} C_{V} T=\left(n_{1}+n_{2}\right) C_{V} T_{1} \\ T_{1}=T \\ \frac{P_{1}}{T}=\frac{0.6}{T} \\ P_{1}=0.6 \text { atm } \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An ideal gas with constant heat capacity $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{n} \mathrm{R}$ is made to carry out a cycle that is depicted by a triangle in the figure given below.
The following statement is true about the cycle-
options: The efficiency is given $1-\frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{P_{2} V_{2}}$
Net heat absorbed in the cycle is $\left(\mathrm{P}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{1}\right)\left(\mathrm{V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)$

Heat absorbed in part $\mathrm{AC}$ is given by $2\left(\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}+\mathrm{R}=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{2} \\
\mathrm{f}=3 \\
\mathrm{~W}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{2}-\mathrm{V}_{1}\right)\left(\mathrm{P}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{1}\right)
\end{array}
$$
For BA $\quad Q=n C p \Delta T=n\left(\frac{5}{2} R\right) \Delta T=\frac{5}{2}\left(P_{1} V_{1}-P_{2} V_{2}\right)$
For AC $\quad Q_{A C}=\frac{1}{2}\left(P_{1}+P_{2}\right)\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)+n C_{V} \Delta T$
now $n \mathrm{Cv} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{3}{2}\left(\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}-\mathrm{P}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}\right)$
now $\eta=\frac{W}{Q}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)\left(P_{2}-P_{1}\right)}{\frac{1}{2} \times\left(P_{1}+P_{2}\right)\left(V_{2}-V_{1}\right)+\frac{3}{2}\left(P_{2} V_{2}-P_{1} V_{1}\right)}$
using formula for heat we can calculate heat absorbed in $\mathrm{AC}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas with heat capacity at constant volume $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}$ undergoes a quasistatic process described by $\mathrm{PV}^{\alpha}$ in a P-V diagram, where $\alpha$ is a constant. The heat capacity of the gas during this process is given by-
questions.solution:
Direct formula is to be used
$$
\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{Cv}+\frac{\mathrm{nR}}{1-\alpha}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an hour-glass approximately 100 grains of sand fall per second (starting from rest); and it takes 2 s for each sand particle to reach the bottom of the hour-glass. If the average mass of each sand particle is 0.2 g, then the average force exerted by the falling sand on the bottom of the hour-glass is close to
questions.solution:
Force = Rate of change of momentum

Velocity with which a sand particle strikes the bottom of hour-glass,
v=u+g t

⇒  v=0+10×2=20 ms-1
Change in momentum of particle
=pf-pi=0-mv

=-0.2×10-3×20
Momentum imparted to base by the particle =4×10-3 kg·ms-1
Total change of momentum imparted per second by all 100 particles
=4×10-8 kg·ms-1×100 s-1=0.4 kg·ms-1
So, force on bottom =0.4 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The graph below shows the variation of a force F with time t on a body which is moving in a straight line. Dependence of force on time is F∝tn. Initially body is at rest.


If the speed of the object is 2 m/s at 3 s, then the speed at 4 s will be approximately (in m/s )
questions.solution:
Given force varies as

F∝tn⇒F=ktn....i

From graph, we observe


at t=2 s,F=2 N

and at t=4 s,F=16 N

So, putting the values in Eq (i), we have

16=k4n and 2=k2n

⇒8=22n2n⇒n=3

Also, 2=k2n⇒2=k23⇒k=14

So, force is given by F=t34

Now, F=dpdt, (where, p=momentum)

⇒dp=Fdt⇒∫vivfdp=∫titfFdt

⇒mvf-vi=∫titft34·dt

⇒mvf-vi=tf4-ti416....ii

Now, when ti=0 and tf=3 s
We have, vi=0 and vf=2 ms-1
So, from Eq. (ii), we have

2m=8116....iii

and when we take ti=0,tf=4 s,vi=0

and vf=v (let), then again from Eq (ii) we gives

mv=25616....iv

Dividing Eq. (iv) by Eq. (iii), we get

v2=25681

⇒v=256×281≈6.5 ms-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an experiment, mass of an object is measured by applying a known force on it, and then measuring its acceleration. IF, in the experiment, the measured values of applied force and the measured acceleration are $F=10.0 \pm 0.2 N$ and $a=1.00 \pm 0.01$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$, respectively, the mass of the object is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{Ma}$
$\mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{a}}$
$\frac{\Delta \mathrm{M} \times 100}{\mathrm{M}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{f}}{\mathrm{f}} \times 100+\frac{\Delta \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{a}} \times 100$
$\quad=\frac{0.2}{10}+\frac{0.01}{1}$
$\quad \Delta \mathrm{M}=0.03 \times 10$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}=10 \pm 0.3 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform rope of total length $l$ is at rest on a table with fraction $\mathrm{f}$ of its length hanging (see figure). If the coefficient of friction between the table and the chain is $\mu$ then
questions.solution: $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mu \mathrm{N}=\mu \mathrm{M}^{\prime \prime} \mathrm{g}=\mu\left(\mathrm{M}-\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{f}}\right) \mathrm{g}$ at equilibrium. $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{M}^{\prime} \mathrm{g}$ $\begin{array}{l} \mu \mathrm{M}(1-\mathrm{f}) \mathrm{g}=\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{F}} \mathrm{g} \\ \mu(1-\mathrm{f})=\mathrm{f} \\ \mu=(\mu+1) \mathrm{f} \\ \mathrm{f}=\frac{1}{\left(1+\frac{1}{\mu}\right)} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Meena applies the front brakes while riding on her bicycle along a flat road. The force that slows her bicycle is provided by the
questions.solution: The frictional force on the tyres is an external force and is being provided by the road. Other options i.e. front tyre, rear tyre and brakes comprise the internal parts of bicycle thus forces applied by them will be internal only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a pulley of mass M hangs a rope with two masses $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}>\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)$ tied at the ends as shown in the figure. The pulley rotates without any friction, whereas the friction between the rope and the pulley is large enough to prevent any slipping. Which of the following plots best represents the difference between the tensions in the rope on the two sides of the pulley as a function of the mass of the pulley?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{T}_{1}=\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{a}......\mathrm{(1)}$ $\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{~g}=\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{a}......\mathrm{(2)}$ $\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}\right) \times \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{I} \alpha \quad[\because \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{r} \alpha] \\ =\frac{\mathrm{Ia}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ (1)+(2) \\ \left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{g}+\left(\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}\right)=\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{a} \\ \left(\mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\left[\frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{g}+\left(\mathrm{T}_{2}-\mathrm{T}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}}\right] \\ \Rightarrow\left(\mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}\right)\left[1+\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{k}^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)}\right] \\ =\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{k}^{2}} \frac{\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)}{\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}} \mathrm{~g} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}=\left[\frac{\mathrm{I}\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{I}+\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{R}^{2}}\right] \\ =\frac{\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{g}}{\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^{2}+\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{R}^{2}\right)} \\ \mathrm{T}_{1}-\mathrm{T}_{2}=\frac{\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{M}+2\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right)} \end{array}$

questions.solution:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A plank is moving in a horizontal direction with a constant acceleration ai^. A uniform rough cubical block of side l rests on the plank and is at rest relative to the plank.


Let the centre of mass of the block be at 0, l/2 at a given instant. If a=g/10, then the normal reaction exerted by the plank on the block at that instant acts at
questions.solution:
Given situation is

Forces acting on the block are

In above diagram,

ma= pseudo force due to acceleration of plank, f= force of static friction, mg= weight of block and N= normal reaction.

Here, we must note that due to motion of plank, normal reaction force does not passes through centre of mass.

If line of action of normal force at a distance x from centre, then for equilibrium of block, net torque about centre of mass must be zero.

⇒τNormal reaction=τFriction

mg and ma does not produces any torque about centre of mass as their line of action passes through centre of mass.

⇒N·x=f·l/2

⇒mgx=mal2

∵N=mg & f=ma

x=agl2
or x=110·l2         ∵a=g10

or x=l20

So, coordinates of point from which reaction passes are -l20,0

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A box, when hung from a spring balance shows a reading of $50 \mathrm{~kg}$. If the same box is hung from the same spring balance inside an evacuated chamber, the reading on the scale will be
options: $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ because the mass of the box remains unchanged
$50 \mathrm{~kg}$ because the effect of the absence of the atmosphere will be indentical on the box and the spring balance
Less than $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ because the weight of the column of air on the box will be absent
More than $50 \mathrm{~kg}$ because the atmospheric buoyancy force will be absent
questions.solution: Sol. No Bnoycncy force in vaccum

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two blocks ( 1 and 2) of equal mass $m$ are connected by an ideal string (see figure shown) over a frictionless pulley. The blocks are attached to the ground by springs having spring constants $\mathrm{k}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{k}_{2}$ such that $\mathrm{k}_{1}>\mathrm{k}_{2}$
Initially, both springs are unstretched. The block 1 is slowly pulled down a distance $\mathrm{x}$ and released. Just after the release the possible values of the magnitude of the acceleration of the blocks $a_{1}$ and $a_{2}$ can be-
options: Either $\left(a_{1}=a_{2}=\frac{\left(k_{1}+k_{2}\right) x}{2 m}\right)$ or $\left(a_{1} \frac{k_{1} x}{m}-g\right.$ and $\left.a_{2}=\frac{k_{2} x}{m}+g\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{T}+\mathrm{k}_{1} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{ma}_{1} \\ \mathrm{k}_{2} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{T}=\mathrm{ma}_{2} \end{array}$ By constraint relation $\mathrm{a}_{1}=\mathrm{a}_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two plates each of the mass $\mathrm{m}$ are connected by a massless spring as shown.
A weight $W$ is put on the upper plate which compresses the spring further. When $W$ is removed, the entire assembly jumps up. The minimum weight $W$ needed for the assembly to jump up when the weight is removed is just more than -
questions.solution:
For lower block +ve lift, $\mathrm{kx} \geq \mathrm{mg}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x} \geq \frac{\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}$
W/E theorem $\begin{array}{l} -\mathrm{mg}(\mathrm{h}+\mathrm{x})+\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kh}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^{2}\right)=0-0 \\ \Rightarrow-\mathrm{mgh}-\frac{\mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2}}{\mathrm{k}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kh}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2}}{\mathrm{k}}=0 \\ \frac{\mathrm{kh}^{2}}{2}-\mathrm{mgh}-\frac{3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{k}}=0 \\ \mathrm{~h}=\frac{\mathrm{mg} \pm \sqrt{\mathrm{m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2}+3 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{2}}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ =\frac{\mathrm{mg} \pm 2 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}}, \frac{-\mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ \therefore \mathrm{h}=\frac{3 \mathrm{mg}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ \mathrm{W}+\mathrm{mg}=\mathrm{kh} \\ \mathrm{W}+\mathrm{mg}=3 \mathrm{mg} \\ \mathrm{W}=2 \mathrm{mg} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small child tries to moves a large rubber toy placed on the ground. The toy does not move but gets deformed under her pushing force $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}})$ which is obliquely upward as shown. Then
options: the resultant of the pushing force ($\vec{F }$ ), weight of the toy, normal force by the ground on the toy and the frictional force is zero
the normal force by the ground is equal and opposite to the weight of the toy
the pushing force ( $\vec{F}$ ) of the child is balanced by the equal and opposite frictional force
the pushing force ($\vec{F }$) \text { of the child is balanced by the total internal force in the toy generated due to } deformation
questions.solution:
$\because \sum \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{ext}}=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}_{\text {system }}=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a horizontal frictionless frozen lake, a girl (36 kg) and a box (9 kg) are connected to each other by means of a rope. Initially they are $20 \mathrm{~m}$ apart. The girl exerts a horizontal force on the box, pulling it towards her. How far has the girl traveled when she meets the box $>$
options: Since there is no friction, the girl will not move
questions.solution: $\because$ external force does not work on system So according to concept of mass $\begin{array}{l} 36 \mathrm{x}=9 \times(20-\mathrm{x}) \\ \mathrm{x}=4 \mathrm{~m} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A pen of mass ' $\mathrm{m}^{\prime}$ is lying on a piece of paper of mass $\mathrm{M}$ placed on a rough table. If the coefficient of friction between the pen and paper, and, the paper and table are $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$, respectively, then the minimum horizontal force with which the paper has to be pulled for the pen to start slipping is given by-
questions.solution:
For pen to start slipping maximum horizontal force on it is $\mathrm{f}=\mu \mathrm{mg}$ $\therefore \mathrm{a}=\mu_{1} \mathrm{~g}$ is the maximum common acceleration for both pen and paper F.B.D. for both pen and paper
$$ \begin{array}{l} \therefore \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{f}_{1}+\mathrm{f}_{2}+\mathrm{Ma} \\ \mathrm{F}=\mu_{1} \mathrm{mg}+\mu_{2}(\mathrm{~m}+\mathrm{M}) g+\mathrm{M}\left(\mu_{1} \mathrm{~g}\right) \\ \therefore \mathrm{F}=(\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{M})\left(\mu_{1}+\mu_{2}\right) \mathrm{g} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two balls of mass M and 2M are thrown horizontally with the same initial velocity v0 from top of a tall tower and experience a drag force of -kvk>0 where v is the instantaneous velocity.

Then,
options: the heavier ball will hit the ground further away than the lighter ball
the heavier ball will hit the ground closer than the lighter ball
both balls will hit the ground at the same point
both balls will hit the ground at the same time
questions.solution:
Only force resisting motion of particle in horizontal direction is drag force given by
F=-kv

But
F=m·ax

where, ax= acceleration in x-direction caused by drag force.
∴max=-kv⇒ax=-kvm

∴ Acceleration of particle is x-direction is inversely proportional to mass.
So, decreases in velocity in x-direction for lighter particle will be more.
Hence, heavier mass will hit the ground further away from the lighter ball.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rope of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ is hanging between two supports as shown. The tension at the lowest point of the rope is close to $\left(\right.$ take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ )
questions.solution:
$\therefore 2 \mathrm{~T}_{1} \cos 30^{\circ}=\mathrm{mg}=5 \times 10=50$ $2 \mathrm{~T}_{1} \cos 30^{\circ}=50$
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{T} &=\mathrm{T}_{1} \sin 30^{\circ} \\ &=\frac{50}{\frac{2 \sqrt{3}}{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}=\frac{25}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{25 \sqrt{3}}{3} \\ \mathrm{~T} &=14.41 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$

A horizontal force $F$ is applied to a block $X$ of mass $8 \mathrm{~kg}$ such that the block $Y$ of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ adjacent to it does not slip downwards under gravity. There is no friction between the horizontal plane and the base of the block $X$. The coefficient of friction between the surfaces of blocks $X$ and $Y$ is $0.5$. Take acceleration due to gravity to be $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. The minimum value of $F$ is
questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four identical beakers contain same amount of water as shown below. Beaker 'a' contains only water. A lead ball is held submerged in the beaker 'b' by string from above. A same sized plastic ball, say a table tennis (TT) ball, is held submerged in beaker 'c' by a string attached to a stand from outside. Beaker 'd' contains same sized TT ball which is held submerged from a string attached to the bottom of the beaker. These beakers (without stand) are placed on weighing pans and register reading $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{a}}, \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{b}}, \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{c}}$ and $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{d}}$ for $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ and d, respectively. (Effects of the mass and volume of the stand and string are to be neglected)
questions.solution:
(A)
$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{Mg}$ (B)
$\begin{aligned}\left(\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}}\right.&=\left(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{L}}\right) \mathrm{g}-\mathrm{T} \\ \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{B}} &=\mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{B} \end{aligned}$ (C)
$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{C}}=\mathrm{T}+\left(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{T}}\right) \mathrm{g}$ Therefore $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{T}} \mathrm{g}+\left(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{T}}\right) \mathrm{g}$ $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{B}+\mathrm{Mg}$ (D)
$\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{D}}=\left(\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{T}}\right) \mathrm{g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A bead of mass $\mathrm{m}$ is attached to the mid-point of a taut, weightless string of length $\ell$ and placed on a frictionless horizontal table.
Under a small trverse displacement $x$, as shown, if the tension in the string is $T$, then the frequency of oscillation is-
questions.solution: Let the angle of $\mathrm{T}$ with the vertical be $\theta$ then F.B.D. $\therefore 2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta=\mathrm{ma}$ also $\cos \theta=\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+\left(\frac{\ell}{2}\right)^{2}}}$ given $\ell>>x \quad \therefore \quad \frac{\ell^{2}}{4}+x^{2} \simeq \frac{\ell^{2}}{4}$ $\therefore \mathrm{a}=\frac{-2 \mathrm{Tx}}{\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\ell}{2}\right)}$ (negative sign for restoring force) or $\mathrm{a}=-\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{~m} \ell}\right) \mathrm{x}$ also this is similar to the equation of $\mathrm{SHM}$ i.e. $\mathrm{a}=-\omega^{2} \mathrm{x}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \therefore \omega=\sqrt{\frac{4 T}{m \ell}} \& f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi} \\ \therefore f=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{4 T}{m \ell}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two infinitely long parallel wires carry currents of magnitude $\mathrm{I}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{I}_{2}$ and are at a distance $4 \mathrm{~cm}$ apart. The magnitude of the net magnetic field is found to reach a non-zero minimum values between the two wires and $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ away from the first wire. The ratio of the two currents and their mutual direction is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{2}}{2 \pi(4-\mathrm{x})}$ $\frac{\mathrm{dB}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0$ for minima of $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{p}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{1}}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}\right]+\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}_{2}}{2 \pi} \frac{1}{(4-\mathrm{x})^{2}}=0$ $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2}}{(4-\mathrm{x})^{2}}$ $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}^{2}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4-\mathrm{x}}\right)^{2}$ $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{1}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{4-1}\right)^{2}$ $\frac{\mathrm{I}_{2}}{\mathrm{I}_{1}}=\frac{9}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In an experiment, setup A consists of two parallel wires which carry currents in opposite directions as shown in the figure. A second setup B is identical to setup A, except that there is a metal plate between the wires
Let $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{B}}$ be the magnitude of the force between the two wires in setup A and setup $\mathrm{B}$, respectively.
questions.solution:
In setup B, A metal is placed, due to which metal may get magnetized and it may also exert force on current carrying wire but force between two wire remain same however net force on wire may get charge due to magnetic field produced by magnetized metal.
Ans. (C)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An electron enters a chamber in which a uniform magnetic field is present as shown
An electric field of appropriate magnitude is also applied so that the electron travels undeviated without any change in its speed thorugh the chamber. We are ignoring gravity. Then, the direction of the electric field is
options: opposite to the direction of the magnetic field
opposite to the direction of the electron's motion
normal to the plane of the paper and coming out of the plane of the paper
normal to the plane of the paper and into the plane of the paper
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}+\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})=0$
Hence, into the paper.

The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is given by.

$$
B=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{4 \pi l} \times 2 \sin \theta
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The current in flowing along the path ABCD of a cube (shown in the left figure) produces a magnetic field at the centre of cube of magnitude B. Dashed line depicts the non-conducting part of the cube.


Consider a cubical shape shown to the right which is identical in size and shape to the left. If the same current now flows in along the path DAEFGCD, then the magnitude of magnetic field at the centre will be
questions.solution:
Given magnetic field due to a square loop DABCA at centre of cube is B.

Now, loop DAEFGCD can be viewed as super position of three square loops as shown below.

So, net field at centre=  resultant of fields of these three loop

∴Bnet=Bi^+Bj^-Bk^

Magnitude of resultant field is

Bnet=B2+B2+B2=3·B

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A collimated beam of charged and uncharged particles is directed towards a hole marked P on a screen as shown below. If the electric and magnetic fields as indicated below are turned on
options: only particles with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>B</mi></math> will go through the hole <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>

only charged particles with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and neutral particles will go through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
only neutral particles will go through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>

only positively charged particles with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and neutral particles will go through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
questions.solution:
For charged particles

net force is in downward direction, so they won't be able to go through the hole P.

And uncharged particle don't deviate so they will be able to go through hole P.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular region of dimensions $\mathrm{w} \times l(\mathrm{w}< < l)$ has a constant magnetic field into the plane of the paper as shown. On one side the region is bounded by a screen. On the other side positive ions of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $\mathrm{q}$ are accelerated from rest and towards the screen by a parallel plate capacitor at constant potential difference $\mathrm{V} < 0$, and come out through a small hole in the upper plate. Which one of the following statements is correct regarding the charge on the ions that hit the screen?
options: Ions with $\mathrm{q}>\frac{2|\mathrm{v}| \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{w}^{2}}$ will hit the screen.
Ions with $\mathrm{q} < \frac{2|\mathrm{v}| \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{B}^{2} \mathrm{w}^{2}}$ will hit the screen.
All ions will hit the screen.
Only ions with $q=\frac{2|v| m}{B^{2} w^{2}}$ will hit the screen.
questions.solution: Ions will hit if $\mathrm{r}>\mathrm{W}$. $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{w}=\mathrm{q} \Delta \mathrm{V} \\ \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=\mathrm{q} \mathrm{V} \\ \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} \\ \frac{\mathrm{mv}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{qvB} \end{array}$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r} &=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{qB}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{m}}} \\ \mathrm{r}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{q}}} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{~B}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{q}}}>\mathrm{w} \\ & \frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{q}}>\mathrm{w}^{2} \mathrm{~B}^{2} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{q} & < \frac{2 \mathrm{mV}}{\mathrm{w}^{2} \mathrm{~B}^{2}} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A conducting rod of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and length $l$ is free to move without friction on two parallel long conducting rails, as shown below. There is a resistance $R$ across the rails. In the entire space around, there is a uniform magnetic field B normal to the plane of the rod and rails. The rod is given an impulsive velocity $\mathrm{v}_{0}-$
Finally, the initial energy $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{0}^{2}$
options: Will be converted fully into heat energy in the resistor
Will enable rod to continue to move with velocity $\mathrm{v}_{0}$ since the rails are frictionless.
Will be converted fully into magnetic energy due to induced current
Will be converted into the work done against the magnetic field
questions.solution: Due to energy conservation

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steady current I flows through a wire of radius $\mathrm{r}$, length $\mathrm{L}$ and resistivity $\rho$. The current produced heat in the wire. The rate of heat loss in a wire is proportional to its surface area. The steady temperature of the wire is independent of-
questions.solution: Concept of fuse wire

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A rectangular loop of wire shown below is coplanar with a long wire carrying current $\mathrm{I}$.
The loop is pulled to the right as indicated. What are the directions of the induced current in the loop and the magnetic forces on the left and the right sides of the loop?
options: Induced current - Counterclockwise, Force on left side - To the left, Force on right side - To the right
Induced current - Clockwise, Force on left side - To the left, Force on right side - To the right
Induced current - Counterclockwise, Force on left side - To the right, Force on right side - To the left
Induced current - Clockwise, Force on left side - To the right, Force on right side - To the left
questions.solution:
Flux is inward and it is decreasing as loop is going away from wire
$\therefore$ direction of induced current is clockwise
Force on left side is in left and force on right side is in right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle of charge $\mathrm{q}$ and mass $\mathrm{m}$, gets deflected through an angle $\theta$ upon passing through a square region of side 'a' which contains a uniform magnetic field B normal to its plane. Assuming that the particle entered the square at right angles to one side, what is the speed of the particle?
questions.solution: Now $\sin \theta=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{R}}, \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}$ $$ \therefore \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{q} \mathrm{Bacot} \theta}{\mathrm{m}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The magnetic field is uniform for y>0 and points into the plane. The magnetic field is uniform and points out of the plane for y<0. A proton denoted by filled circle leaves y=0 in the -y-direction with some speed as shown below.

Which of the following best denotes the trajectory of the proton?
questions.solution:
Following Fleming's left hand rule, proton initially rotates clockwise and it enters region y>0 with its velocity in positive y-direction.

Here in region y>0, magnetic field is into plane of paper. So, proton again follows left hand rule and now tend to rotate anti-clockwise as shown below.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two infinitely long wires each carrying current I along the same direction are made into the geometry as shown in the figure. The magnetic field at the point $\mathrm{P}$ is
questions.solution: $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})_{0}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})_{\text {wire } \mathrm{AB}}+(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})_{\mathrm{BC} \mathrm{Arc}}$ $\quad+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{CD} \text { wire }}+(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})_{\mathrm{PQ} \text { wire }}$ $\quad+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{QR}(\text { Arc })}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{RS} \text { wire }}$ $\quad \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{RS}}=0$ $\quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{BC}}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{QR}}$ $(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})_{\mathrm{wire} \mathrm{AB}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{CD}}$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\text {net }}=(\mathrm{B})_{\text {wire } \mathrm{AB}}+(\mathrm{B})_{\text {wire } \mathrm{CD}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}}+\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}}$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\text {net }}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam of neutrons performs circular motion of radius, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> under the influence of an inhomogeneous magnetic field with inhomogeneity extending over <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The speed of the neutrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>54</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The mass and magnetic moment of the neutrons respectively are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>67</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The average variation of the magnetic field over <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><mi>r</mi></math> is approximately.
questions.solution:
F=M∂B∂r=mv2r

ΔB=mv2MrΔr

=1.67×10-27×542×0.019.67×10-27×1=5.03 T

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:

During its motion inside the chamber

the kinetic energy of the electron remains constant
the momentum of the electron remains constant
the speed of the electron increases at a uniform rate

$\because$ work done $=0$
Hence kinetic energy $=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A loop carrying current $\mathrm{I}$ has the shape of a regular polygon of $\mathrm{n}$ sides. If $\mathrm{R}$ is the distance from the centre to any vertex, then the magnitude of the magnetic induction vector $\vec{B}$ at the centre of the loop is -
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{B}_{\text {net }} &=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{B}_{1} \\ &=\mathrm{n} \cdot \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R} \cos \frac{\pi}{\mathrm{n}}} 2 \sin \frac{\pi}{\mathrm{n}} \end{aligned}$

average change in orientation per year =180°105 degree year-1

$$
\frac{d F}{d L}=\frac{B}{R}\left(\frac{d B \cdot A}{d t}\right)
$$
Where $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{B}_{0} \sin \omega t$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The distance s travelled by a particle in time $\mathrm{t}$ is
$$
\mathrm{s}=\mathrm{ut}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^{2}
$$
The initial velocity of the particle was measured to be $\mathrm{u}=1.11 \pm 0.01 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and the time interval of the experiment was $\mathrm{t}=1.01 \pm$ $0.1 \mathrm{~s}$. The acceleration was taken to be $\mathrm{g}=9.8$ $\pm 0.1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$. With these measurements, the student estimates the total distance travelled. How should the student report the result ?
questions.solution:
This question is related to significant numbers as in the question it is asked how student REPORT the result.

On analysis the values of $\mathrm{u}, \mathrm{t}$ and $\mathrm{g}$ the reported result must have three significant number.
Hence correct answer is [B]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Students <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> measure the length of a room using <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long measuring tape of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>LC</mi><mo>)</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> meter-scale of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LC</mi><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and a foot-scale of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>LC</mi><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the specified length of the room is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then which of the following students will report the lowest relative error in the measured length?
questions.solution:
Student A: Length of scale =25 m
Least count=0.5 cm=0.005 m
Student A can measure the length of 9.5 m by using the scale only once so there will be an error of 0.005 m in 9.5 m
∴ Relative error =0.0059.5=0.0005
Student B: Length of scale:1 m=100 cm
Least count =0.05 cm
To measure 9.5 m, student B has to use this meter scale atleast 10 times
∴Relative error =0.05100×10=0.005 cm
Student C: Length of scale:1 foot =30.48 cm
Least count=0.05 cm

To measure 9.5 m, student C has to use this scale
approximately 31 times
∴Relative error =0.0530.48×31=0.05 cm
∴ Relative error is least for Student A

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Force $\vec{F}$ applied on a body is written as $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \hat{\mathrm{F}}) \hat{\mathrm{n}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{G}}$, where $\hat{\mathrm{n}}$ is a unit vector The vector $\vec{G}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}) \times \hat{\mathrm{n}}=-[\hat{\mathrm{n}} \times(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}})]$
$=-[\hat{\mathrm{n}}(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}})-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \hat{\mathrm{n}})]$
$=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}-\hat{\mathrm{n}}(\hat{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}})]$
$=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{G}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length and width of a rectangular room are measured to be $3.95 \pm 0.05 \mathrm{~m}$ and $3.05 \pm 0.05 \mathrm{~m}$, respectively, the area of the floor is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{A}=\ell \mathrm{B}$
$\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}=\ell \mathrm{dB}+\mathrm{B} \mathrm{d} \ell$
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\ell \mathrm{dB}}{\ell \mathrm{B}}+\frac{\mathrm{Bd} \ell}{\ell \mathrm{B}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{B}}+\frac{\mathrm{d} \ell}{\ell}$
$\quad=\frac{0.05}{3.05}+\frac{0.05}{3.95}$
$\quad=0.016+0.012$
$\quad=0.028 \times 12.05$
$\mathrm{dA}=0.33$
$12.05 \pm 0.34$ Ans. (C)

By Bernoulli's equation for streamline flow,

p+12ρv2+ρgh=a (constant)
In given situation h= constant

When velocity of flow in narrow section increases, pressure decreases.

So, current level in vertical tubes is as shown below.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The platelets are drifting with the blood flowing in a streamline flow through a horizontal artery as shown below:

Artery is contracted in region II. Choose the correct statement.
options: As the platelets enter a constriction, the platelets gets squeezed closer together in the narrow region and hence the fluid pressure must rise there.
As the platelets enter a constriction, pressure is lower there.
The artery's cross section area is smaller in the constriction and thus the pressure must be larger there because pressure equals the force divided by area
Pressure is same in all the parts of the artery
questions.solution:
Using equation of continuity

A1V1=A2V2

where A1 & A2 are cross-section area of region I & region-II.

as A2<A1

⇒V2>V1

Using Bernouilli's equation

P+12ρV2= constant

as V2>V1

P2<P1

therefore pressure will be lower at constriction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A very large block of ice of the size of a volleyball court and of uniform thickness of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi></math> is floating on water. A person standing near its edge wishes to fetch a bucketful of water using a rope. The smallest length of rope required for this is about
questions.solution:
Fraction of thickness of ice block out of water is

x=1-ρice ρwater =1-0.91 or x=0.1

So, minimum length of rope required ≈ thickness of ice ×0.1=8×0.1=0.8 m
Hence, nearest option is 0.9 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A submarine has a window of area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> on its ceiling and is at a depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> below sea level in a sea. If the pressure inside the submarine is maintained at the sea-level atmosphere pressure, then the force acting on the window is (consider density of sea water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> acceleration due to gravity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>):
questions.solution:
P=P0+ρgh

P= Pressure on upper surface of window =P0+ρgh

Pin= Pressure inside the submarine =P0

Net force =P0+ρghA-P0A

=ρghA

=1.03×103×10×100×900×10-4

=9.27×104 Newton

=0.93×105 Newton

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bottle has a thin nozzle on top. It is filled with water, held horizontally at a height of 1 m and squeezed slowly by hands so that the water jet coming out of the nozzle hits the ground at a distance of 2 m. If the area over which the handss s sare which the hands squeeze it is 10 cm2, the force applied by hands is close to (take g=10 m/s2 and density of water =1000 kg/m3)
questions.solution:
Apply Bernoulli between point- 1 and point-2.

P1+12ρV12=P2+12ρV22

P1=Patm + Force by hand  Area 

V1 tends to zero becomes area of point- 2 is very small.

P2=Patm

Patm +F/A=Patm +1/2ρV22⇒V22=2FPA …i

From Kinematics.

2=2( h)g×V2

∴V22=20 …ii

Using (i) and (ii) we get

20=2FρA ∴F=10 N

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider the configuration of a stationary water tank of cross-section area A0 and a small bucket as shown in figure below;

What should be the speed v of the bucket, so that the water leaking out of a hole of cross-section area A (as shown) from the water tank does not fall outside the bucket?

(Take, h=5 m,H=5 m, g=10 m/s2,A=5 cm2 and A0=500 cm2).
questions.solution:
Velocity of outflow (efflux) from hole  =2gh.

Range of water jet, 

x=2gh × Time of fall 

=2gh×2Hg=2hH

Velocity v of bucket is 

v=dxdt=ddt2hH

=2H.dhdt

⇒v=2H.12h.dhdt

=Hh.dhdt  ...(i)

Now, using equation of continuity at area A0 and area A, we have 

Av1=A0V

where, v1=2gh

and V=dhdt.

∴ A2gh=A0dhdt

Substituting for dhdt from Eq (i), we get

v=Hh.AA0.2gh

=55.5500.2×10×5

=110 ms-1

=0.1 ms-1

questions.q: In Guericke's experiment to show the effect of atmospheric pressure, two copper hemispheres were tightly fitted to each other to fom a hollow sphere and the air from the sphere was pumped out to create vacuum inside. If the radius of each hemisphere is $R$ and the atmospheric pressure is $P$, then the minimum force required (when the two hemispheres are pulled apart by the same force) to separate the hemispheres is
questions.solution: $F=\left(P_{0}-0\right) A$ $F=\pi R^{2} P$

questions.solution: due to soap bubble surface tension is reduced therefore in that area. Black paper powder will sink.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical marble of radius $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ is stuck in a circular hole of radius slightly smaller than its own radius (for calculation purpose, both can be taken same) at the bottom of a bucket of height $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ and filled with water up to $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. If the mass of the marble is $20 \mathrm{~g}$, the net force on the marble due to water is close to
questions.solution: $=(\rho g h) A-\left(\frac{2}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) \rho g$ $=A\left(10^{3} \times 10 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}\right)-\frac{2}{3} \pi \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{3} \times 10$ Area $=0.04 \mathrm{~N}$ dowanward

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical vessel of base radius $\mathrm{R}$ and height $\mathrm{H}$ has a narrow neck of height $\mathrm{h}$ and radius $\mathrm{r}$ at one end (see figure). The vessel is filled with water (density $\rho_{w}$ ) and its neck is filled with immiscible oil (density $\left.\rho_{O}\right)$. Then the pressure at
questions.solution: Pressure at same height is same $P_{M}=P_{N}=\rho_{0} g H+\rho_{w} g H$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The bhagirathi and the Alaknanda merge at Deoprayag toform the Ganga with their speeds in the ratio $1: 1.5$. The cross-sectional areas of the Bhagirathi, the Alaknanda and the Ganga are in the ratio $1: 2: 3$. Assuming stremline flow, the ratio of the speed of Ganga to that of the Alaknands is
questions.solution:
By equation of continuity
Area of Bhagirathi $\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}$
Area of Alaknanda $\Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{~A}$
Area of Ganga $\Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~A}$
$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AL}}: \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{G}}: \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}: \frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~V}: \mathrm{V}_{1}$
By equation of continuity
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{AV}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~A} \cdot 2 . \mathrm{V} \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{~A} \cdot \mathrm{V}_{1} \\
\mathrm{~V}_{\text {ganga }}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~V} \\
\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {Alaknanda }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {ganga }}} \Rightarrow \frac{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{~V}}{\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~V}}=\frac{9}{8} \\
\mathrm{~V}_{\text {ganga }}: \mathrm{V}_{\text {Alaknanda }}=8: 9
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Stoke's law states that the viscous drag force $F$ experience by a sphere of radius a, moving with a speed $\mathrm{v}$ through a fluid with coefficient of viscosity $\eta$, is given by $F=6 \pi n a v$
If this fluid is flowing through a cylindrical pipe of radius $\mathrm{r}$, length 1 and a pressure difference of P across its two ends, then the volume of water $\mathrm{V}$ which flows through the pipe in time $\mathrm{t}$ can be written as
$$
\frac{v}{t}=k\left(\frac{p}{l}\right)^{a} \eta^{b} r^{c}
$$
Where $\mathrm{k}$ is a dimensionless constant. Correct values of $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and $\mathrm{c}$ are
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{V}{t}=\left(\frac{p}{l}\right)^{a} \eta^{b} r^{c} \\
L^{3} T^{-1}=\left(M L^{-2} T^{-2}\right)^{a} L^{c}\left(M L^{-1} T^{-1}\right)^{b} \\
\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}=0 \\
-2 a-b+c=3 \\
-2 a-b=-1 \\
\quad c=4, a=1, b=-1
\end{array}
$$
Ans (A) is correct

move with lesser speed and are smaller than in the rest of the pipe.

questions.q:
A tall tank filled with water has an irregular shape as shown. The wall $\mathrm{CD}$ makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal; the wall $\mathrm{AB}$ is normal to the base $\mathrm{BC}$. The lengths $\mathrm{AB}$ and $\mathrm{CD}$ are much smaller than the height h of water (figure not to scale).
Let $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{3}$ be the pressures exerted by the water on the wall $\mathrm{AB}$, base $\mathrm{BC}$ and the wall $\mathrm{CD}$ respectively. Density of water is $\rho$ and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity. Then, approximately
questions.solution: All are nearly at same height hence $\mathrm{P}_{1}=\mathrm{P}_{2}=\mathrm{P}_{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid cube and a solid sphere both made of same material are completely submerged in water but to different depths. The sphere and the cube have same surface area. The buoyant force is-
options: Greater for the cube than the sphere
Greater for the sphere than the cube
Same for the sphere and the cube
Greater for the object that is submerged deeper
questions.solution:
buyount force $\mathrm{B}=\mathrm{V} \rho_{l} \mathrm{~g}, \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {cube }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {spbere }}}=\frac{\mathrm{a}^{3}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^{3}}$
but it is given $6 \mathrm{a}^{2}=4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{2}$
so, $\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {cube }}}{\mathrm{V}_{\text {spbere }}}=\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{6}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
An object with uniform density $\rho$ is attached to a spring that is known to stretch linearly with applied force as shown below.
When the spring-object system is immersed in a liquid of density $\rho_{1}$ as shown in the figure, the spring stretches by an amount $x_{1}\left(\rho>\rho_{1}\right)$. When the experiment is repeated in a liquid of density $\rho_{2} < \rho_{1}$, the spring stretches by an amount $x_{2}$. Neglecting any buoyant force on the spring, the density of the object is
questions.solution:
F.B.D
$\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{kx}_{1}+\rho_{1} \mathrm{Vg}=\rho \mathrm{Vg} ...(1) \\ \mathrm{kx}_{2}+\rho_{2} \mathrm{Vg}=\rho \mathrm{Vg} ...(2) \end{array}$ from (1) and (2) $\rho=\frac{\rho_{2} \mathrm{x}_{1}-\rho_{1} \mathrm{x}_{2}}{\mathrm{x}_{1}-\mathrm{x}_{2}}=\frac{\rho_{1} \mathrm{x}_{2}-\rho_{2} \mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{x}_{2}-\mathrm{x}_{1}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle released from rest is falling through a thick fluid under gravity. The fluid exerts a resistive force on the particle proportional to the square of its speed. Which one of the following graphs best depicts the variation of its speed $\mathrm{v}$ with time $\mathrm{t}-$
questions.solution: $\mathrm{mg}^{\prime}-\mathrm{kv}^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{mdv}}{\mathrm{dt}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: We sit in the room with windows open. Then
options: air pressure on the floor of the room equals the atmospheric pressure but the air pressure on the ceiling is negligible
air pressure is nearly the same on the floor, the walls and the ceiling
air pressure on the floor equals the weight of the air column inside the room (from floor to ceiling) per unit area
air pressure on the walls is zero since the weight of air acts downward
questions.solution: Pressure of gas is app. same everywhere in the vessel

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> in water of density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> is expanding uniformly at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math>. Given that water is incompressible, the kinetic energy of water being pushed is
questions.solution:
Let bubble of radius x expands and pushes water of density ρ by a distance dx.

Then, kinetic energy of water of strip is dx is 

dK=12dm·vx2⇒dK=124πx2·dx·ρ·vx2
or dK=2πR2·ρ·vx2·dx   ...(i)

Considering laminar flow, for section 1 and 2 shown in figure, by equation of continuity

A1v1=A2v2⇒4πx2vx=4πR2v

⇒vx=R2x2.v  ...(iii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

dK=2πx2ρR2x2·v·2dx

⇒dK=2πR4ρv2.dxx2

So, kinetic energy of complete volume of
water, 

K=∫R∞dK=2πR4ρv2∫R∞dxx2

⇒K=2πR4·ρ·v-1xR∞

=2πρR3v2

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the wall of a dam to be straight with height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. It holds a lake of water of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>h</mi><mo><</mo><mi>H</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> on one side. Let the density of water be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mi>w</mi></msub></math> Denote the torque about the axis along the bottom length of the wall by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>τ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. Denote also a similar torque due to the water up to height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and wall length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></math> by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>τ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>τ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>/</mo><msub><mi>τ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> (ignore atmospheric pressure) is
questions.solution:
Consider a strip of width dy at a depth h-y below the surface. 

Torque on bottom length ( OZ in diagram) due to force of water on strip of width dy is 

dτ= Force × Perpendicular distance = Pressure × Area × Distance

dτ=ρgh-y·L·dy·y

So, torque on bottom length due to complete volume of water, 

τ1=∫0hLρgh-yy·dy

=Lρg∫0hhy-y2dy

=Lρghy22-y330h

=Lρgh32-h33

or τ1=Lρgh36  ...(i)

and torque due to water upto height h2 and wall length 12, τ2=∫0h2L2ρgh2-yydy

=L2ρghy24-hy330h

=L2ρgh316-h324=Lρgh316×6

So, τ1τ2=16

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of wood is floating on water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi></math> with volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> above water. When the temperature of water increases from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the change in the volume of the block that is above water is best described schematically by the graph.
questions.solution:
As temnerature of water is increased from 0°C to 10°C, density of water initially increases upto a maximum at 4°C and then it reduces.
So, buoyant force on block of wood also increases till temperature reaches 4°C and then decreases from 4°C to 10°C.
Hence, volume of block above water also increases upto 4°C and then decreases from 4°C to 10°C

∴ Variation of V0 versus t as shown below.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Air (density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> ) is being blown on a soap film (surface tension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> ) by a pipe of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with its opening right next to the film. The film is deformed and a bubble detaches from the film when the shape of the deformed surface is a hemisphere. Given that the dynamic pressure on the film due to the air blown at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>ρ</mi><msup><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the speed at which the bubble formed is
questions.solution:
Bubble detached from the look at ends of circumference, when force of dynamic pressure exceeds force of surface tension.

⇒pdynamic× Area ≥ Force of surface tension

⇒12ρv2×πR2≥22πRT

Note- that factor 2 on right hand side appears as there are two sides of film surface.

⇒v≥8TρR

⇒vmin=8TρR

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nurse measures the blood pressure of a seated patient to be $190 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$ -
options: The blood pressure at the patient's feet is less than $190 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$
The actual pressure is about $0.25$ times the atmospheric pressure
The blood pressure at the patient's neck is more than $190 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}
The actual pressure is about $1.25$ times the atmospheric pressure
questions.solution:
Blood pressure is gauge pressure $=190 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
Atmospheric pressure $=760 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
Actual pressure $=190+760 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}=950 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}=1.25 \times 760 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{Hg}$
