Assume that the thickness of the soap film in all bubbles is uniform and equal. Assume that the density of soap solution is same as that of water $\left(=1000 \mathrm{kgm}^{-3}\right)$. The density of helium inside the bubbles and air are $0.18 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ and $1.23 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$, respectively. Then the thickness of the soap film of the bubbles is (note $1 \mu \mathrm{m}=10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}$ )
questions.solution:
Weight $=F_{0}$ 
$4 \pi r^{2} t \rho_{w} g+4 / 3 \pi r^{3} \rho_{N e} g=4 / 3 \pi r^{3} \rho_{\text {air }} g$ 
$\therefore t=3.5$ um

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A healthy adult of height 1.7 m has an average blood pressure (BP) of 100 mm of Hg. The heart is typically at a height of 1.3 m from the foot. Take, the density of blood to be 103 kg/m3 and note that 100 mm of Hg is equivalent to 13.3kPa (kilo pascals). The ratio of BP in the foot region to that in the head region is close to
questions.solution:
Pressure at head level =pheart -ρgh

=13.3-103×10×0.4

=9.3kPa

Pressure at foot level =pheat +ρgh

=13.3+103×10×1.3

=26.3kPa

So, ratio =26.39.3≈2.9 or 3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A horizontal steel railroad track has a length of $100 \mathrm{~m}$ when the temperature is $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The track is constrained from expanding or bending. The stress on the track on a hot summer day, when the temperature is $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, is (Note: The linear coefficient of thermal expansion for steel is $1.1 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the Young's modulus of steel is
$\left.2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{~Pa}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\frac{F}{A}=Y \alpha . \Delta T=2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 15$
$3.3 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~Pa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rods, one made of copper and the other steel of the same length and cross sectional area are joined together. (The thermal conductivity of copper is $385 \mathrm{~J} \cdot \mathrm{s}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{K}^{-1}$ and steel is $50 \mathrm{~J} \cdot \mathrm{s}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{-1} \cdot \mathrm{K}^{-1}$.) If the copper end is held at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and the steel end is held at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, what is the junction temperature (assuming no other heat losses)?
questions.solution: $\frac{100-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{T}-0}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}$ $\frac{100-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}$ $\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{KA}}$ $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}$ $\frac{100-\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{50}{385}=\frac{10}{77}$ $7700-77 \mathrm{~T}=10 \mathrm{~T}$ $7700=87 \mathrm{~T}$ $\mathrm{~T}=\frac{7700}{87}=88^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.q: Consider two wires of same material having their ratio of radii to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. If these two wires are stretched by equal force, the ratio of stress produced in them is :
questions.solution:
Stress = Force  Area ∝1 Area 

Stress ∝1r2  (Area =πr2)

ratio of stress =122=14

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal rod of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is hanging in a chamber kept at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> with a weight attached to its free end. The coefficient of thermal expansion of the rod is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and its Young's modulus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. When the temperature of the chamber is lowered to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, then a weight of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> needs to be attached to the rod, so that its length is unchanged. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Length of rod given remains unchanged. This means contraction due to cooling is equals to elongation due to hanging of weight. 

As, thermal strain = strain caused by weight

⇒αΔθ=Δll

⇒Δθ=Δl/lα=FYAα  ∵Y=F/AΔl/l

Note Δθ in K is same as Δθ in C∘.

∴ 20-T=5°C or T=15°C

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An aluminum piece of mass $50 \mathrm{~g}$ initially at $300{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is dipped quickly and taken out of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water, initially at $30{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the teperature of the aluminum piece be $160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, what is the temperature of the water then (Specific heat capacities of aluminum and water are $900 \mathrm{JKf}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ and $4200 \mathrm{Jkg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$, respectively)
questions.solution:
Heat lost $=$ heat gas
$0.05 \times 900 \times(300-160)=1 \times 4200 \times(T-30)$
$T=31.5^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone thrown down with a seed $u$ takes a time $t_{1}$ to reach the ground, while another stone, thrown upwards from the same point with the same speed, takes time $t_{2}$. The maximum height the second stone reaches from the ground is
questions.solution:
$-\mathrm{h}=-\mathrm{Ut}_{1}+\frac{-1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2}$ $\mathrm{~h}=\mathrm{Ut}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2} \ldots .$(1)
$-\mathrm{h}=\mathrm{Ut}_{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{2}{ }^{2} \ldots \ldots$ (2) $\mathrm{Ut}_{1}+\mathrm{Ut}_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{2}{ }^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2} \quad($ from $1 \& 2)$ $\mathrm{U}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right) \times\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)$ $\mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore & \text { Max height }=\mathrm{h}+\frac{\mathrm{U}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ &=(\mathrm{h})+\frac{\mathrm{U}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ &=\mathrm{U} \mathrm{t}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\frac{\mathrm{U}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2} \mathrm{t}_{1}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~g}} \frac{\mathrm{g}^{2}}{4}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)^{2} \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right) \mathrm{t}_{1}+\frac{\mathrm{gt}_{1}{ }^{2}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{g}}{8}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)^{2} \\ &=\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\right)\left\{\left(\mathrm{t}_{2} \mathrm{t}_{1}-\mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2}\right)+\mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2}+\frac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{2}-\mathrm{t}_{1}\right)^{2}}{4}\right\} \\ &=\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\right) \frac{\left\{4 \mathrm{t}_{2} \mathrm{t}_{1}-4 \mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2}+4 \mathrm{t}_{1}{ }^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}-2 \mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}\right\}}{4} \\ &=\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\right) \frac{\left\{\mathrm{t}_{1}^{2}+\mathrm{t}_{2}^{2}+2 \mathrm{t}_{1} \mathrm{t}_{2}\right\}}{4} \\ &=\left(\frac{\mathrm{g}}{2}\right) \frac{\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)^{2}}{4} \\ &=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{8}\left(\mathrm{t}_{1}+\mathrm{t}_{2}\right)^{2} \end{aligned}$ Hence correct Answer is (B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A box filled with water has a small hole on its side near the bottom. It is dropped from the top of a tower. As it falls, a camera attached on the side of the box records the shape of the water stream coming out of the hole. The resulting video will show
options: the water coming down forming a parabolic stream
the water going up forming a parabolic stream
the water coming out in a straight line
questions.solution: When box with hole is in free fall, both water and box cover equal distance downwards in equal time. Hence, no water comes out of hole in free fall of box.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person goes from point $\mathrm{P}$ to point $\mathrm{Q}$ covergin $1 / 3$ of the distance with speed $10 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$, the next $1 / 3$ of the distance at $20 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$ and the last $1 / 3$ of the distance at $60 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$. The average speed of the person is
questions.solution:
$V_{\text {avg }}=\frac{\text { Total Distance }}{\text { total time }}=\frac{\mathrm{S}}{\frac{\mathrm{S} / 3}{10}+\frac{\mathrm{S} / 3}{20}+\frac{\mathrm{S} / 3}{60}}$
$\mathrm{~V}_{\text {avg }}=18 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three balls, A,B and C, are released and all reach the point X (shonw in the figure). Balls A and B are released from two identical structures, one kept on the ground and the other at height, h, from the ground as shown in the figure. They take time tA and tB respectively to reach X (time starts after they leave the end of the horizontal portion of the structure). The ball C is released from a point at height, h, vertically above X and reaches X in time tC. Choose the correct statement.
questions.solution:
Work done by gravity on A and B is same. 

∴ Horizontal velocity of A= horizontal velocity of B as they leave the horizontal portion of the structure.

∴ tA=tB   …(i)

Also vertical velocity of A and vertical velocity of C when released are both zero

∴ They both will cover same vertical distance in same time. 

∴ tA=tC  ...(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

tA=tB=tC

Note : Correction in option option B should be tA=tB=tC

questions.q: A mouse jumps off from the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>15</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> floor of high-rise building and lands <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the building. Assume that each floor is of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> height. The horizontal speed with which the mouse jumps is closest to :

Time of fall =2hg=2×4510

t=3 sec

horizontal distance = horizontal velocity × time 12=v×33$

=4×185 km hr-1

v=14.4 km hr-1

v≈15 km hr-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a ball is thrown at a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>s</mi></math> in vertical upward direction, then what would be the velocity profile as function of height? (Assume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
30. (a) For the ball, we have u=45 ms-1,g=-10 ms-2

Now using, v2-u2=2 g h, we have
v2=(45)2-20 h

⇒  v=2025-20h
At v=0,h=202520=101 m

at h=0,v=45 ms-1
As velocity decreases-with height, slope of v-h graph must be negative at all points. Hence, correct graph is (a).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object at rest at the origin begins to move in the $+x$ direction with a uniform acceleration of $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$ for $4 \mathrm{~s}$ and then it continues moving with a uniform velocity of $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in the same direction. The $\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}$ graph for object's motion will be
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{V}=0+1 \times \mathrm{t} \\
\frac{d x}{d t}=t \\
d x=t \mathrm{dt} \\
x \propto t^{2} \quad \ldots \ldots \ldots \\ Parabolic
\frac{d x}{d t}=4 \\
d x=4 d t \\
x \propto t \quad \ldots \ldots \ldots Linear
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A juggler tosses a ball up in the air with initial speed $u$. At the instant it reaches its maximum height $\mathrm{H}$, he tosses up a second ball with the same initial speed. The two balls will collide at a height
questions.solution: $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^{2}$ $\mathrm{~h}=\mathrm{ut}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^{2}$ $\mathrm{H}=\mathrm{ut}$ ...(1) $\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}=\mathrm{H}$ ...(2) $\mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{u}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}($ from $(1)$ and $(2))$ $\therefore \mathrm{h}=\mathrm{u} \times \frac{\mathrm{u}}{2 \mathrm{~g}}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \times \frac{\mathrm{u}^{2}}{4 \mathrm{~g}^{2}}$ $\quad=\frac{3 \mathrm{u}^{2}}{8 \mathrm{~g}}=\frac{3 \mathrm{H}}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two skaters $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{Q}$ are skating towards each other. Skater $\mathrm{P}$ throws a ball towards $\mathrm{W}$ every $5 \mathrm{~s}$ such that it always leaves her hand with speed $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ with respect to the ground. Consider two cases:
$\mathrm{(I)}$ $\mathrm{P}$ runs with speed $1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ towards $\mathrm{Q}$ while $\mathrm{Q}$ remains stationary
$\mathrm{(II)}$ $\mathrm{Q}$ runs with speed $1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ towards $\mathrm{P}$ while $\mathrm{P}$ remains stationary.
Note that irrespective of speed of $\mathrm{P}$, ball always leaves $\mathrm{P's}$ hand with speed $2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ with respect to the ground. Ignore gravity. Balls will be received by $\mathrm{Q}$
options: one every $2.5 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(I)}$ and one every $3.3 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(II)}$
one every $2 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(I)}$ and one every $4 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(II)}$
one every $3.3 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(I)}$ and one every $2.5 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(II)}$
one every $2.5 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(I)}$ and one every $2.5 \mathrm{~s}$ in case $\mathrm{(II)}$
questions.solution:
(I) Let initial distance between $\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{Q}$ is $\mathrm{x}$
at $\mathrm{t}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{2}$ a receive the ball. Next ball. $\begin{array}{l} t_{2}=\frac{x-5}{2}+5 \\ \Delta t=\frac{5}{2} \end{array}$ (II) in second case at $t=0$ $\mathrm{P}$ throws the ball.
$t_{1}=\frac{x}{3}$. Next ball $\begin{array}{l} t_{2}=\frac{x-5}{3}+5 \\ \Delta t=\frac{10}{3} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A boat crossing a river moves with a velocity $v$ relative to still water. The river is flowing with a velocity $v / 2$ with respect to the bank. The angle with respect to the flow direction with which the boat should move to minimize the drift is-
questions.solution: To minimize the drifting $\begin{array}{l} \sin \theta=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{w}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{bw}}}=\frac{1}{2} \\ \theta=30^{\circ} \\ 90^{\circ}+\theta=120^{\circ} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves in a plane along an elliptic path given by $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 .$ At point $(0, b)$, the $x$-component of velocity is $\mathrm{u}$. The y-component of acceleration at this point is-
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1 \\
u_{x}=u \text { at }(0, b) \\
u_{y}=0 \\
\frac{2 x}{a^{2}} \frac{d x}{d t}+\frac{2 y}{b^{2}} \frac{d y}{d t}=0
\end{array}$
Again diff. w.r.t. to time
$\frac{2 x}{a^{2}} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\frac{2}{a^{2}}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{2}+\begin{array}{ll}
2 y & d^{2} y \\
b^{2} & {d t^{2}}+\frac{2}{b^{2}}\left(\begin{array}{l}
d y \\
d t
\end{array}\right)=0
\end{array}$
acceleration at $(0, b)$ is
$a_{y}=\frac{-b}{a^{2}} u^{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A body of $0.5 \mathrm{~kg}$ moves along the positive $\mathrm{x}$-axis under the influence of a varying force $\mathrm{F}$ (in Newtons) as shown below.
If the speed of the object at $x=4 \mathrm{~m}$ in $3.16 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ then its speed at $x=8 \mathrm{~m}$ is
questions.solution:
According to work-energy principle
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{C}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{nc}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\Delta \mathrm{KE} \\
\int_{4}^{8} \mathrm{Fdx}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{f}}^{2}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}
\end{array}$
$\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 8-\frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times 4=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\left[v_{f}^{2}-(3.16)^{2}\right]$
$v_{f}=6.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{t}}=\mathrm{u}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{t} \\
\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{m} \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{c}
\end{array}$
stope will define the value gravity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is acted upon by a force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. Here, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The motion is one-dimensional. Then, the speed at which the particle acceleration is zero again, is
questions.solution:
Force on the object is F=k-βt2

Acceleration of the particle,

a=Fm=k-βt2m

Acceleration is zero when k=βt2 or t2=kβ or t2=82⇒t=2 s

Now, a=dvdt=k-βt2m⇒dv=k-βt2m·dt

Integrating between given limits, we have 

⇒∫v=-15vt=2 sdv=∫t=-2 st=2 sk-βt2mdt

=32∫t=-2t=28-2t2dt

vt=2 s--15=328t-2t33-22

⇒vat t=2s=-15+32×82--2-238--23

⇒vat t=2s=-15+3232-323

=-15+32643

=-15+32=17 ms-1

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small boy is throwing a ball towards a wall 6 in front of him. He releases the ball at a height of $1.4 \mathrm{~m}$ from the ground. The ball bounces from the wall at a height of $3 \mathrm{~m}$, rebounds from the ground and reaches the boy's hand exactly at the point of release. Assuming the two bounces (one from the wall and the other from the ground) to be perfectly elastic, how far ahead of the boy did the ball bounce from the ground?
questions.solution: $3=(6+x) \tan \theta\left[1-\frac{6+x}{12}\right]$ $3=\frac{(6+x)(6-x) \tan \theta}{12}$ $1.4=x \tan \theta\left[1-\frac{x}{12}\right]$ $1.4=\frac{x(12-x)}{12} \tan \theta$ $\frac{30}{14}=\frac{36-x^{2}}{12 x-x^{2}}$ $360 x-30 x^{2}=36 \times 14-14 x^{2}$ $16 x^{2}-360 x+36 \times 14=0$ $x=\frac{360 \pm \sqrt{(360)^{2}-4 \times 36 \times 14 \times 16}}{32}$ $x=\frac{360 \pm 312}{32}=\frac{48}{32}=1.5$

For the same range, another projection angle will be 90°-30°=60°

If the ball is suddenly stopped at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the magnitude of the displacement of the ball with respect to its position at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is closest to
questions.solution:
Time period of rotation of ball =1.5 s

So, in time interval of 7.5 s(=1.5×5)s ball completes 5 revolutions. Also, ball covers one-fourth of circular path in time 1.54=0.375 s
So, in remaining 0.8 s(=8.3-7.5 s) ball is very near to other end of diameter as shown in the figure.

Cleary, displacement of ball is nearly equals to diameter (=2 m) of circular path.

questions.q: In a circus, a performer throws an apple towards a hoop held at 45 m height by another performer standing on a high platform (see figure below). The thrower aim s for the hoop and throws the apple with a speed of 24 m s-1. At the exact moment that the thrower released the apple, the other performer drops the hoop. The hoop falls straight down. At what height above the ground does the apple go through the hoop?
questions.solution:
Velocity of projection =24 m s-1

Distance between point of projection and hoop =252+452

∴ Time taken by ball to reach the hoop

=252+45224

(Note :- We are analysing the motion wrt hoop)

∴ Distance by which hoop will fall

=12at2=12×10×252+452242

∴ Height above the ground where apple go through the hoop is given by

45-12×10×252+452242=22 m

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wheel of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is trapped in a mud pit and spinning. As the wheel is spinning, it splashes mud blobs with initial speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> from various points on its circumference. questions.solution:
Let a mud blob is detached from the circumference of wheel with initial speed u at angle θ as shown below.

Height upto which mud blob can be thrown is

h= Maximum height of projectile+Height at which mud blob is thrown

h=u2sin2θ2g+Rcosθ....i
h is maximum when dhdθ=0

⇒ddθu2sin2θ2g+Rcosθ

⇒u22g.2sinθcosθ-Rsinθ=0

⇒u2g·cosθ=R or cosθ=Rgu2

⇒sin2θ=1-R2g2u2

Substituting these values in Eq (i), we get

λmax=u22g1-R2g2u4+RRgu2

=u22g-R2g2u2+R2gu2=u22g+R2g2u2

questions.q: A thin metallic disc is rotating with constant angular velocity about a vertical axis that is perpendicular to its plane and passes through its centre. The rotation causes the free electrons in the disc to redistribute. Assume that, there is no external electric or magnetic field. Then,
options: a point on the rim of the disc is at a higher potential than its centre
a point on the rim of the disc is at a lower potential than its centre
a point on the rim of the disc is at the same potential as its centre
the potential in the material has an extremum between centre and the rim
questions.solution:
Centrifugal action causes electrons to accumulate around rim of disc.

So, rim of disc is at a negative potential with respect to centre of disc. So, points of rim are at lower potential than its centre.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface of a planet is found to be uniformly charged. When a particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and no charge is thrown at an angle from the surface of the planet, it has a parabolic trajectory as in projectile motion with horizontal range $\mathrm{L}$. A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $\mathrm{q}$, with the same initial conditions has a range $\mathrm{L} / 2$. The range of particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $2 \mathrm{q}$ with the same initial conditions is-
questions.solution:
For uncharged particle
$\mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$ ...(i)
Range for particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $\mathrm{q}$.
$\frac{L}{2}=\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g+\frac{q E}{m}}$ ...(ii)
From (i) and (ii)
$\begin{array}{l}
\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{2 g}=\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g+\frac{q E}{m}} \\
\Rightarrow m g=q E
\end{array}$
Range of particle of mass $m \&$ charge $2 \mathrm{q}$.
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}+\frac{2 \mathrm{q} \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{m}}}=\frac{\mathrm{u}^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{\mathrm{g}\left(1+\frac{2 \mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{mg}}\right)}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car goes around uniform circular track of radius $R$ at a uniform speed $v$ once in every $T$ seconds. The magnitude of the centripetal acceleration is $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}$ If the car now goes uniformly around a larger circular track of radius $2 R$ and experiences a centripetal acceleration of magnitude $8 \mathrm{a}_{c}$, then its time period is
questions.solution: $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{V}}$ $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $8 \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime 2}}{2 \mathrm{R}}$ (8) $\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{\prime 2}}{2 \mathrm{R}}$ $\mathrm{V}^{\prime 2}=16 \mathrm{~V}^{2}$ $\mathrm{~V}^{\prime}=4 \mathrm{~V}$ $\therefore$ Time period $=\frac{(2 \pi) \mathrm{R}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{V}^{\prime}}$ $=\frac{(2 \pi) 2 \mathrm{R}}{4 \mathrm{~V}}$ $=\frac{\pi \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{V}}$ $=(\mathrm{T} / 2)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Half life of a particle of mass $1.6 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg}$ is $6.9 \mathrm{~s}$ and a stream of such particles is travelling with the K.E. of a particle being $0.05 \mathrm{eV}$. The fraction of particles which will decay when they travel a distance of $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is :

$\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\lambda \mathrm{dt}$
$\frac{6.93}{6.9} \times 10^{-4}$
$=10^{-4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nucleus has a half-life of 30 minutes. At $3 \mathrm{PM}$ its decay rate was measured as 120,000 counts/sec. What will be the decay rate at $5 \mathrm{PM} ?$
questions.solution:
Given $\mathrm{T}=30$ minutes. $\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{dt}}=120 \mathrm{~K} \frac{\text { counts }}{\mathrm{sec}}$
After each half life, activity is reduced to half therefore after $\mathrm{n}$ half lives activity reduces to $\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$.
Also $\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \mathrm{N}$
$\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}$ at $5 \mathrm{P} . \mathrm{M}$. will be equal to activity remaining after four half lives.
i.e. $\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{4}=\left(\frac{1}{16} \mathrm{th}\right)$ of the initial activity
$\left(\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{\text {at } 5 \mathrm{PM}}=\left(\frac{1}{16}\right.$ th $)$ of the initial activity
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{5 \mathrm{P}_{. \mathrm{M}}}=\left(\frac{1}{16}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right)_{3 \mathrm{PM}}
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The nuclear radius is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><msup><mi>A</mi><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is constant and A is the atomic mass number. Then, the nuclear mass density of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mn>238</mn></msup></math> is
questions.solution:
Given, nuclear radius is
R=r0A13
Here, atomic mass number of nucleus =A
∴ Nuclear density d is given by d= Mass number  Volume 

⇒  d=A43πR3=A43πr0A133
⇒  d=A43πr03·A=34πr03

As r0= a constant, so nuclear density is a constant quantity.
∴ Nuclear mass density of U238 is same as that of Sn119

$$
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{Z}}^{\mathrm{A}} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{Z}+1}^{\mathrm{A}}+\beta^{-}+\overline{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{e}}
$$

questions.solution: No Solution

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Carbon-11 decays to boron-11 according to the following formula.
$$
{ }_{6}^{11} C \rightarrow{ }_{5}^{11} B+e^{+}+v_{e}+0.96 \mathrm{MeV}
$$
Assume that positrons $\left(\mathrm{e}^{+}\right)$produced in the decay combine with free electrons in the atmosphere and annihilate each other almost immediately. Also assume that the neutrinos $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ are massless and do not intersect with the environment. At $\mathrm{t}=0$ we have 1 pg of ${ }_{6}^{12} C .$ If the half-life of the decay process is $t_{0}$, the net energy produced between time 
$\mathrm{t}=0$ and $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{t}_{0}$ will be nearly
questions.solution:
Amount decay $=0.75 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{gm}$
Number of atoms $=0.4 \times 10^{17}$
Energy(in nuclear reaction) $=0.96 \times$ number of atoms
$\approx 4 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{MeV}$
By $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{mc}^{2}$
As 1 electron from reaction annihilate by another electrons from atmosphere so
Energy from annihilation $=2 \mathrm{mc}^{2} \approx 4 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{MeV}$
Total energy $=8 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{MeV}$

questions.q:
The graph shows the log of activity logR of a radioactive material as a function of time t in minutes.

The half-life (in minute) for the decay is closest to
questions.solution:
Activity of a radioactive sample is given by

R=-dNdt=-ddtN0e-λt=λN0·e-λt

So,logR=logλN0+loge-λt

⇒logR=-λt+logλN0

This equation is form of y=mx+C

So, absolute value of slope of logR versus t graph gives decay constant λ.

Now, from graph,

We get, slope =8-68-16=14=λ

So, half-life time period of sample is

T1/2=log2λ=0.6931/4≈3.0 min

questions.q: A ball is rolling without slipping in a spherical shallow bowl (radius $\mathrm{R}$ ) as shown in the figure and is executing simple harmonic motion. If the radius of the ball is doubled, the period of oscillation
options: is reduced by a factor of $1 / 2$
is increased by a factor of 2
questions.solution:
$m g \sin \theta-F_{r}=m a$
$F_{r}=\frac{2}{5} m r^{2} \frac{a}{r^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow a=\frac{5}{7} \frac{g \sin \theta}{R-r}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{5 g}{7(R-r)}}$
$\Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{7(R-r)}{5 r}}$

questions.solution: $\omega_{1}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ $\omega_{2}=\frac{2 \pi}{24}$ time for meet (closest) $=\frac{\pi(2 \pi+1)}{\omega_{1}+\omega_{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3}(2 n+1)$ $\mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{3} \sec , 4, \frac{20}{3}, \frac{28}{3}, 12$ time of farthest $\Rightarrow \frac{2 \pi n}{\omega_{1}+\omega_{2}}$ $\mathrm{t} \neq 2 \mathrm{n} / \frac{4}{3}$

questions.q: A point particle is acted upon by a restoring force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The time period of oscillation is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> when the amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The time period for an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> will be:

Given F=-kx3
-dUdx=-kx3

⇒U=14kx4
∴ Energy of oscillations will be
E=12mv2+U=12 mdxdt2+14kx4…1
If we pull dxdt=0 in above equation, we will
get amplitude as A=4Ek4…2

dt=±dxm2E1-k4Ex4-1/2
Now, use A=4Ek4, to reduce above equation
as dt=±dx2 mkA-21-xA4-1/2
The time period can be found by integrating above equation.
T=4∫0Adx2 mkA-21-xA4-1/2

=42mkA-2∫0A1-xA4-1/2·dx

T=42 mkA-1(I)

questions.solution: According to law of conservation of mechanical energy $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{f}}+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}} \\ 0+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=0+\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}} \\ \mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{f}} \end{array}$ Point $\mathrm{D}$ is at line $\mathrm{AB}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two masses $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{m}_{2}$ connected by a spring of spring constant $\mathrm{k}$ rest on a frictionless surface. If the masses are pulled apart and let go, the time period of oscillation is-
questions.solution: Let the masses be slightly displaced by $\mathrm{x}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{x}_{2}$ from this equilibrium position in opposite direction so net stretch in spring is $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}$. Because of this a restoring force $\mathrm{kx}$ will act on each mass and therefore equation for $\mathrm{m}_{1} \& \mathrm{~m}_{2}$ will be $$ \mathrm{m}_{1} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=-\mathrm{kx} \quad \text { and } \quad \mathrm{m}_{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{2}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=-\mathrm{kx} $$ but as $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}_{1}+\mathrm{x}_{2}$ $$ \therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{2}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}} $$ replacing values of $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{1}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{2}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}$ from acceleration equations we get $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}_{2}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=-\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}\right) \mathrm{kx}$ also if $\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}_{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~m}}$ then $\mathrm{m}$ is the effective mass in this case therefore $\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^{2}}=-\omega^{2} \mathrm{x}=\frac{-\mathrm{kx}}{\mathrm{m}}$ or $\omega^{2}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{m}}$ and $\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}$ $$ \therefore \sqrt{\Gamma=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m_{1} m_{2}}{\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right) k}}} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light balloon filled with helium of density $\rho_{\mathrm{He}}$ is tied to a long light string of length $\ell$ and the string is attached to the ground. If the balloon is displaced slightly in the horizontal direction from the equilibrium and released then.
options: The ballon undergoes simple harmonic motion with period $2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{\rho_{\text {air }}}{\rho_{\text {air }}-\rho_{\text {He }}}\right) \frac{\ell}{g}}$
The ballon undergoes simple harmonic motion with period $2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{\rho_{\text {air }}-\rho_{\text {He }}}{\rho_{\text {air }}}\right) \frac{\ell}{g}}$
The ballon undergoes simple harmonic motion with period $2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}{\rho_{\text {air }}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}\right) \frac{\ell}{\mathrm{g}}}$
The ballon undergoes conical oscillations with period $2 \pi \sqrt{\left(\frac{\rho_{\text {air }}+\rho_{\text {He }}}{\rho_{\text {air }}-\rho_{\text {He }}}\right) \frac{\ell}{g}}$
questions.solution: $$ \tau_{0}=\mathrm{V}\left(\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}\right) \mathrm{g} \ell \sin \theta $$ For small angular displacement $(\theta)$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \tau_{0}=\mathrm{V}\left(\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}\right) \mathrm{g} \ell \theta \\ \mathrm{I} \alpha=\mathrm{V}\left[\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}\right] \mathrm{g} \ell \theta \\ \rho_{\mathrm{He}} \mathrm{V} \ell^{2} \alpha=\mathrm{V}\left[\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}\right] \ell \theta \mathrm{g} \\ \alpha=\left[\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}\right] \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell} \theta \\ \omega=\sqrt{\left(\frac{\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}{\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}\right) \frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell}} \\ =2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\ell}{\mathrm{g}} \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{He}}}{\left(\rho_{\mathrm{Air}}-\rho_{\mathrm{He}}\right)}} \end{array} $$

questions.q: If the speed $(v)$ of the bob in a simple pendulum is plotted against the tangential acceleration $(a)$, the correct graph will be represented by -
questions.solution:
In SHM
$\mathrm{a}=-\omega^{2} \mathrm{x}$
$\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{v}=\omega \sqrt{\mathrm{A}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}} \\
\mathrm{v}^{2}=\omega^{2}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{2}-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \\
\mathrm{v}^{2}=\omega^{2} \mathrm{~A}^{2}-\omega^{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\omega^{2}} \\
\mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\omega^{2}}=\omega^{2} \mathrm{~A}^{2} \\
\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{\omega^{2} \mathrm{~A}^{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{\omega^{4} \mathrm{~A}^{2}}=1
\end{array}$

questions.solution: $\begin{array}{l} \cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{L}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{Rx}} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}=2 \mathrm{Rx} \operatorname{cosec} \theta+\mathrm{L}^{2}-\mathrm{R}^{2} \end{array}$ displacement of S.H. M. is in the form of $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{c}$ Therefore it is not S. H. M. It is S. H. M. only which. It $\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{R}$. $\begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}^{2}=2 \mathrm{Rx} \cos \theta \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{R} \cos \theta \\ \mathrm{x}=2 \mathrm{R} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}[\text { S.H. M.] } \end{array}$ But period of this motion is $\mathrm{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which one of the following parts of the eye is affected in cataract?
questions.solution: Cataract affects the lens in the eye. It occurs due to the clouding of the crystalline lens and prevents light and image from reaching the retina. A cataract makes a person vision blurry and less colourful. There is partial or complete loss of vision due to the lens becoming milky and cloudy.

questions.q: Four ray 1,2,3 and 4 are incident normally on the face <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi></math> of an isosceles prism <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Q</mi><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math> with apex angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>120</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The refractive indices of the material of the prism for the above rays 1,2,3 and 4 are 1.85 , 1.95,2.05 and 2.15 respectively and the surrounding medium is air. questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider two equivalent, triangular hollow prisms A and B made of thin glass plates and arranged with negligible spacing as shown in the figure. A beam of white light is incident on prism A from the left. Given that, the refractive index of water is inversely related to temperature, the beam to the right of prism B would not appear white, if
options: both prisms are filled with hot water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>70</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
both prisms are filled with cold water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>7</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

prism A is filled with hot water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>70</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and prism B with cold water <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>7</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
Prism B is inverted relative to prism A. So, dispersion of light caused by prism A and B is in opposite direction.

If bending of light caused by B is less than or more than that of A, then out going beam of light is not white. So, when both prism are filled with water at different temperatures, their refractive indices are different and the dispersion produced by A and B are not equal and opposite. Hence, with condition in
(d) beam to right of prism B will be coloured.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monochromatic beam of light enters a square enclosure with mirrored interior surfaces at an angle of incidence θi=(≠0) (see the figure below). For some value(s) of θi, the beam is reflected by every mirrored wall (other than the one with opening) exactly once and exists the enclosure through the same hole. options: The beam will not come out the enclosure for any value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mi>i</mi></math>.

The beam will come out for exactly two values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mi>i</mi></math>.
questions.solution:
From geometry, α+θi=π2⇒α=π2-θi

β+α=π2⇒β=θi

β+γ=π2⇒γ=π2-θi

Also AD∥BC & AB∥CD

∴ ABCD is a parallelogram & AB=CD

Also, ∆ABQ≅∆CDS

∴ From trignometry

AQQB=CRBR

Let length of each side of square by l, AQ=x & QB=y

∴ xy=l-xl-y⇒x=y

∴ θi=π4

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider a glass cube slab of dielectric bound by the planes x=0, x=a ; y=0, y=b ; z=0, z=c ; with
b>a>c. The slab is placed in air and has a refractive index of n. The minimum value for n, such that all rays entering the dielectric at y=0 reach y=b is
questions.solution:
Planes bounding the dielectric are as shown below.


Due to dielectric, a light ray incident at y=0, at some angle θ bends towards normal and it strikes plane x=a.

If angle of incidence at x=a, is more than angle of critical incidence, TIR occurs and light ray, then emerges out of plane y=b.


For TIR at

x=a sin90°-r=1n or cosr=1n....i

Also, n=sinθsinr
Now θ at y=0, varies from 0° to a maximum of 90°

So, for limiting incidence,

n=sin90°sinr⇒n=1sinr....ii

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

sinr=cosr⇒r=45°

Substituting r=45° in Eq. (i) or Eq. (ii),

we get nmin=1cos45°=2

Consider the following vision defects listed in Columns I and II and the corrective measures in Column III. Choose the correct combination.

   Column I    Column II    Column III
 P.  Hypermetropia  (i)  Near-sightedness  a.  Convex lens
 Q.  Myopia  (i)  Far-sightedness  b.  Concave lens
questions.solution:
Hypermetropia is far-sightedness, a vision condition in which nearby objects are blurry. It is corrected by using convex lens or positive power lens.

Myopia is near-sightedness, a condition in which close objects appear clearly but far ones do not. It is corrected using concave lens or negative power lens.

In hypermetropia, image is formed behind the retina, while in myopia image is formed in front of the retina.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The image of an object O due to reflection from the surface of a lake is elongated due to the ripples on the water surface caused by a light breeze. This is because the ripples act as tilted mirrors as shown below. Consider the case, where O and the observer E are at the same height above the surface of the lake. If the maximum angle that the ripples make with the horizontal is α, then the angular extent δ of the image will be
questions.solution:
We can consider ripples as plane mirrors reflecting light to form image with angular extent δ.

It is given O and E are at same level, so OEBC is a trapezium with OE||BC.

Now, different angles of trapezium OEBC are as shown below.

∠1=90°-β-α

∠2=90°-γ-α

∠OPB=∠EPC

and ∠OPE=∠BOC

⇒90°-β-α=90°-γ-α

⇒β=γ....i

As, ∠OPC=180°

⇒180°=β+γ+2α+180-2γ-δ

⇒2α+β-γ-δ=0

⇒δ=2α  [∵β=r from Eq. (i) ]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Select the correct statement about rainbow.
options: We can see a rainbow in the western sky in the late afternoon
The double rainbow has red on the inside and violet on the outside
A rainbow has an arc shape, since the earth is round
A rainbow on the moon is violet on the inside and red on the outside
questions.solution:
In late afternoon rainbow is visible in east side when light of sun in west side is reflected and refracted by a layer of water droplets. Rainbow is circular because locous of reflected rays reaching eye of observer is a circle. Its roundness is not due to roundness of earth.
There is no rainbow on moon due to lack of atmosphere. In case of a primary rainbow, violet colour is on inside and red colour is on outside of arc. In case of a secondary rainbow, red colour is on inside and violet colour is on outside of arc.

So, none of the option is correct. Option
(b) is correct, if only secondary rainbow is considered.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass beaker is filled with water up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is kept on top of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> thick glass slab. When a coin at the bottom of the glass slab is viewed at the normal incidence from above the beaker, its apparent depth from the water surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo mathvariant="italic"> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi><mo mathvariant="italic">.</mo></math> Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> is close to (the refractive indices of water and glass are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>33</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> , respectively)
questions.solution:
Apparent depth d in case of more than one medium is

d=d1μ1+d2μ2+  ... (i)

where, d1 and d2 are the thickness of slabs of medium with refractive index μ1 and μ2, respectively.

Here, d1=5 cm, μ1=133

 

d2=2 cm, μ2=15

Substituting these values in Eq. (i),

we get

Apparent depth, d=5133+215

=5.088 cm=5.1 cm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Back surface of a glass (refractive index $\mathrm{n}$ and thickness $\mathrm{t}$ ) is polished to work as a mirror as shown below. A laser beam falls on it and is partially reflected and refracted at the air-glass interface and fully reflected at the mirror surface respectively. A pattern of discrete spots of light is observed on the screen.
The spacing between the spots on the screen will be
questions.solution: Therefore $\Delta \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{h}_{1}-\mathrm{h}_{2}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{h}_{1}} \& \tan \theta=\frac{\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{h}_{2}} \\ \Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\tan \theta}-\frac{\mathrm{d}-\mathrm{L}}{\tan \theta} \\ \Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\tan \theta} \end{array} $$ Snell's Law $$ \begin{array}{l} 1 \cdot \sin \theta=\mathrm{n} \cdot \sin \mathrm{r} \Rightarrow 1 \sin \mathrm{r}=\frac{\sin \theta}{\mathrm{n}} \\ \tan \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{2 . \mathrm{t}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=2 \mathrm{t} \tan \mathrm{r} \end{array} $$ $$ \mathrm{L}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t} \cdot \sin \theta}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}^{2}-\sin ^{2} \theta}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \therefore \Delta \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t} \sin \theta}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}^{2}-\sin ^{2} \theta} \tan \theta} \\ \tan \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{t} \cos \theta}{\sqrt{\mathrm{n}^{2}-\sin ^{2} \theta}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A glass prism has a right-triangular cross section $\mathrm{ABC}$, with $\angle \mathrm{A}=90^{\circ}$. A ray of light parallel to the hypotenuse BC and incident on the side AB emerges grazing the side AC. Another ray, again parallel to the hypotenuse BC, incident on the side AC suffers total internal reflection at the side $\mathrm{AB}$. Which one of the following must be true about the refractive index $\mu$ of the material of the prism?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{r}+\theta_{\mathrm{C}}=90^{\circ}......\mathrm{(1)}$ $1 \times \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\alpha\right)=\mu \sin \mathrm{r}$ $\cos \alpha=\mu \sin \mathrm{r}......\mathrm{(2)}$ $90^{\circ}-\mathrm{e}>\theta_{\mathrm{C}}......\mathrm{(3)}$ $\mu \sin \mathrm{e}=1 \times \sin \alpha......\mathrm{(4)}$ $(3) \&(4)$ $90^{\circ}-\theta_{C}>\mathrm{e}$ $\cos \theta_{\mathrm{C}}>\sin \mathrm{e}$ $\cos \theta_{\mathrm{C}}>\frac{\sin \alpha}{\mu}$ $1-\sin ^{2} \theta_{\mathrm{C}}>\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}\left[1-\mu^{2} \sin ^{2} \mathrm{r}\right]$ $1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}>\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}\left[1-\mu^{2} \sin ^{2}\left(90^{\circ}-\theta_{\mathrm{C}}\right)\right]$ $1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}>\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}-\cos ^{2} \theta_{\mathrm{C}}$ $1-\frac{2}{\mu^{2}}>-\left[1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}\right]$ $2>\frac{3}{\mu^{2}}$ $\mu>\sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$ $(1) \&(2)$ $\cos \alpha=\mu \sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta_{C}\right)$ $\cos \alpha=\mu \cos \theta_{C}$ $\cos \alpha < 1$ $\mu \cos \theta_{C} < 1$ $\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}}} < \frac{1}{\mu}$ $1-\frac{1}{\mu^{2}} < \frac{1}{\mu^{2}}$ $1 < \frac{2}{\mu^{2}}$ $\mu < \sqrt{2}$ $\therefore \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}} < \mu < \sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A rectangular block is composed of three different glass prisms (with refractive indices $\mu_{1}, \mu_{2}$ and $\mu_{3}$ ) as shown in the figure below. A ray of light incident normal to the left face emerges normal to the right face. Then the refractive indices are related by
questions.solution: for surface $\mathrm{AB}$ : $\mu_{1} \sin 45^{\circ}=\mu_{2} \sin \theta_{1}$ $\ldots$ (1) for surface $\mathrm{AC}$ : $\mu_{2} \sin \left(\omega-\theta_{1}\right)=\mu_{3} \sin 45$ $\mu_{3} \sin 45^{\circ}=\mu_{2} \cos \theta_{1}$ $\ldots$ (2) $\omega=90^{\circ}$ Squaring and adding equation (1) \& (2) $\frac{\mu_{1}^{2}}{2}+\frac{\mu_{3}^{2}}{2}=\mu_{2}^{2} \Rightarrow \mu_{1}^{2}+\mu_{3}^{2}=2 \mu_{2}^{2}$

questions.q: A point source of light is moving at a rate of $2 \mathrm{~cm}-\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ towards a thin convex lens of focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ along its optical axis. When the source is $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ away from the lens the image is moving at
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{-v^{2}}{u^{2}} \frac{d u}{d t} \\
\& \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{F}+\frac{1}{u} \\
\frac{1}{\mathrm{~V}}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{-15} \\
\frac{1}{v}=\frac{3-2}{30} \\
\mathrm{v}=30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\left(\frac{30}{15}\right)^{2} \cdot 2
$$
$=8 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ away from lens

questions.solution: $\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$ $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f m}-\frac{2}{f \mid}$ $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{2}{10}=-\frac{1}{5}$ $f=-5$ $\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-30}=-\frac{1}{5}$ $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{30}$ $=\frac{-6+1}{30}=\frac{-5}{30}$ $v=-6 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: It was found that the refractive index of material of a certain prism varied as $1.5+0.004 / \lambda^{2}$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of light used to measure the refractive index. The same material was then used to construct a thin prism of apex angle $10^{\circ}$. Angles of minimum deviation $\left(\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\right)$ of the prism were recorded for the sources with wavelengths $\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2}$ respectively. Then
options: $\delta_{\mathrm{m}}$ is the same in both the cases.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m}}+\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}$
$1.5+\frac{0.004}{\lambda^{2}}=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\delta_{\mathrm{m}}+\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\mathrm{A}}{2}\right)}$
$\delta_{\mathrm{m}} \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$
$\therefore \delta_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\lambda_{1}\right)>\delta_{\mathrm{m}}\left(\lambda_{2}\right)$ if $\lambda_{1} < \lambda_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hollow lens is made of thin glass and in the shape of a double concave lens. It can be filled with air, water of refractive index $1.33$ or $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ of refractive index $1.6$. It will act as a diverging lens if it is
options: filled with air and immersed in water.
filled with water and immersed in $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$.
filled with air and immersed in $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$
filled with $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ and immersed in water.
questions.solution:
$\mu_{\text {air }}=1$ $\mu_{\text {water }}=1.33$ $\mu_{\mathrm{cs}_{2}}=1.6$
$\begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}-1\right)\left[-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right] \\ \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=-\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right] \end{array}$ for diverging lens $\mathrm{f}$ must be $-\mathrm{ve}$. $\therefore$ for this $\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}>1$ $\mathrm{n}_{2}>\mathrm{n}_{1}$ $\therefore$ Lens should be filled with liquid which has more refractive index in comparison to liquid in which lens is immersed. $\therefore$ Ans (D) is the correct option as $\mu_{\alpha_{2}}>\mu_{\text {water }}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Frosted glass is widely used for translucent windows. The region, where a transparent adhesive tape is stuck over the frosted glass becomes transparent. The most reasonable explanation for this is
options: diffusion of adhesive glue into glass
chemical reaction at adhesive tape-glass interface
refractive index of adhesive glue is close to that of glass
adhesive tape is more transparent than glass
questions.solution:
Frosted glass has a rough layer which causes irregular refraction and makes glass translucent.

When a transparent tape which has refractive index close to that of glass is pasted over the rough surface of glass, the tape glue fills the roughness of glass. This makes glass surface more smooth and so refraction is more regular. This makes region of tape transparent.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:

Then-
questions.solution:
At first incidence light is deviated towards the normal therefore $\mu_{2}>\mu_{1} .$ Also at second incidence TIR takes place therefore $\mu_{2}>\mu_{3}$, also $\mu_{1}>\mu_{3}$ because for the same angle in medium $\mu_{2}$, angle in $\mu_{1}$ medium is less.
$\therefore \mu_{3} < \mu_{1} < \mu_{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Remote sensing satellites move in an orbit that is at an average height of about 500 km from the surface of the earth. The camera onboard one such satellite has a screen of area A on which the images captured by it are formed. If the focal length of the camera lens is 50 cm, then the terrestrial area that can be observed from the satellite is close to
questions.solution:
Consider the given diagram,

Assuming area observed and screen both circular, we have

θ1=θ2⇒d1f=d2h⇒d2d1=hf

where, d1= diameter of camera screen and d2=diameter of area on earth.

Now,  area observed on earth  area of screen =A0A

=π·d224π·d124=d22d12

⇒  A0A=hf12=500×10+350×10-22

=10×103×1022=1062=1012

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One end of a rod of length $L=1 \mathrm{~m}$ is fixed to a point on the circumference of a wheel of radius $\mathrm{R}=1 / \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m}$. The other end is sliding freely along a straight channel passing through the center $\mathrm{O}$ of the wheel as shown in the figure below. The wheel is rotating with a constant angular velocity $\omega$ about $\mathrm{O}$.
questions.solution: $\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{1 / \sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{3}=\tan \theta$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{x}=\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{3}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$ $\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{R}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{L}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{Rx}}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}^{2}+\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{L}^{2}=2 \mathrm{Rx} \cos \theta$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{R}\left[\mathrm{x}(-\sin \theta)+\cos \theta \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right] \\ & \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}[\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{R} \cos \theta]=-\mathrm{Rx} \sin \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \\ \therefore \quad-&{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\mathrm{v} \& \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=\omega\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{R} \mathrm{x} \sin \theta \omega}{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{R} \cos \theta}$ $\begin{aligned} &=\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \omega}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\frac{\omega}{\sqrt{3}}}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \frac{3}{2}}=\frac{2 \omega}{3} \\ \mathrm{v}=& \frac{2 \omega}{3} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform thin wooden plank AB of length $\mathrm{L}$ and mass $\mathrm{M}$ is kept on a table with its $\mathrm{B}$ end slightly outside the edge of the table. When an impulse $\mathrm{J}$ is given to the end $\mathrm{B}$, the plank moves up with centre of mass rising a distance 'h' from the surface of the table. Then-
questions.solution: Case-I Considering angular momentum w.r.t. end A $\omega=$ Angular velocity just after impulse then $$ (\omega)\left(\frac{\mathrm{ML}^{2}}{3}\right)=\mathrm{J}(\mathrm{L}) $$ $$ \omega=\frac{3 \mathrm{~J}}{\mathrm{ML}} $$ $\Rightarrow$ velocity of $\mathrm{CM}$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{1}}=\frac{\omega \mathrm{L}}{2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~J}}{2 \mathrm{M}}......(1) $$ Case-II Apply conservation o moment, if $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{2}}=$ Velocity of $\mathrm{CM}$ just after impulse then $\mathrm{M} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{2}}=\mathrm{J}$ $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{M}}......(2) $$ comparing (1) \& (2) $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{2}} < \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}_{1}} $$ $\Rightarrow$ velocity of CM just after impulse would be between above two extreme values with (1), $\mathrm{Mgh}_{\max } < \frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{M})\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{~J}}{2 \mathrm{M}}\right)^{2}$ $$ \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{h}_{\max } < \frac{9 \mathrm{~J}^{2}}{8 \mathrm{M}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}......(3) $$ with (2), $\mathrm{Mgh}_{\max }>\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{M}\left(\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{M}}\right)^{2}$ $$ \mathrm{h}_{\max }>\frac{\mathrm{J}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{M}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}......(4) $$ Use (3) \& (4) Hence $\frac{\mathrm{J}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{M}^{2} \mathrm{~g}} < \mathrm{h}_{\max } < \frac{9 \mathrm{~J}^{2}}{8 \mathrm{M}^{2} \mathrm{~g}}$

options: It is opposite to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}_{\mathrm{CM}}$ at the points $\mathrm{P}_{1}, \mathrm{P}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical rigid ball is released from rest and starts rolling down an inclined plane from height h=7 m, as shown in the figure. It hits a block at rest on the horizontal plane (assume elastic collision). If the mass of both the ball and the block is m and the ball is rolling without sliding, then the speed of the block after collision is close to
questions.solution:
As collision is elastic and masses of colliding objects are equal, so there is exchange of total energy of moving mass to stationary mass. 

Hence, velocity of block = velocity of sphere just before collision. 

For sphere, if v= velocity of translation of centre of mass at the bottom of plane and ω= angular speed, then by energy conservation, we have

mgh=12mv2+12Iω2

Here, ω=vR and I=25mR2

Substituting and solving for velocity, we have 

v=107gh=10×10×77=10 m/s

∴ Velocity of block after collision =10 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A table has a heavy circular top of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> placed on four light (considered massless) legs placed symmetrically on its circumference. The maximum mass that can be kept anywhere on the table without toppling it is close to
questions.solution:
From the above figure, we can see that due to mass placed at edge of table, it tends to rotate about a line joining two legs. Hence, we take pivot in balancing of weight of mass m and weight of mass M of table at point B. In critical condition,

Moment of weight of mass m about B= Moment of weight of table about B

⇒mg·AB=Mg·BC

⇒m=M·BCAB........(i)

From geometry of table,

BC=Rcos45°=R2

So, AB=R-R2

=R1-12

Hence, from Eq. (i), we have

m=M×R/2R1-12

Here, M=20 kg

∴m=202-1≈200.4≈50 kg

Nearest value is m=47 kg.

questions.solution: For one arm to remain horizontal the net torque about $\mathrm{O}$ must be zero (in the position shown in the figure) for this $\mathrm{OP}=\mathrm{OQ}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{OQ}=\frac{\ell}{2} \cos \theta$ from figure $\mathrm{AE}=\mathrm{AC}+\mathrm{CE}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{AE}=\ell \cos \theta+\mathrm{OQ}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{\ell}{2}=\ell \cos \theta+\frac{\ell}{2} \cos \theta$ $\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{3}$ hence $\theta=\cos ^{-1}(1 / 3)$ correct Answer is (A)

questions.q: A horizontal disk of moment of inertia $4.25 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^{2}$ with respect to its axis of symmetry is spinning counter clockwise at 15 revolutions per second about its axis, as viewed from above. A second disk of moment of inertia $1.80 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{m}^{2}$ with respect to its axis of symmetry is spinning clockwise at 25 revolutions per second as viewed from above about the same axis and is dropped on top of the first disk. Take anticlockwise positive
$$

4.25 \times 15-1.80 \times 25=6.05 \times \omega
\end{array}
$$
$\omega=3$ revolutions/second and clockwise

questions.q: Figure below shows a small mass connected to a string, which is attached to a vertical post. If the ball is released when the string is horizontal as shown, the magnitude of the total acceleration (including radial and tangential) of the mass as a function of the angle $\theta$ is- Position
questions.solution: Radical acceleration $=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~g} \ell \sin \theta}{\ell}=2 \mathrm{~g} \sin \theta$ tangential acceleration $=g \cos \theta$ total acceleration $=\sqrt{4 \mathrm{~g}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta+\mathrm{g}^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta} \Rightarrow \mathrm{g} \sqrt{1+3 \sin ^{2} \theta}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid cube of wood of side $2 \mathrm{a}$ and mass $\mathrm{M}$ is resting on a horizontal surface as shown in the figure. The cube is free to rotate about the fixed axis $\mathrm{AB}$. A bullet of mass $\mathrm{m}(< < \mathrm{M})$ and speed $\mathrm{v}$ is shot horizontally at the face opposite to $\mathrm{ABCD}$ at a height ' $\mathrm{h}$ ' above the surface to impart the cube an angular speed $\omega_{\mathrm{c}}$ so that the cube just topples over. Then $\omega_{\mathrm{c}}$ is (note : the moment of inertia of the cube about an axis perpendicular to the face and passing through the center of mass is $2 \mathrm{Ma}^{3} / 3$ )
questions.solution: conservation of energy $$ \begin{array}{l} \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}} \omega_{\mathrm{c}}^{2}=\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2}-\mathrm{a}) \\ \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{cm}}+\mathrm{Ma}^{2}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{Ma}^{2}+\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{a} \sqrt{2})^{2} \\ \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{8}{9} \mathrm{Ma}^{2} \\ \frac{1}{2} \frac{8}{3} \mathrm{Ma}^{2} \omega_{\mathrm{c}}^{2}=\operatorname{Mg} \mathrm{a}(\sqrt{2}-1) \\ \omega_{\mathrm{c}}=\sqrt{3 \mathrm{~g}(\sqrt{2}-1) / 4 \mathrm{a}} \end{array} $$

It strike the face and embeds in the cube. Then $\omega_{\mathrm{c}}$ is close to (note : the moment of inertia of the cube about an axis perpendicular to the face and passing through the center of mass is $2 \mathrm{Ma}^{2} / 3$ )
questions.solution: $\therefore \mathrm{mvr}=\mathrm{I} \omega$ $\Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{mv}\left(\frac{4 \mathrm{a}}{3}\right)}{\frac{8}{3} \mathrm{Ma}^{2}}$ $=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{2 \mathrm{Ma}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train is moving slowly at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> next to a railway platform. A man, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall, alights from the train such that his feet are fixed on the ground. Taking him to be a rigid body, the instantaneous angular velocity (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>rad</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>) is
questions.solution:
As man alight, its angular momentum about a point on platform is conserved. 

By angular momentum conservation,

Linitial =Lfinal ⇒mvr=Iω

⇒mvr=ml23·ω⇒3vrl2=ω

or ω=3×2×1.521.52=2 rad/s

So, instantaneous angular speed of man =2 rad/s.

Maximum vlaue of $m=\frac{170}{5} \times 2.5=85$ Opction (C) is correct

questions.q: Consider two masses with $\mathrm{m}_{1}>\mathrm{m}_{2}$ connected by a light inextensible string that passes over a pulley of radius $\mathrm{R}$ and moment of inertia I about its axis of rotation. The string does not slip on the pulley and the pulley turns without friction. The two masses are released from rest separated by a vertical distance $2 \mathrm{~h}$. When the two masses pass each other, the speed of the masses is proportional to
questions.solution: The total mechanical energy of system $=$ conserved Hence $$ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{PE}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{f}}+\mathrm{PE}_{\mathrm{f}} \\ 0-\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{~g} \times 2 \mathrm{~h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_{2} \mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}_{1} \mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^{2}-\mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{gh}-\mathrm{m}_{2} \mathrm{gh} \end{array} $$ Also $\omega=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}}$ $$ \begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{gh}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{v}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)^{2} \\ \left(\mathrm{~m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}\right) \mathrm{gh}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^{2}}{2}\left[\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}\right] \\ \mathrm{v} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}-\mathrm{m}_{2}}{\mathrm{~m}_{1}+\mathrm{m}_{2}+\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}}}} \end{array} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform metal plate shaped like a triangle $\mathrm{ABC}$ has a mass of $540 \mathrm{gm}$. the length of the sides $\mathrm{AB}, \mathrm{BC}$ and CA are $3 \mathrm{~cm}, 5 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $4 \mathrm{~cm}$, respectively. The plate is pivoted freely about the point $\mathrm{A}$. What mass must be added to a vertex, so that the plate can hang with the long edge horizontal ?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{CM}$ of triangular plate is on the median. If we put a mass say $\mathrm{m}_{1}$ on $\mathrm{C}$ it will produce torque about A which balance the torque produce $m g$ about $A$. Thus plate will can be in equilibrium position $\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{~g} \times 4 \cos 37=\mathrm{mg} \times \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{m}_{1} \mathrm{~g} \times 4 \times \frac{4}{5}=\mathrm{mg} \times \mathrm{y} \\ \mathrm{m}_{1}=\mathrm{m} \times \mathrm{y} \times \frac{5}{16} \\ \frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}}=\mathrm{y} \times \frac{5}{16} \\ \mathrm{y} < 3 \quad \therefore \frac{\mathrm{m}_{1}}{\mathrm{~m}} < 1 \\ \mathrm{~m}_{1} < \mathrm{m} \\ \mathrm{m}_{1} < 540 \mathrm{~g} \end{array}$ from given option Ans. (A)

questions.q:
A solid cylinder P rolls without slipping from rest down an inclined plane attaining a speed $v_{p}$ at the bottom. Another smooth solid cyclinder $\mathrm{Q}$ of same mass and dimensions slides without friction from rest down the
inclined plane attaining a speed $v_{q}$ at the bottom. The ratio of the speeds $\left(\frac{v_{p}}{v_{q}}\right)$ is :
questions.solution: $m g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ $m g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{M R^{2} \omega^{2}}{2}$ $g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{4} m v^{2}=\frac{3}{4} v^{2}$ $\begin{array}{l}V^{2}=\sqrt{\frac{4 g h}{3}} & V=\sqrt{\frac{4 g h}{3}} \\ V=\sqrt{2 g h} \\ & {V_{R}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{3 \times 2}}=\sqrt{2 / 3}\end{array}$

questions.q: A heater supplying constant power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> watts is switched ON at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math> to raise the temperature of a liquid kept in a calorimeter of negligible heat capacity. A student records the temperature of the liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> at equal time intervals. A graph is plotted with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>-axis versus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>-axis. options: the graph is a straight line parallel to the time axis
the heat capacity of the liquid is inversely proportional to the slope of the graph
if some heat were lost at a constant rate to the surroundings during heating, the graph would be a straight line but with a larger slope
the internal energy of the liquid increases quadratically with time
questions.solution:
Assuming no heat loss, 

Heat gained by liquid in calorimeter = Heat supplied by heater

⇒mSTf-Ti=Pt

⇒Tf=PmSt+Ti

Comparing above equation with y=mx+c,

S!ope of line =PmS

or slope ∝1S

So, graph of T versus t is as shown below.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A certain tiquid has a melting point of -50°C and a boiling point of 150°C. A thermometer is designed with this liquid and its melting and boiling points are designated at 0°L and 100°L. The melting and boiling points of water on this scale are
questions.solution:
From principle of thermometry,

T-TLFPTUFP-TLFP=a constant for every

thermometric scale.
Now, for any temperature L on a thermometer designed with given liquid and equivalent temperature C on centigrade scale, we have

L-TLFTUFP-TLFPLiquid based scale =C-TLFPTUFP-TLFPCentigrade Scale 

⇒  L-(-50)150-(-50)=C-0100-0

 

L+50150+50=C100

 

⇒  L+50=2C

Now at 0°L, centigrade scale reading will be

0+50=2 C or C=502=25°L

and at 100°L, centigrade scale reading will be

100+50=2 C or C=1502=75°L

questions.q: Mumbai needs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>12</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of water annually. Its effective surface area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>km</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and it receives an average rainfall of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> annually. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of this rain water is conserved, it will meet approximately

Surface area over which rain is

=600×1032 m2

=6×108 m2

Average rainfall, h=2.4 m Volume of water received by rain, V

=1.4×108×103 L=1.4×1011 L
Percentage of total water consumption received by rain is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The "Kangri" is an earthen pot used to stay warm in Kashmir during the winter months. Assume that the "Kangri" is spherical and of surface area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> It contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of a mixture of coal, wood and leaves with calorific value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (and provides heat with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> efficiency). The surface temperature of the 'Kangri' is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the room temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then, a reasonable estimate for the duration t (in hours) that the 'kangri' heat will last is (take the 'kangri' to be a black body):
questions.solution:
dQdt=eAσT04-Ts4

e=1, A=7×10-2, s=5.67×10-8

T0=333 K, Ts=273 K

dQdt=26.75 Watt

total energy produced=10100×30×103×300

⇒9×105 J

∴ time =9×10526.75×3600hrs=9.35 hrs

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: One gram mole of an ideal gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> with the ratio of constant pressure and constant volume specific heats <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></math> is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> gram moles of another ideal gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>γ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>5</mn></math>. If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> for the mixture is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>19</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>13</mn></math>, then what will be the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math>?
questions.solution:
By using formula,

γmixture=μ1Cp1+μ2Cp2μ1+μ2μ1Cu1+μ2Cv2μ1+μ2

γmixture=μ1Cp1+μ2Cp2μ1Cv1+μ2Cv2

=μ1γ1γ1-1R+μ2γ2γ2-1Rμ1Rγ1-1+μ2Rγ2-1

or γmixture=μ1γ1γ2-1+μ2γ2γ1-1μ1γ2-1+μ2γ1-1

It is given, γmixture=1913

γ1=53,γ2=75

n1=1,n2=n

Substituting values, we have 

1913=5375-1+n7553-175-1+n53-1

⇒1913=53×25+n75×2325+n23

⇒1925+n23=1323+n1415

⇒n=2

∴ Number of moles of gas B=2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Many exoplanets have been discovered by the transit method, where in one monitors, a dip in the intensity of the parent star as the exoplanet moves in front of it. The exoplanet has a radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and the parent star has radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>R</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>is the intensity observed on earth due to the parent star, then as the exoplanet transits
options: the minimum observed intensity of the parent star is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>
the minimum observed intensity of the parent star is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>99</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>
the minimum observed intensity of the parent star is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>999</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>
the minimum observed intensity of the parent star is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9999</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>I</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>
questions.solution:
Intensity of radiation (mainly visible light) emitted from surface of a star is proportional to its area.

So,  I∝A or I=k A

where, k= constant.
Now, if I0= intensity of parent star.

Then, I0=kπ (100R)2=kπR2×10000

When exoplanet is in front of star, observed intensity will be minimum. Let intensity minimum is Imin, then

 

Imin=k π(100R)2-πR2

⇒  Imin=k π R2 (10000-1)

=kπ R2×9999

So,   IminI0=kπR2×9999kπR2×10000

⇒  Imin=I0×0.9999

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bottles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ have radii $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}$ and heights $\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{B}}$ respectively with $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~h}_{\mathrm{A}}$. These are filled with hot water at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Consider that heat loss for the bottles takes place only from side surfaces. If the time the water to cool down to $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $t_{\mathrm{B}}$ for the bottles $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, respectively, then $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $t_{\mathrm{B}}$ are best related as
questions.solution: $\mathrm{t} \propto \frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{A}}$ $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{k} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{V} \rho}{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{k} \cdot \rho \mathrm{h}$ $\mathrm{t} \propto \mathrm{h}$ $\therefore \frac{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}_{0}}{2 \mathrm{~h}_{0}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{A}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A planet of radius $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}$ is revolving around a star of radius $\mathrm{R}^{*}$, which is at temperature $\mathrm{T}^{*}$. The distance between the star and the planet is $\mathrm{d}$. If the planet's temperature is $f \mathrm{~T}^{*}$, then $f$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
The planet should be in Thermal equilibrium with star.

Amount of heat energy emitted by start per second $\mathrm{E}_{1}=\left(\sigma \mathrm{T}^{* 4}\right)\left(4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{*^{2}}\right)$
$\because \mathrm{d}=$ distance between star and planet
Hence, amount of energy reaching planet per
$\begin{aligned}
\text { unit area per second } &=\frac{E_{1}}{4 \pi \mathrm{d}^{2}} \\
&=\frac{\left(\sigma \mathrm{T}^{* 4}\right)\left(4 \pi \mathrm{R}^{* 2}\right)}{4 \pi \mathrm{d}^{2}} \\
&=\frac{\sigma \mathrm{T}^{*^{4}} \mathrm{R}^{* 2}}{\mathrm{~d}^{2}}
\end{aligned}$
Hence energy received by planet per second
$\mathrm{E}_{2}=\left(\frac{\sigma \mathrm{T}^{* 4} \mathrm{R}^{* 2}}{\mathrm{~d}^{2}}\right) \pi \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}^{2}$ $\mathrm{~T}=$ Temperature of planet then amount of energy emitted by planet per second
$$
E_{3}=\left(\sigma T^{4}\right)\left(4 \pi R_{P}^{2}\right)
$$
For thermal equilibrium $E_{3}=E_{2}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow\left(\sigma \mathrm{T}^{4}\right)\left(4 \pi \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}^{2}\right)=\frac{\left(\sigma \mathrm{T}^{* 4} \mathrm{R}^{* 2}\right)}{\mathrm{d}^{2}}\left(\pi \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{p}}^{2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}^{4}=\frac{\mathrm{T}^{* 4} \mathrm{R}^{* 2}}{4 \mathrm{~d}^{2}} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{T}^{*} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}^{*}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}}=\mathrm{fT}^{*} \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{f} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{R}^{*}}{\mathrm{~d}}}
\end{array}
$$
Hence correct answer is (A)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ice in a freezeris at $-7^{6} \mathrm{C} .100 \mathrm{~g}$ of this ice is mixed with $200 \mathrm{~g}$ of water at $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Take the freezing temperature of water to be $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the specific heat of ice equal to $2.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, specific heat of water equal to $4.2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and the latent heat of ice equal to $335 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g}$. Assuming no loss of heat to the environment, the mass of ice in the final mixture is closest to
questions.solution: Let $\mathrm{mgm}$ of ice melted Mixture will be at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ Heat given $=200 \times(4.2)(15) \mathrm{J}$ Heat absorbed $=100 \times 2.2 \times(7)+m(335)$ by solving $\mathrm{m}=33 \mathrm{gm}$ So ice remain $=100-33=67 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A nuclear fuel rod generates energy at a rate of $5 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{Watt} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. It is in the shape of a cylinder of radius $4.0 \mathrm{~mm}$ and length $0.20 \mathrm{~m}$. A coolant of specific heat $4 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~J} /(\mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K})$ flows past it at a rate of $0.2 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}$. The temperature rise in this coolant is approximately -
questions.solution: $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \times \mathrm{S} \Delta \mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}$ $\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=5 \times 10^{8} \times$ volume of rod $=5 \times 10^{8} \times \pi \times(4)^{2} \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{0.2}{10}$ $=5 \times 10 \times \pi \times 16 \times 2$ $=1600 \pi$ $0.2 \times 4 \times 10^{3} \Delta \mathrm{T}=1600 \pi$ $8 \times 10^{2} \Delta \mathrm{T}=16 \times 10^{2} \pi$ $\Delta \mathrm{T}=3.14 \times 2$ $\Rightarrow 6.28^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

options: the water will come out as a spherical ball
the water decompose into hydrogen and oxygen

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid expands upon heating because :
options: the potential energy of interaction between atoms in the solid is asymmetric about the equilbrium positions of atoms
the frequency of vibration of the atoms increases
the heating generates a thermal gradient between opposite sides
a fluid called the caloric flows into the interatomic spacing of the solid during heating thereby expanding it.
questions.solution: As the potential energy is asymmetric about the equilibrium position of the atoms, as the temperature increases, the average separation between the atoms (green dots) increases. Thus, the solids expand on heating.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin paper cup filled with water does not catch fire when placed over a flame. This is because
options: The water cuts off oxygen supply to the paper cup
Water is an excellent conductor of heat
The paper cup does not become appreciably hotter than the water it contain
Paper is a poor conductor of heat
questions.solution: No Solution
